All visible Plans-specific components (error notification, filter/sort band, plans list, comparison selector, and comparison modal) are children of this wrapper and align to its grid, reinforcing the single-column, top-to-bottom flow seen in the mockup. The background beneath this wrapper remains the global dark tone, creating a seamless transition from header through content to footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Full-width, full-height structural wrapper between header and footer; non-visual and scrolls naturally with the document; establishes the centered 12-column grid for all child components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager is conceptually anchored to the top of the main content stack, but it does not occupy any visual space. Think of it as a transparent layer sitting just underneath the page heading and above all interactive components. It synchronizes the state of filters, sorting, selected plans, and comparison view with the URL. Visually, nothing changes when this component updates; instead, its influence is seen indirectly: the filter bar controls, the plans grid content, and the comparison selector all reflect URL-driven state while staying aligned in their linear layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendered logic layer with no visual footprint; tied to the page-init scope; influences the layout state of other components without affecting their positioning or spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar sits near the top of the Plans content area, directly beneath the page heading and any error notification banner. It spans the full width of the centered content column, aligning to the same 12-column grid. Visually, it presents as a low-profile, dark strip slightly elevated in contrast from the page background, with subtle rounding at the corners and no visible shadows, echoing the flat style of the mockup. Vertical padding is around 16–24px, with 16–24px horizontal padding inside the bar before controls begin. Filter controls (e.g., category dropdowns, duration toggles, price range) are left-aligned in a horizontal row, starting from the first grid columns. On larger screens, the filter elements occupy roughly the left 2/3 of the bar, leaving breathing room on the right. The bar is separated from the content above and below by ~24–32px of vertical spacing, creating a clear, airy band that the eye naturally drops to after the page heading or notification strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level component in the main content flow; spans the content column and scrolls with the page; gains a slightly more prominent appearance when filters are active (e.g., with subtle neon accents) but does not change its spatial footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control is visually integrated into the same horizontal band as the filter bar but aligned toward the right side, echoing the header pattern of content left and CTA right. On desktop, it sits on the same baseline as the primary filter inputs, occupying roughly the rightmost 1/3 of the filter bar’s internal space. The sort dropdown label and control are right-aligned to the final grid columns, providing a clear visual endpoint to the row. Adequate spacing (~16px) separates it from the nearest filter control so the band doesn’t feel cramped. On narrower screens, the sort control wraps beneath the main filters, left-aligned with them, but still within the same bar, maintaining the single-column vertical flow. The control itself is compact, with 8–12px vertical padding and 16–20px horizontal padding, matching other input elements and staying subtle compared to brighter CTAs elsewhere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the filter bar; its horizontal position shifts responsively (right-aligned beside filters on wide screens, stacked below on narrow screens) but remains part of the same static strip that scrolls with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans list occupies the largest portion of the central content area, positioned directly below the filter/sort bar with a vertical gap of about 32–40px. It is rendered as a responsive grid aligned to the 12-column system: on large screens, cards might occupy 3 or 4 columns each, resulting in 3–4 cards per row, with consistent horizontal gutters (~24px) between columns. The grid spans the full width of the content column but maintains a clear margin to the left and right grid edges to avoid crowding. Vertical spacing between rows of cards is around 24–32px, lending the layout a medium density—visually richer than the empty cart mockup but still clean. The background between cards remains the dark site background, allowing the slightly lighter/differentiated plan cards to stand out as discrete tiles. The list stretches downward as many rows as needed, eventually leading the user’s gaze toward the footer area as they scroll.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard grid within the main flow; expands vertically with content, pushing the footer downward; responds to viewport size by adjusting column count and card width while preserving grid alignment and spacing rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan card is a dark, slightly rounded tile that occupies a single grid cell in the plans list (e.g., 3 or 4 of 12 columns wide on desktop). Internally, the card uses a vertical, top-to-bottom hierarchy: plan name and tag (top), pricing and key benefits (middle), and action area (bottom). Padding inside the card is consistent—around 16–24px on all sides—creating a contained, breathable block. The card background is a shade lighter than the global page background or subtly tinted, so cards are visually distinct without using shadows. The top section aligns left, with plan name taking prominence; below that, pricing is slightly larger and bolder, drawing the eye naturally downward. Feature bullets are stacked with 8px line spacing. At the bottom, a bright neon-accent CTA button (e.g., 'Select plan') spans much of the card’s width (around 70–80%), left-aligned or centered, with a secondary text link or comparison toggle aligned to the opposite side, echoing the header’s split-left/right structure. Vertical spacing between sections inside the card follows the 8–16px scale, ensuring medium density while maintaining clarity.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Self-contained tile within the grid; its width is determined by the grid column configuration and adjusts responsively (e.g., full-width on small screens, half-width on tablets, one-third or one-quarter on desktops); height grows with content but maintains consistent internal spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison selector manifests as a dark, slim bar that appears only after one or more plans have been marked for comparison. Spatially, it is aligned to the same centered content column as the rest of the page but is pinned near the bottom of the viewport so that it remains visible as users scroll through the plans list. It spans the full usable width of the content column, with 16–20px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding. Inside, text on the left indicates how many plans are selected for comparison, while on the right a bright neon-accent button ('Compare plans') anchors the bar’s right edge, mirroring the header’s CTA placement and drawing attention. On very small screens, the text stacks above the button but remains within the same narrow band. A slight increase in contrast or a subtle neon border helps the bar stand out against the black background without resorting to shadows, maintaining the flat aesthetic. The bar sits just above the global floating call/help button when both are visible, ensuring that controls do not overlap.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditionally visible and typically sticky to the bottom of the viewport within the main content area; overlays the lower edge of the plans list while active but does not cover the footer—once the user scrolls to the footer, it may either dock just above the footer or yield to ensure the footer content remains fully accessible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The comparison view appears as a full-page modal overlay when activated by the comparison selector. It dims the underlying page with a semi-opaque dark scrim, leaving the header, plans grid, footer, and floating buttons visible but de-emphasized. Centered within the viewport is a large, horizontally oriented panel that occupies roughly 80% of the viewport width (aligned to the same 12-column grid) and up to 80–90% of the viewport height, with 48–64px margins from the top and bottom to preserve the sense of airiness from the mockup. The modal panel has slightly rounded corners and a solid dark background, with a subtle neon accent line or highlight at the top edge or under the header text. Inside, the layout follows a clear vertical structure: a top bar with a left-aligned 'Compare plans' title and a right-aligned close icon or button; beneath that, a horizontally scrollable or multi-column comparison table aligning plans side by side, each column matching the width of a plan card where possible. Rows represent features, with consistent vertical spacing (~8–12px), and a slightly denser feel than the cards but still readable. A persistent action row at the bottom of the modal offers per-plan CTAs and a global 'Done' or 'Close' action aligned to the right. The overall effect is of a focused, high-importance overlay that temporarily becomes the main stage.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed-position overlay centered in the viewport, sitting above all other page content including the floating call button and comparison selector; scrollable internally if content exceeds available height; disables interaction with the background until dismissed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan change handler is an invisible behavioral layer associated with actions taken on plan cards and within the comparison view. Conceptually, it sits just underneath these interactive components in the hierarchy, orchestrating transitions such as initiating a subscription, confirming a plan change, or redirecting to checkout. It does not render any visible UI of its own; instead, its presence is reflected through state changes (e.g., showing a success or error banner, updating selected plan highlights) within existing visual components. Spatially, you can imagine it as a thin, non-visible strip spanning the content width just below the plans list, listening for actions from cards and modals.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual logic component; has no physical footprint and does not alter spatial layout directly; interacts with other components to trigger visual feedback and navigation without affecting their positions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error notification banner mirrors the mockup’s light, high-contrast notification strip. It is positioned near the top of the main content area, directly below the page heading and above the filter/sort bar, following the same single-column stack. The banner spans the full width of the centered content column, with 16–20px vertical padding and 24px horizontal padding, and uses a light background color that strongly contrasts with the dark page, immediately drawing attention. The left side of the banner houses an icon or status indicator (error, warning, info), aligned vertically with the text. The main message text is left-aligned and occupies most of the horizontal space. On the far right, there may be a small action (e.g., 'Retry', 'Dismiss') or a close icon, aligned to the banner’s right edge, echoing the right-side emphasis of CTAs in the header. The edges are subtly rounded in line with the site’s style, and the banner is separated from surrounding components by ~24–32px of vertical space. When no errors exist, this component collapses entirely, allowing the content to shift upward smoothly.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level strip within the main flow; appears and disappears conditionally without leaving empty space; scrolls naturally with the page and does not overlay other components when visible.

name: My Plan
title: My Plan
description: Shows the user’s current IPTV subscription plan, add-ons, channels, and any pending changes, with actions to adjust the plan, manage add-ons, and proceed to checkout to confirm changes.

core_features: Load and display the current subscription and plan details using getMyPlanSummary, including billing cycle, status, and simultaneous stream limits.
List channels included in the current base plan using the included_channels data.
Display all active add-ons and indicate any pending add-on additions or removals based on pending_subscription_change data.
Show a pricing summary comparing current totals to pending new totals (monthly and annual) using pricing_summary.
Highlight pending subscription changes when pending_subscription_change.exists is true, especially when the URL parameter changePreview is 'true'.
Allow navigation to change the base plan (plans.html) and manage add-ons (addons.html) from relevant sections.
Provide a primary action to proceed to checkout (checkout.html) with context for confirming plan or add-on changes.
Reflect the navigation source context (from parameter) to adjust copy and emphasis (e.g., after selecting a new plan or add-on vs. arriving from dashboard.html).
user_workflows:
View current plan from dashboard:
1) User navigates from dashboard.html to my_plan.html (from=dashboard, no changePreview parameter).
2) Page initializes and calls getMyPlanSummary.
3) Plan summary section shows current plan, billing cycle, and simultaneous streams.
4) Add-ons section lists active add-ons without any pending-change highlighting (pending_subscription_change.exists is false or status not relevant).
5) Pricing summary shows current monthly and annual totals only; pending values are either not displayed or visually de-emphasized when there is no pending change.
6) User can click 'Change Base Plan' (navigates to plans.html?context=change_plan) or 'Manage Add-ons' (navigates to addons.html?from=my_plan) if they want to adjust their subscription.

Review plan change after selecting a new plan:
1) User selects a different plan on plans.html; that page creates or updates a SubscriptionChange record and redirects to my_plan.html?from=plans&changePreview=true.
2) On load, my_plan.html reads from=plans and changePreview=true, then calls getMyPlanSummary.
3) The data loader receives current_subscription, current_plan, included_channels, active_addons, pending_subscription_change, and pricing_summary.
4) Pending-change indicator component detects pending_subscription_change.exists is true and changePreview='true', and shows a prominent review state.
5) Plan summary section compares current_plan to pending selected_plan_id from pending_subscription_change.change and visually distinguishes the pending plan.
6) Add-ons section highlights add-ons to be added or removed based on selected_addon_ids and removed_addon_ids in the pending change object.
7) Pricing summary shows current vs pending totals side by side using pricing_summary.current_* and pricing_summary.pending_new_* values.
8) User reviews details, then clicks 'Proceed to Checkout' which navigates to checkout.html?from=my_plan&context=plan_or_addon_change for final confirmation and payment handling.

Review add-on changes after selecting an add-on:
1) User chooses to add or remove an add-on on addon_detail.html or addons.html; that page creates or updates SubscriptionChange and redirects to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail (or from=addons)&changePreview=true.
2) my_plan.html initializes, reads parameters, and calls getMyPlanSummary.
3) Page shows current plan unchanged but marks add-ons that will be active after the change using pending_subscription_change.change.selected_addon_ids and removed_addon_ids.
4) Pricing summary updates to show the impact of add-on changes on monthly and annual totals.
5) User can either click 'Manage Add-ons' to adjust again (addons.html?from=my_plan) or proceed to checkout (checkout.html?from=my_plan&context=plan_or_addon_change).

Review previously created pending change without explicit preview parameter:
1) User has a pending SubscriptionChange from a prior session and navigates to my_plan.html via header navigation (no from or changePreview parameters).
2) Page calls getMyPlanSummary; pending_subscription_change.exists is true but changePreview is not 'true'.
3) Pending-change indicator shows a subtle notice that there are pending changes awaiting confirmation.
4) User can click an inline 'Review Changes' control in the indicator that reloads or updates the URL to set changePreview=true (e.g., updating the query string), keeping the same my_plan.html but switching to preview mode.
5) In preview mode, plan summary, add-ons, and pricing comparison are emphasized, and the 'Proceed to Checkout' action becomes the primary call-to-action.

Proceed to checkout to confirm changes:
1) At any point when pending_subscription_change.exists is true, the user can click 'Proceed to Checkout'.
2) Page verifies that a pending change exists in the client state (based on getMyPlanSummary response) before enabling the button.
3) On click, the browser navigates to checkout.html?from=my_plan&context=plan_or_addon_change.
4) The checkout page (outside this spec) uses that context and existing backend SubscriptionChange state to finalize the changes.
interactions:
Page initialization:
- Event: onPageLoad
- Behavior: Parse URL parameters 'from' and 'changePreview'; call getMyPlanSummary once.
- Result: Populate internal state with current_subscription, current_plan, included_channels, active_addons, pending_subscription_change, and pricing_summary; derive flags like hasPendingChange and isPreviewMode (based on changePreview and pending_subscription_change.exists).

Retry loading data:
- Event: onClickRetry (e.g., from an error state in the data loader component)
- Behavior: Re-call getMyPlanSummary without changing URL parameters.
- Result: Replace previous error state with new data or a new error if it persists.

Change Base Plan navigation:
- Event: onClickChangePlanLink in plan summary or action bar.
- Precondition: Page is loaded; user wants to modify base plan.
- Behavior: Navigate to plans.html?context=change_plan.
- Result: User enters plan selection flow, which can create or update a SubscriptionChange and later redirect back with changePreview=true.

Manage Add-ons navigation:
- Event: onClickManageAddonsLink in add-ons summary or action bar.
- Behavior: Navigate to addons.html?from=my_plan.
- Result: User manages add-ons; after modifications, other pages may redirect back to my_plan.html with appropriate parameters.

Toggle channels list expansion:
- Event: onToggleChannelsView on a control such as 'Show all channels' / 'Hide channels'.
- Behavior: Update in-memory component state (e.g., expanded vs collapsed) to control how many items from included_channels are rendered.
- Result: No API calls; purely visual control of channel list visibility.

Toggle pricing display focus (monthly vs annual emphasis):
- Event: onToggleBillingCycleView in pricing summary component.
- Behavior: Update local component state to emphasize either the monthly or annual values from pricing_summary (and possibly reflect current_subscription.billing_cycle as default).
- Result: No API calls; influences which totals are prominent, while both values remain visible or accessible.

Proceed to Checkout:
- Event: onClickProceedToCheckout in the action bar.
- Precondition: pending_subscription_change.exists is true and pending_subscription_change.change.status indicates a state that is eligible for confirmation (e.g., draft or pending_confirmation; exact interpretation is based on value but does not trigger additional API on this page).
- Behavior: If hasPendingChange is true, navigate to checkout.html?from=my_plan&context=plan_or_addon_change. If not, the button is disabled and may display a tooltip or inline note indicating that the user must make a change first.
- Result: User is taken to the checkout flow to finalize pending subscription changes.

Switch to explicit preview mode when pending changes exist:
- Event: onClickPreviewChanges in the pending-change indicator when pending_subscription_change.exists is true and changePreview is not 'true'.
- Behavior: Update the browser URL to include or change changePreview=true (e.g., through a navigation or history API), then reload or re-render the page view using existing getMyPlanSummary data.
- Result: The page enters preview mode, visually highlighting pending vs current states without additional API calls.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is controlled by a combination of URL parameters and the response from getMyPlanSummary.

1) URL parameter: from
- Possible values originate from other pages (plans.html, addon_detail.html, addons.html, dashboard.html).
- This parameter is read on page load and used to adjust contextual messaging or emphasis, for example:
  - from='plans': interpret the visit as part of a plan-change workflow and emphasize the plan comparison area.
  - from='addon_detail' or from='addons': emphasize the add-ons section.
  - from='dashboard' or missing: treat it as a neutral visit from general navigation.
- The 'from' parameter does not change which data is loaded; it only affects presentation and which sections may scroll into view by default.

2) URL parameter: changePreview
- When changePreview='true', the page enters preview mode if pending_subscription_change.exists is true.
- In preview mode:
  - The pending-change indicator component is visible and prominent.
  - Plan summary and add-ons sections highlight differences between current and pending states (e.g., show both current_plan and the plan referenced by pending_subscription_change.change.selected_plan_id, if provided; highlight add-ons in selected_addon_ids and removed_addon_ids).
  - Pricing summary compares pricing_summary.current_monthly_total / current_annual_total against pricing_summary.pending_new_monthly_total / pending_new_annual_total.
  - The 'Proceed to Checkout' action is emphasized and enabled when there is a pending change.
- When changePreview is missing or not 'true':
  - If pending_subscription_change.exists is false: the page simply shows the current subscription without any pending-change UI.
  - If pending_subscription_change.exists is true: a subtle pending-change indicator is shown with an option to re-enter preview mode by updating the URL to set changePreview=true.

3) Data-driven state flags from getMyPlanSummary
- hasPendingChange: derived from pending_subscription_change.exists.
- pendingChangeStatus: derived from pending_subscription_change.change.status (e.g., 'draft', 'pending_confirmation'); used only for display and to decide if the 'Proceed to Checkout' button should be enabled.
- currentBillingCycle: from current_subscription.billing_cycle; used to set initial emphasis for pricing display.
- These flags are computed once per successful getMyPlanSummary call and shared across components, avoiding extra API calls.

4) No localStorage or persistent client storage
- All cross-page state relies on backend Subscription / SubscriptionChange and URL parameters ('from' and 'changePreview').
- In-page, ephemeral UI states such as 'channels list expanded' or 'pricing focus: monthly vs annual' are maintained only in component memory and are reset on full page reload or navigation.
- No static or hardcoded subscription data is stored on the client; all subscription-related values come directly from getMyPlanSummary.

components.functionality: On page load, parse URL parameters ('from', 'changePreview'), call getMyPlanSummary, manage loading and error states, and compute derived flags such as hasPendingChange and isPreviewMode for other components.

Display the presence and basic status of a pending subscription change. When changePreview is 'true' and hasPendingChange is true, show a strong preview banner; otherwise show a subtle notice with an option to switch to preview mode.

Show current base plan details (name, description, channel count, simultaneous streams) based on current_plan and current_subscription, and when in preview mode with a pending plan change, compare current plan to the plan referenced by pending_subscription_change.change.selected_plan_id.

List all active add-ons from active_addons and, when a pending change exists, highlight add-ons that will be added or removed by comparing active_addons to pending_subscription_change.change.selected_addon_ids and removed_addon_ids.
Display channels included in the current base plan using included_channels, with the ability to collapse or expand the list for usability.

Show current vs pending subscription totals using pricing_summary. When hasPendingChange is true and preview mode is enabled, display a comparison between current_monthly_total/current_annual_total and pending_new_monthly_total/pending_new_annual_total, optionally emphasizing the billing cycle from current_subscription.billing_cycle.

Interpret the 'from' URL parameter and hasPendingChange state to show concise contextual text (for example, indicating that the user is reviewing a recent plan or add-on change) without altering data.
Provide primary actions related to the plan: navigate to change base plan (plans.html), manage add-ons (addons.html), and proceed to checkout when a pending change exists.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the mockup’s structure. Global site header and global footer remain outside this scope. Within the page content, the navigation context header appears first, followed immediately by the pending-change status banner. Below that, plan summary, add-ons, channels, and pricing summary stack vertically in that order. A plan actions footer functions as a persistent bottom-of-page action bar analogous to the mockup’s floating action button, aligned to the same inner grid rather than floating freely.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the mockup’s structure. Global site header and global footer remain outside this scope. Within the page content, the navigation context header appears first, followed immediately by the pending-change status banner. Below that, plan summary, add-ons, channels, and pricing summary stack vertically in that order. A plan actions footer functions as a persistent bottom-of-page action bar analogous to the mockup’s floating action button, aligned to the same inner grid rather than floating freely.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Respect the mockup’s proportions by keeping the central content visually light and airy. The main content column sits within generous left/right margins (~64–80px from viewport edges on large screens) and adheres to a 12-column grid. Vertical spacing between major sections (context header, status banner, each summary section, and pricing) is around 32–48px. The status banner and action bar use full-width strips across the content container, while the summary sections occupy roughly 70–80% of the container width to preserve breathing room. The plan actions footer hugs the bottom of the viewport or page with enough top separation (~24–32px) from the last content section, similar to the gap above the footer copyright bar in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium density: individual sections are clearly separated with ample vertical whitespace and moderate line spacing. The status banner and plan actions footer remain visually simple, with a clear message and key CTA rather than heavy detail. Plan summary, add-ons, channels, and pricing are denser than the heading but still structured as flat blocks with clear typographic hierarchy and no crowded tables. Content is primarily text and simple rows, avoiding dense card grids, echoing the mockup’s minimal empty cart state while accommodating necessary information.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the user’s eye from top to bottom: start with the context header as the main page anchor, then immediately into the high-contrast pending-change banner (similar to the light notification strip in the mockup). From there, the eye travels downward through plan summary, add-ons, and channels, culminating in the pricing summary where the financial implications of changes are clear. The plan actions footer at the bottom serves as the final focal point and primary conversion area, echoing the mockup’s floating call button by being visually strong and always accessible near the bottom edge. Neon accent colors highlight key actions and monetary values, reinforcing the tech/streaming aesthetic while supporting this vertical visual narrative.

layout.overall_description: Inside the site’s dark full-width background, the My Plan page content sits in a centered, max-width column aligned to the global 12-column grid, with generous horizontal margins that echo the mockup’s spacious feel. At the top of this column, just below the global navigation, a left-aligned page title and brief context header establish where the user is and what this area controls. Directly beneath it, a full-width light status banner spans the content container, standing out against the dark background to call attention to any pending changes. Below this, a sequence of flat, dark panels flows downward: first the current plan summary, then add-ons, then channels, each occupying most of the column width with clear headings and vertically stacked content, separated by comfortable gaps. Near the bottom of this stack, the pricing summary block groups totals and change deltas into a concise, easy-to-scan section. Anchored at the bottom of the viewport or page, a dark, full-width action bar aligned to the same inner grid holds the primary neon CTA on the right and supporting text/actions on the left, functioning as the persistent decision zone. The result is a tall, vertically driven layout that feels modern and cinematic: lots of dark space, crisp typographic hierarchy, subtle rounding, and a single strong accent color that appears consistently on key actions and prices.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader component sits logically at the top of the My Plan content tree, just inside the page’s main content container and beneath the global header. Visually, when active, it may present as a full-width, low-height strip or overlay on the dark background, centered horizontally, spanning the same max-width as the content (~70–80% of viewport width on large screens, constrained within ~1200px). It should appear above the navigation context header, creating a temporary, subtle layer that communicates loading or fetching state without significantly altering the perceived vertical hierarchy. Once loading completes, this component collapses completely, freeing the top of the stack for the navigation-context-header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a transient overlay or inline strip at the very top of the page-specific content flow. It may briefly cover or push down other components while active, then collapses to zero height when not needed, leaving no persistent space in the layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pending change indicator appears as a full-width, horizontally oriented status banner directly under the navigation context header, occupying the same inner width as the content container while contrasting strongly with the dark background (using a light or slightly tinted background similar to the mockup’s notification strip). It anchors to the left edge of the 12-column grid and spans all columns, with content padded by 16–24px on all sides. Inside the banner, on larger screens, an icon or status label sits on the far left, followed by succinct message text that extends toward the right. If there’s a small CTA (e.g., “Review changes”), it aligns to the right edge within the banner. Vertically, the banner is relatively shallow (roughly the height of 1–2 lines of text plus padding), and there’s a clear 24–32px gap both above (to the context header) and below (to the plan summary), mirroring the separation seen around the mockup’s notification bar.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow element that always appears near the top of the page content, immediately after the context header. It does not stick to the viewport, but because it sits high in the stack and uses high contrast, it is one of the first components seen when the page loads.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan summary section is the first major dark content block after the pending change banner. It is centered within the main content area, aligned to the 12-column grid, and spans most of the container width (approximately 8–10 of 12 columns, or about 70–80% of the max-width content area) to keep generous margins on either side. The section appears as a flat, dark panel with slightly rounded corners and internal padding of 24–32px, giving it a distinct but subtle card-like presence without visible drop shadows. At the top-left of the section, a bold heading like “Current Plan” aligns with other section headings on the page, with supporting information—plan name, resolution, device limits, billing frequency—stacked vertically beneath in 8–16px spaced rows. Key attributes or prices can be arranged in 2–3 columns across the grid on large screens, but the overall impression remains a simple, linear block with good breathing room. Vertically, this section sits about 32–40px below the status banner and about 32–48px above the addons summary, helping maintain the page’s airy vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow content block that grows vertically with content while maintaining fixed horizontal alignment within the grid. On smaller screens it collapses to a full-width single column but retains the same top-down hierarchy and spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The addons summary section follows directly beneath the plan summary, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 32–40px of dark background. It uses the same width, alignment, and visual treatment: a flat, dark panel with subtle corner rounding, aligned to the left edge of the content grid and spanning roughly 8–10 columns. A left-aligned section title such as “Add-ons” appears at the top with consistent typography relative to other section headings. Below it, each add-on appears as a horizontally arranged row: add-on name and brief description on the left, and pricing or status (active, pending removal) aligned to the right. Rows are stacked vertically with 8–12px between them, keeping the density moderate but organized. If there are controls like toggle switches or “Manage” links, they sit to the far right in each row, aligning with the pricing summary’s right edge. Overall, this section feels like a concise list of options, clearly separated from but visually similar to the plan summary above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow content block mirroring the plan summary’s horizontal behavior. It expands vertically as add-ons are added without overlapping adjacent sections, maintaining consistent vertical gaps before and after.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channels list section appears beneath the addons summary, again separated by approximately 32–40px of vertical space. It occupies the same horizontal footprint as the other main sections (about 8–10 columns within the centered content container) but may feel slightly denser due to the nature of channel lists. At the top-left, a heading such as “Included Channels” anchors the section. Below, channels can be presented in a lightly structured list: simple rows of channel names, optionally grouped by category. On larger screens, channels may form 2–3 narrow vertical columns within the same panel to avoid excessive scrolling while retaining adequate line spacing (about 8px between rows). The panel’s background remains the same dark tone with subtle rounding, and internal padding (24–32px) mirrors other sections to keep the layout cohesive. This section transitions the user from high-level plan information into more granular content, visually bridging toward the pricing summary that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow block that may become taller than other sections. On small screens it collapses to a single-column list; on large screens it can use multiple internal columns while still conforming to the outer 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pricing summary section sits close to the bottom of the main content stack, positioned 32–48px below the channels list and 24–32px above the plan actions footer. It is visually emphasized compared to other sections through careful use of neon accent color on key prices and totals. Horizontally, it is aligned and sized consistently with other content blocks (about 8–10 columns wide, centered within the max-width container). Inside the flat, dark, slightly rounded panel, the top-left features a clear heading like “Pricing Summary” or “Your Monthly Total.” Beneath, a vertical stack of line items shows base plan, add-ons, discounts, and tax. Each line item is a left-right pair: label on the left, amount on the right, aligned with a consistent gutter. The final total appears at the bottom in a larger, bolder type and uses the neon accent color to draw attention. Generous internal padding (24–32px) and clear separation between line items (8–12px) ensure that this section is immediately scannable and visually anchors the decision-making area of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow block that maintains fixed positioning in the vertical reading order but is intentionally placed close to the bottom action bar. It does not stick on scroll, but in typical viewport heights it will feel visually coupled with the plan actions footer below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation context header is the first visible element of the My Plan page content, sitting directly beneath the global site header and spanning the full width of the centered content container. It is left-aligned and occupies only a shallow vertical space, consistent with the mockup’s large ‘Cart’ heading area. A prominent page title such as “My Plan” appears on the top-left, likely spanning 4–6 columns, with any supporting breadcrumb or short descriptive line directly below it in lighter text. The background remains the same dark canvas as the page body—no separate card—giving this header a clean, integrated feel. Vertical padding around the title (about 24–32px above and below) creates a spacious intro area before the more contrast-heavy pending-change indicator. This header acts as the anchor for the page’s hierarchy, visually framing subsequent sections and aligning all content to the same left baseline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, in-flow header for this page’s content, immediately following the global nav. It scrolls away with the rest of the page and does not stick, serving as a structural rather than persistent element.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan actions footer is a horizontal action bar anchored at the bottom of the viewport or just above the global site footer, functioning as the page’s persistent decision zone similar in emphasis to the mockup’s floating call button but stretched across the content width. It spans the full width of the main content container (aligned to the same inner margins as other sections) and sits on the dark background, subtly elevated in importance by a slightly different dark tint or a thin top border line. Internal padding of 16–24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally ensures it feels substantial yet unobtrusive. Inside, content is arranged in a two-part layout: on the left (starting at the grid’s left edge), concise explanatory text or a summary of pending changes appears, possibly including a small icon or ‘You have pending changes’ note. On the right, aligned to the grid’s right edge, a high-contrast neon CTA button (e.g., “Confirm Changes” or “Go to Checkout”) stands out as the primary interaction, potentially accompanied by a secondary text link like “Discard changes” beside or below it. This bar maintains a modest height (roughly 64–96px total) and ensures that, even with scrolling, the primary action remains visually accessible within the bottom third of the viewport.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a sticky bottom action bar: anchored to the bottom of the viewport on larger screens so it remains visible as the user scrolls through content, and then transitions to a standard in-flow footer-like block above the global footer on very small screens if necessary. It overlays the dark page background without covering essential content, respecting safe bottom margins and OS UI (e.g., mobile home indicators).

name: Add-ons
title: Add-ons
description: Browse, filter, and manage TV add-on packages, with category tabs, price and quality filters, and quick actions to view details or add them to your subscription.

core_features: Load available add-on categories and filter configuration using getAddonFilterOptions, then display them as selectable tabs and filter controls.
List add-ons for the currently selected category using getAddonsForListing, with filters (monthly price and quality flags) and optional sort passed from the page state.
Allow users to filter add-ons by maximum monthly price and quality attributes such as HD or 4K channels, using configuration from getAddonFilterOptions and applied_filters from getAddonsForListing.
Indicate for each add-on whether it is already included in the user’s current subscription by comparing addon IDs against already_included_addon_ids returned from getAddonsForListing.
Provide navigation from each add-on card to addon_detail.html with the appropriate addonId via URL parameters.
Provide a quick "Add to My Plan" action on eligible add-on cards that calls addAddonToDraftSubscription and, on success, navigates to my_plan.html with from=addons and changePreview=true.
user_workflows:
Browse and filter add-ons from header navigation:
1. User opens addons.html from the main header navigation without URL parameters.
2. The page controller reads URL parameters (none present for category or filters in this case).
3. The page calls getAddonFilterOptions to retrieve categories, price range, and quality filter labels.
4. The controller selects a default category from the returned categories and initializes filter values using the returned max_monthly_price_range and quality_filters.
5. The page calls getAddonsForListing with the selected category, the initialized filters, and the current sort value (if any).
6. The add-on grid displays the returned addons, with each card showing status based on already_included_addon_ids.
7. The user can switch category tabs, adjust filters, and re-apply them; each change updates URL parameters and triggers a new call to getAddonsForListing.

Navigate from My Plan to a specific category and add an add-on:
1. From my_plan.html, the user clicks a link to explore add-ons, navigating to addons.html with URL parameters such as category and from=my_plan.
2. The page controller reads category and from from the URL. The from parameter is used to configure the breadcrumb/back navigation component.
3. The page calls getAddonFilterOptions, validates the category parameter against the returned categories, and sets the active category accordingly.
4. Filters are initialized from URL parameters if present (e.g., max price or quality flags), otherwise from defaults based on getAddonFilterOptions.
5. The page calls getAddonsForListing with the active category, the computed filters, and the sort value from the URL.
6. The user reviews the add-ons, identifies one that is not in already_included_addon_ids, and clicks the card’s quick "Add to My Plan" action.
7. The page calls addAddonToDraftSubscription with the selected add-on ID.
8. If the response indicates success, the controller navigates the user to my_plan.html with URL parameters from=addons and changePreview=true. If the call is not successful, an error message is displayed and the user remains on addons.html.

View details for a specific add-on from the listing:
1. The user arrives on addons.html (with or without URL parameters) and the page has already loaded categories, filters, and the add-on list via getAddonFilterOptions and getAddonsForListing.
2. The user identifies an add-on of interest (for example, a sports add-on with certain quality characteristics) in the grid.
3. The user clicks anywhere configured as the details trigger on the add-on card (e.g., the card body or a "View details" link).
4. The page navigates to addon_detail.html, passing the add-on’s ID in the addonId URL parameter.
5. The detail page then uses the addonId to render its own content.

Refine listing using filters and URL-based state:
1. The user is on addons.html with an initial list already loaded.
2. The user adjusts the maximum monthly price control and toggles one or more quality checkboxes in the filter bar.
3. The user confirms the filter changes (for example, via an "Apply" action) which updates the URL parameters to reflect the chosen filter values.
4. The controller reads the updated URL parameters and calls getAddonsForListing with the current category, the new filters, and the current sort.
5. The listing updates with the new addons and applied_filters returned from the interface; the filter bar synchronizes its visible state with applied_filters.
6. The user can share or bookmark the URL; when revisited, the controller uses those URL parameters to re-initialize filters and reload the listing in the same state.
interactions:
Category tab click:
- When the user clicks a category tab, the active category is updated.
- The URL parameter category is set to the selected category value.
- The controller calls getAddonsForListing with the new category and the current filters and sort.
- The add-on list component re-renders with the returned addons.

Filter control change (price slider or equivalent):
- When the user adjusts the maximum monthly price input, the new value is stored in the page’s in-memory state.
- This change alone does not have to trigger data loading until the user confirms filter application (depending on the chosen interaction pattern).
- On filter application, the corresponding URL parameter for maximum price is updated.
- The controller calls getAddonsForListing with the updated filter object; the listing updates and applied_filters is used to display the effective filter values.

Filter control change (quality checkboxes):
- When the user toggles a quality checkbox (e.g., HD or 4K), the selected boolean values are stored in the page state.
- On applying the filters, the URL parameters for these quality flags are updated.
- The controller calls getAddonsForListing with the updated filter object, including hasHdChannels and has4kChannels.
- The component reconciles the UI with applied_filters from the response to ensure it reflects the actual filters in use.

"Reset filters" interaction:
- When the user triggers a filter reset action, the page removes filter-related URL parameters and sets filters back to defaults derived from getAddonFilterOptions.
- The controller calls getAddonsForListing again with the default filters.
- The listing and filter UI both update to reflect the reset state.

Sort selection interaction:
- When the user changes the sort option (if exposed by the UI), the selected sort key is stored and the URL parameter for sort is updated.
- The controller calls getAddonsForListing with the current category, current filters, and the updated sort value.
- The addon list re-renders in the new order.

Add-on card click for details:
- When the user clicks the designated area on an add-on card to view details, the page reads that card’s add-on ID.
- The page navigates to addon_detail.html with addonId set to that ID in the URL.
- No changes are made to the subscription state in this interaction.

"Add to My Plan" button click on an add-on card:
- When the user clicks the quick add button on an add-on that is not already included, the page calls addAddonToDraftSubscription with that add-on’s ID.
- If the response indicates success, the response.subscription_change is available in case it is needed for pre-navigation logic; then the page navigates to my_plan.html with from=addons and changePreview=true.
- If success is false, the page shows the returned message in an error feedback component and does not navigate away.

Included add-on indicator:
- When rendering each add-on card, the component checks whether the add-on’s ID exists in already_included_addon_ids from getAddonsForListing.
- If it is included, the card shows an "Included" state (and the quick add action is disabled or hidden as defined by the interaction rules).
- If it is not included, the quick add action is available.

Back navigation based on origin:
- When the user activates the back link in the breadcrumb or navigation header, the page checks the from URL parameter.
- If from indicates a known origin (such as my_plan or dashboard), the page navigates to that source page.
- If from is not set or not recognized, the back navigation falls back to a generic destination or browser history (implementation-specific, but driven by the presence or absence of from).
page_functionality.state_logic: On initial load, the page controller reads URL parameters category and from, along with any page-defined filter and sort parameters (for example, a maximum monthly price, HD flag, 4K flag, and sort key). The controller calls getAddonFilterOptions to determine available categories and filter bounds. If a category parameter exists and matches one of the returned categories, that category is used as the active category; otherwise the controller sets the active category based on the first or otherwise designated default category from getAddonFilterOptions. Filter values are initialized from URL parameters when present and valid; if not present, defaults are derived from getAddonFilterOptions (for example, using the provided min and max values and quality filter definitions). The controller then calls getAddonsForListing with: (1) the active category value, (2) a filters object constructed from the current maximum price and quality flags, and (3) the current sort value (if present in the URL). The response.addons populates the listing; response.already_included_addon_ids is used to determine which cards are marked as included; response.applied_filters is used to normalize the filter UI to the effective values applied by the backend. Whenever the user changes category, applies filters, resets filters, or selects a different sort order, the page updates the relevant URL parameters and triggers a new call to getAddonsForListing so that the URL always reflects the current state. The from parameter is preserved in the URL across such interactions, so that back navigation continues to behave consistently. The page does not use localStorage for state; all sharable state is derived from or synchronized back into URL parameters, and the only state stored in memory between calls is transient UI state used to construct parameters for subsequent interface calls.

components.functionality: Coordinates page initialization and data loading: reads URL parameters, calls getAddonFilterOptions, determines the active category and initial filters, calls getAddonsForListing with the derived parameters, and updates child components with the results. It also reacts to URL changes triggered by interactions (category change, filter application, sort change) by reloading the data.
Displays contextual navigation based on the from URL parameter and provides a back action to return to the originating page when available.

Renders category tabs using categories from getAddonFilterOptions. Highlights the active category based on the category URL parameter and allows the user to switch categories, which updates the URL and triggers a new listing load.

Provides controls for maximum monthly price and quality attributes. Initializes control bounds and labels from getAddonFilterOptions and current values from URL parameters or applied_filters. On apply, it updates filter-related URL parameters and requests a reload of the add-on listing; on reset, it clears filter parameters and returns to defaults.

Allows the user to select a sort order for the add-on listing. The selected sort key is stored in a URL parameter and passed unchanged to getAddonsForListing; changes trigger a reload of the listing via the page controller.

Displays the list of add-ons returned from getAddonsForListing as a grid or list of cards. Each card reflects whether the add-on is already included (using already_included_addon_ids) and exposes interactions to view details or perform a quick add to the user’s plan.

Represents a single add-on within the addons-list component. Shows main fields from the Addon model and an inclusion indicator if the add-on ID is present in already_included_addon_ids. Provides a clickable area to navigate to addon_detail.html and a quick add button when the add-on is not already included.

Handles the quick add flow from an add-on card. When invoked with an add-on ID, calls addAddonToDraftSubscription; on success, triggers navigation to my_plan.html with from=addons and changePreview=true; on failure, updates the feedback component with the returned message.

Displays success or error messages related to add-on actions, such as failures from addAddonToDraftSubscription. Messages are ephemeral and are cleared when the user dismisses the banner or performs a new action.

Shows an appropriate message or guidance when getAddonsForListing returns no addons for the current category and filter combination. This component is only visible when the addons array is empty and there is no active error.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page content follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow within a centered max-width container: (1) global header (outside this spec); (2) Add-ons page region starting with breadcrumbs and a large left-aligned 'Add-ons' heading; (3) a full-width feedback notification banner on a lighter strip directly under the heading when active; (4) category tabs in a single horizontal row; (5) a combined control row with filters on the left and sorting on the right; (6) the add-ons list as a multi-column grid of addon cards occupying the main vertical space; (7) an empty state panel replacing the grid when there are no results; (8) global footer below (outside this spec). A floating support/call button remains anchored to the bottom-right, overlaying all content but not affecting layout flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page content follows the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom flow within a centered max-width container: (1) global header (outside this spec); (2) Add-ons page region starting with breadcrumbs and a large left-aligned 'Add-ons' heading; (3) a full-width feedback notification banner on a lighter strip directly under the heading when active; (4) category tabs in a single horizontal row; (5) a combined control row with filters on the left and sorting on the right; (6) the add-ons list as a multi-column grid of addon cards occupying the main vertical space; (7) an empty state panel replacing the grid when there are no results; (8) global footer below (outside this spec). A floating support/call button remains anchored to the bottom-right, overlaying all content but not affecting layout flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and footer remain relatively shallow bands compared to the tall, content-focused center. Inside the Add-ons page controller, horizontal padding mirrors the mockup: the main content column is centered with generous side margins (~64–80px from viewport edges or a ~1200px max-width). Vertical spacing follows the system: 32–48px gap below the header to the page heading, 16–24px between closely related elements (breadcrumbs to heading, heading to feedback bar, tabs to filters), and larger 32–48px gaps above the grid and before the footer. The add-ons grid consumes the majority of the page height, while the notification banner and control rows remain shallow bands. The empty state, when shown, opens up more vertical space with a tall central block reminiscent of the empty cart feel.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is medium and consistent with the mockup. The top of the page (breadcrumbs, heading, notification, tabs, filters/sort) feels airy, with clear separation and generous padding. The grid introduces more visual density via multiple addon cards, but each card still has comfortable internal padding and clear separation between rows and columns. The empty state is low-density, with a central cluster of content surrounded by dark negative space. Overall, there are no crowded tables; components are simple, flat, and easy to scan.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow mirrors the mockup. The eye first catches the neon accent CTA in the global header (outside this spec), then drops into the Add-ons region: breadcrumbs subtly cue location, then the large 'Add-ons' heading anchors the page. Immediately below, the high-contrast feedback banner (on a lighter background) draws attention to system messages. From there, the gaze moves down through the category tabs and the filters/sort controls, setting context for browsing. The eye then settles into a vertical scanning pattern over the addon cards in the grid, moving left-to-right across rows. When no results exist, the centralized empty state content becomes the main focal point. Throughout, the bottom-right floating action button remains a secondary attractor, outside the main flow.

layout.overall_description: Within a full-width dark background bounded by a global header on top and a tall footer at the bottom, the Add-ons page content sits inside a centered, max-width column aligned to a 12-column grid. At the top of this column, slim breadcrumbs sit just above a large, left-aligned 'Add-ons' page title, both hugging the left grid edge. Just below, a light-toned notification strip spans the full content width, creating a strong horizontal band that stands out against the dark theme when feedback is present. Under that, a row of category tabs runs horizontally, aligned to the left but extending across most of the column, followed by a control bar where filters cluster on the left and a sort dropdown anchors to the right. Beneath this airy control area, the primary content—the add-ons list—fills the screen with a responsive grid of dark, flat cards arranged in two to four columns depending on viewport size. Each card is compact but well-padded, with neon-accented CTAs serving as focal points against the black background. If there are no matching add-ons, the grid area is replaced by a centered empty-state message and call-to-action, surrounded by generous negative space to echo the original cart-empty feeling. The entire main content block maintains consistent left alignment with the logo and footer content, while a floating support/call button hovers in the bottom-right corner atop all content, reinforcing the modern, tech-focused aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines the main Add-ons content region between the global header and footer. It centers a max-width content column (approximately 1120–1200px or about 80% of the viewport on large screens) within the full-width dark background, aligning to a 12-column grid. There is a vertical offset of roughly 32–40px below the bottom edge of the global navigation bar before the first element inside (the breadcrumbs) appears. Within this region, all other Add-ons components (breadcrumbs, heading, feedback banner, tabs, filter/sort row, grid, and empty state) are stacked in a top-to-bottom order, left-aligned to the same grid start. The page controller ensures consistent horizontal padding of around 64px from the viewport edges on desktop, collapsing to 16–24px on smaller screens. It also provides a bottom margin of about 40–48px above the start of the footer so that the grid or empty state does not visually collide with the footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static container in normal document flow; governs the placement and alignment of all Add-ons components but does not scroll independently from the page (the entire document scrolls as one).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs appear at the very top of the Add-ons content area inside the page controller, directly beneath the global header. They form a single, thin horizontal line of text, left-aligned with the main grid (starting at the same column as the page title) and spanning only as much width as needed for the breadcrumb trail, leaving empty space to the right. There is a modest top margin of about 8–12px from the page controller’s top edge and a gap of roughly 8–16px below before the 'Add-ons' heading. The typography is smaller and lower-contrast than the main title, keeping the row visually light so it doesn’t compete for attention.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element in normal flow. On narrower screens it may wrap to two lines but remains anchored to the top of the Add-ons content, without any sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category tabs form a horizontal navigation bar positioned below the heading/feedback cluster. In the default state where the feedback banner is visible, the tabs sit approximately 24px below the bottom edge of the feedback strip; if the banner is absent, they move up to sit 24–32px below the 'Add-ons' heading. The tab row spans the full content width within the page controller but visually groups toward the left, with individual tabs laid out side-by-side in a single line. Each tab occupies enough horizontal space for its label with around 16–24px horizontal padding, and 8–12px vertical padding, following the flat, slightly rounded style (small corner radius) without heavy shadows. Selected and hover states use the neon accent color either as an underline or pill background, creating a subtle but visible horizontal band that helps the eye move from left to right across categories. Below the tab row there is a vertical gap of about 16–24px before the filter/sort row begins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and in flow; the entire tab bar scrolls away with the rest of the content. On smaller screens, tabs can horizontally scroll within the same row if they overflow, but the row’s top position relative to nearby elements remains fixed in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter bar is part of a shared control row located immediately below the category tabs. This row stretches across the full width of the content column, with the filter bar occupying the left two-thirds to three-quarters of the horizontal space. Inside this left segment, filter controls (such as price range, quality, and channel count) are arranged in a single line where possible, using compact inputs and pill-like filter chips with 8–12px vertical and 12–16px horizontal padding. Items are left-aligned and spaced roughly 8–16px apart, maintaining good separation without feeling dense. The control row has a total height of around 48–56px, with 16px vertical padding, and sits about 16–24px below the tabs. Below this row, a larger gap of around 24–32px opens before the first row of addon cards or the empty state, emphasizing that the filters form a distinct control band above the content results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static control block within normal flow. On wider screens, filters remain in a single horizontal strip; on narrower screens, they can wrap to two lines or stack, but they remain above the grid and below the tabs and do not become sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort selector lives on the same horizontal row as the filter bar, but anchored to the far right edge of the content column. It appears as a compact dropdown or segmented control with a label (e.g., 'Sort by') and a select element, collectively occupying about 160–220px of width. The control is right-aligned so that its right edge lines up with the grid’s rightmost column, visually balancing the filters on the left. Vertically, it is centered within the 48–56px-high control row shared with the filter bar, giving the appearance of a single cohesive toolbar. On smaller screens, when the horizontal space is limited, the sort selector drops below the filters, left-aligning beneath them while preserving an 8–12px vertical gap so the two remain visually separated but tightly linked.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, aligned to the right within a flex-style row on large screens and stacking below filters on small screens. It does not overlay or float; it simply shifts layout responsively.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-ons list occupies the main central area of the page as a responsive grid sitting directly below the filter/sort control row. It begins about 24–32px beneath the control row, preserving clear separation between controls and content. The grid spans the full content width within the page controller and aligns to the 12-column system: on large screens, this typically renders as a three- or four-column layout (e.g., 3 cards per row with generous 24px gutters, or 4 narrower cards with 16–24px gutters). Each row of cards is separated by about 24–32px vertically, creating a consistent rhythm down the page. The grid extends downward as needed, likely consuming the majority of the viewport’s height, and continues below the fold when many add-ons are available. The dark background remains visible between cards, keeping a breathable structure even when the grid is full. There is a bottom margin of roughly 40–48px between the end of the last row and the start of the footer to maintain the same visual cushion as the mockup’s cart and footer transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A standard responsive grid in normal flow, expanding vertically as more add-ons exist. Card widths adapt with breakpoints (e.g., 1 column on small screens, 2 on tablets, 3–4 on desktop), but the grid always remains centered within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each addon card is an individual tile within the addons list grid, occupying one grid column with consistent width and height across a row. The card is a flat, dark rectangle with slightly rounded corners (around 8px radius) and no visible shadow, relying instead on a subtle border or glow on hover to signal interactivity. Internal padding is generous—about 16–24px on all sides—creating a comfortable layout for its contents. At the top-left, the add-on name and short descriptor stack vertically, while a small logo or badge can sit either to the left of the name or in the top-right corner. Just below, a row displays key attributes such as price, HD/4K quality, and channel count, arranged in a single line or two compact lines with 8px spacing. The lower portion of the card features action elements: the primary 'Add' CTA button, styled with the fluorescent green accent, sits either full-width along the bottom or aligned to the bottom-right, with perhaps a secondary 'Details' link aligned to the left. The button uses 8–12px vertical and 16–24px horizontal padding. Overall card height is moderate—roughly 220–260px—so that multiple rows can fit on screen, keeping the page feeling active but not crowded.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static tile element within the grid. On hover or focus, the card may slightly brighten its border or background and the CTA may glow, but it does not lift with strong shadows. It resizes responsively with its grid column but maintains its internal spacing proportions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-ons add-action handler is a non-visual controller that underpins the 'Add' or 'Remove' actions on each addon card. It does not occupy any visible space in the layout. Conceptually, it is attached to the CTA elements within each card, orchestrating state changes (e.g., switching the button to 'Added' or triggering feedback messages) without introducing separate UI components. When actions are processed, any visible effect appears through state changes in the cards themselves and through the feedback notification banner rather than through additional on-screen elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No spatial footprint; behaves purely as logic behind interactive elements. It does not alter the page flow directly, only indirectly via state changes in other components like cards and the feedback banner.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The feedback component mirrors the mockup’s notification strip. It appears as a full-width horizontal banner inside the page controller, positioned directly under the 'Add-ons' heading and above the category tabs. It stretches from the left to the right edges of the main content column, forming a shallow band roughly 48–64px tall. The background is noticeably lighter than the surrounding dark page (e.g., a near-black gray or muted dark tone), increasing contrast with the main content. Within the strip, an icon or status indicator sits on the far left followed by the message text, which occupies the majority of the width; optional actions (like 'Undo') can appear on the right. Content inside the banner is left-aligned, with 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. There is a small gap of about 16–24px above (from the heading) and below (to the tabs), matching the mockup’s spacing around the cart notification bar. When not active, this entire strip collapses, allowing the tabs to move up directly under the heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying a single row in the normal flow when visible and completely removed from layout when not. It does not stick to the top of the viewport; it scrolls away with the rest of the content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state appears in place of the addons list whenever filters or category selections yield no add-ons. It uses the same horizontal footprint as the grid—full width within the page controller—but organizes its content into a single, centered column. This block begins at the same vertical position where the first row of cards would normally appear, approximately 24–32px below the filter/sort controls. Inside, an icon or simple illustration sits at the top center, followed by a short headline like 'No add-ons match your filters' and explanatory text beneath, all center-aligned. A primary CTA button (e.g., 'Reset filters' or 'Browse all add-ons') in the neon accent color appears below the text. Vertical spacing within this column is generous (16–24px between elements), and the entire empty state area has tall top and bottom padding (40–64px), producing a large, calm pocket of negative space reminiscent of the empty cart’s open feel. The dark background of the page surrounds this central content, reinforcing the sense of an empty canvas.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Mutually exclusive with the addons list; when active, it occupies that space in the normal flow. It remains static as the user scrolls and centers horizontally within the main content column. On taller screens, it may appear roughly in the middle of the viewport, but it does not fix to the viewport; content above can scroll off-screen.

name: Add-on Detail
title: Add-on Detail
description: Displays full details for a selected IPTV add-on package, including included channels and pricing, and lets the user add the add-on to their subscription and continue to the plan summary.

core_features: Read the add-on identifier from the URL (addonId) and load detailed add-on data using getAddonDetail.
Display the add-on’s name, description, marketing highlight, pricing, HD/4K availability, and included channel count.
List all channels included in the add-on using the included_channels data returned by getAddonDetail, with optional client-side filtering (e.g., by genre or HD availability).
Indicate whether the add-on is already part of the user’s current subscription using is_already_in_plan from getAddonDetail.
Allow the user to add the add-on to a draft subscription change by calling addAddonToDraftSubscription, and then route to my_plan.html for review.
Provide navigation back to the add-ons listing (addons.html), preserving the category context via URL parameters when available.
user_workflows: View add-on details: (1) User lands on addon_detail.html with ?addonId=... in the URL (optionally also ?category=...). (2) Page initializes and calls getAddonDetail(addonId). (3) On success, the page displays the add-on summary (name, price, HD/4K flags, highlight, included channel count) and full description. (4) The page lists all included channels with basic metadata such as name, genre, language, and HD/4K flags.

Add an add-on that is not yet in the plan: (1) User reviews the add-on details and confirms it is not already in their plan (UI reflects is_already_in_plan=false). (2) User clicks the primary action button (e.g., “Add to My Plan”). (3) Page calls addAddonToDraftSubscription(addonId) and shows a loading state for the button. (4) If the API call fails (success=false), the page displays message from the response and leaves the user on the same page. (5) If the API call succeeds (success=true), the page navigates to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail&changePreview=true so the user can review the updated plan summary reflecting the add-on.

Review an add-on already in the plan: (1) User lands on addon_detail.html for an add-on where getAddonDetail returns is_already_in_plan=true. (2) Page indicates that the add-on is already included in the current subscription and adjusts the call-to-action (e.g., disabled add button or alternative action like “Review in My Plan”). (3) If the user clicks the review action, the page redirects to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail&changePreview=true without calling addAddonToDraftSubscription, allowing the user to review their existing plan configuration.

Navigate back to add-on listings: (1) User arrives at addon_detail.html from addons.html with an optional category parameter (e.g., addon_detail.html?addonId=...&category=...). (2) The breadcrumb and “Back to Add-ons” link use the category parameter to build a return URL to addons.html?category=... . (3) When the user clicks the back link, the page navigates to addons.html with the same category value so the user sees the same category listing as before.
interactions: On page load, the page reads addonId from the URL query string; if addonId is missing or empty, it shows an error state instead of calling getAddonDetail.

On successful getAddonDetail response, the page transitions from a loading state to a content state, binding the returned addon object to summary, description, and pricing components, and binding included_channels to the channel list component.
If getAddonDetail fails (e.g., network error or invalid addonId), the page shows an error message with an option to navigate back to addons.html.
The user can click client-side filters (such as genre or HD-only toggles) in the channel list component, which filter the already-loaded included_channels array without additional API calls.
The primary call-to-action button for adding the add-on is enabled only when getAddonDetail has succeeded and is_already_in_plan is false; clicking it triggers addAddonToDraftSubscription(addonId).
While addAddonToDraftSubscription is in progress, the primary button shows a loading state and is temporarily disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.
If addAddonToDraftSubscription returns success=false, the page displays the message string from the response in an inline notification area and keeps the user on the add-on detail page.
If addAddonToDraftSubscription returns success=true, the page redirects the user to my_plan.html with URL parameters from=addon_detail and changePreview=true.
The breadcrumb and “Back to Add-ons” action use the category parameter from the current page URL, if present, to construct the navigation target addons.html?category=<currentCategory>.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary page state is driven by URL parameters and the results of the assigned interfaces. On initialization, the page reads addonId from the query string; if addonId is absent or invalid, the page enters an "invalid-parameters" error state and does not call getAddonDetail. If addonId is present, the page calls getAddonDetail(addonId) and enters a "loading" state. On success, it transitions to a "loaded" state with the addon object, the included_channels array, and the is_already_in_plan flag available to all child components via data binding. The optional category parameter (if present in the URL) is stored in memory for navigation construction only and is not persisted beyond the current page; it is used to build the link back to addons.html. The call-to-action component derives its behavior from is_already_in_plan: when false, the primary button triggers addAddonToDraftSubscription(addonId); when true, the button instead navigates directly to my_plan.html without mutating the draft subscription. After an addAddonToDraftSubscription call, if success=true, the page issues a client-side redirect to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail&changePreview=true. All state transitions (loading, loaded, error, submitting) are maintained in memory and are not stored in localStorage; URL parameters are the only persisted state across navigation.

components.functionality: Initializes the page by reading addonId (and optional category) from the URL, validates addonId, calls getAddonDetail(addonId), and exposes loading, error, and loaded states plus the loaded data (addon, included_channels, is_already_in_plan) to child components.
event_handlers: onInit: Read addonId and category from URL query parameters and validate addonId.
onInitValidAddonId: Invoke getAddonDetail(addonId) and set internal state to loading.
onGetAddonDetailSuccess: Store addon, included_channels, and is_already_in_plan from the response and set state to loaded.
onGetAddonDetailError: Set state to error and provide an error message for display.
onInvalidAddonId: Set state to error with an appropriate message without calling getAddonDetail.

components.functionality: Displays a breadcrumb trail and a "Back to Add-ons" link, using the optional category URL parameter and loaded add-on data to construct navigation paths.
event_handlers: onRender: Build breadcrumb items using Add-ons > [Derived Category] > [Addon.name] when addon data is available.
onClickBackToAddons: Navigate to addons.html, including ?category=URLParam.category when category is present in the current URL.
