onClickCategoryCrumb: Navigate to addons.html?category=URLParam.category when both addon and category data are available.

components.functionality: Shows the key summary details of the add-on including name, monthly price, marketing highlight, included channel count, and indicators for HD/4K availability and whether the add-on is already part of the user’s plan.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: When addon data is available, render summary information and state indicators.
onPriceInfoToggle: Optional interaction to show or hide additional price breakdown information, if implemented using the available monthly_price field.

components.functionality: Displays the full description of the add-on for users to understand what the add-on includes and any relevant details beyond the summary.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: Render the description when available; if the description is empty or not provided, hide or collapse this section.

components.functionality: Lists all channels included in the add-on using the included_channels array returned by getAddonDetail, and optionally allows client-side filtering (e.g., by genre, HD/4K) without additional API calls.
event_handlers: onDataLoaded: Render a list of channels using the included_channels array.
onFilterChange: Update internal filter state (such as selected genre or HD-only toggle) and re-render the list using only the channels that match the selected filters.
onClearFilters: Reset filters to show the full included_channels list.

components.functionality: Provides the main subscription-related action buttons. If the add-on is not in the current plan, lets the user add it by calling addAddonToDraftSubscription and then routes to my_plan.html for review; if the add-on is already in the plan, provides a direct navigation to my_plan.html for review without modifying the draft subscription.
event_handlers: onRender: Determine the primary button label and enabled/disabled state based on getAddonDetail.is_already_in_plan and the loader’s error/loading states.
onClickAddToPlan: When is_already_in_plan is false, call addAddonToDraftSubscription(Addon.id), set a submitting state, and disable the button until the API call completes.
onAddAddonSuccess: If addAddonToDraftSubscription returns success=true, redirect to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail&changePreview=true.
onAddAddonFailure: If addAddonToDraftSubscription returns success=false, display addAddonToDraftSubscription.message in an inline error area and re-enable the button.
onClickReviewInPlan: When is_already_in_plan is true, navigate directly to my_plan.html?from=addon_detail&changePreview=true without calling addAddonToDraftSubscription.

components.functionality: Displays status messages and errors related to loading the add-on details or adding the add-on to the draft subscription.
event_handlers: onGetAddonDetailError: Show an error notification when loading add-on details fails, with guidance to retry or go back to the add-ons page.
onAddAddonFailure: Show an error notification using addAddonToDraftSubscription.message when the add-on cannot be added to the draft subscription.
onDismissNotification: Allow the user to manually dismiss visible notifications while keeping the underlying error state for debugging or telemetry purposes.

components.functionality: Manages and displays global loading and error indicators for the page based on the state of getAddonDetail and addAddonToDraftSubscription.
event_handlers: onInit: Show a global loading indicator while getAddonDetail is in progress.
onGetAddonDetailSuccess: Hide the global loading indicator and show main content sections.
onGetAddonDetailError: Hide loading indicators and show a full-page error state with a navigation option back to addons.html.
onAddAddonSubmitting: Optionally show an inline loading indicator on the CTA panel when addAddonToDraftSubscription is in progress.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the cart mockup. After the global dark header (shared across the site), the Add-on Detail page content flows as: breadcrumb navigation at the very top of the content column, then the add-on summary header, followed (when present) by a full-width notification bar. Below that, the descriptive text and channel list stack vertically. The plan CTA panel sits toward the bottom of the main content area as the last major block before the site-wide footer. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this central column, while the data loader orchestrates what is visible.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page uses a linear, top-to-bottom single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the cart mockup. After the global dark header (shared across the site), the Add-on Detail page content flows as: breadcrumb navigation at the very top of the content column, then the add-on summary header, followed (when present) by a full-width notification bar. Below that, the descriptive text and channel list stack vertically. The plan CTA panel sits toward the bottom of the main content area as the last major block before the site-wide footer. Loading and error states temporarily replace or overlay this central column, while the data loader orchestrates what is visible.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and footer span full width, but the Add-on Detail content is constrained to a centered container with generous side margins (~64–80px from viewport edges). Within this container, the breadcrumb and notification bar occupy shallow horizontal bands. The summary header and CTA panel each get slightly more vertical height to emphasize decision points. The description and channel list together form the main vertical body. Major section separations follow the 32–48px vertical rhythm: ~24px between closely related elements (breadcrumb ↔ heading, heading ↔ notification), and ~32–48px between major blocks (notification ↔ description, description ↔ channel list, channel list ↔ CTA panel, CTA panel ↔ footer).
layout_strategies.content_density: The top of the page (breadcrumb + summary header + notification) is visually airy with ample breathing room, reflecting the mockup's light central area. Text elements in description and channel list are moderately dense but maintain comfortable line spacing and clear grouping. The CTA panel is concise with few elements and generous padding, while still feeling substantial. Overall density stays medium: no large tables or complex grids, just stacked sections with clear separation, echoing the cart page’s minimal, focused feel.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: User attention enters from the global header (logo and neon primary CTA), then drops into this page's breadcrumb (orientation), then moves immediately to the add-on summary header (title and key pricing). Next, the eye is drawn horizontally across the light notification bar, which stands out against the dark background. From there, the flow continues straight down through the description, then into the more detailed channel list. Finally, attention lands on the CTA panel at the bottom of the content column, which reprises the neon accent and frames the primary action before transitioning into the dense, multi-column global footer and the floating call button in the bottom-right corner.

layout.overall_description: The Add-on Detail page sits on a dark, full-width background that matches the site’s high-contrast, tech-focused aesthetic. A global header spans the top, but the main content is constrained to a wide, centered column aligned to a 12-column grid. At the very top of this column, a slim breadcrumb sits with subtle text, followed by a more prominent add-on summary header that anchors the page with the add-on name and core pricing. Directly below, a high-contrast light notification bar, stretching nearly the full width of the content container, communicates status information and visually echoes the cart page’s notification strip. The midsection of the page is a clean, single column: first the add-on’s description in a readable width, then the channel list, which extends downward and becomes the most text-dense part of the page. Toward the bottom of the main content, a bold CTA panel with neon-accented buttons and concise plan summary spans the content width, acting as the main decision point before the user reaches the multi-column global footer. Throughout, vertical spacing follows a consistent 8px-based rhythm, and the user’s eye travels in a straightforward top-to-bottom path, with the floating call button in the bottom-right remaining visible as an optional secondary action outside the main document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader component is not visually represented as a discrete block in the standard layout; instead, it controls which of the main content sections are visible. It conceptually spans the entire main content area between the global header and the footer, matching the width of the centered content container. When active in a full-loading state, it can trigger an overlay that covers this central column—from just below the header to just above the footer—while leaving the background header and footer visually intact. This overlay is aligned to the same centered grid and occupies roughly the middle third of the viewport height, with any spinner or loading message centered both horizontally and vertically within the visible area. When idle, it collapses entirely and occupies no visible space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as an invisible controller in normal use. During loading, it may activate a semi-opaque overlay fixed to the viewport over the main content column, keeping the global header and footer still visible behind or above/below it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumb navigation is placed at the very top of the page’s main content column, sitting immediately below the global header and aligned to the left edge of the 12-column centered grid. It spans roughly 8–10 columns on larger screens, but its text content remains relatively compact, leaving generous empty space to its right. There is a modest vertical gap (~24px) between the bottom of the global header and the top of the breadcrumb, and another ~16–24px between the bottom of the breadcrumb and the start of the add-on summary header. Vertically, it forms a shallow horizontal band that visually orients the user without competing with the main heading. Padding above and below the breadcrumb text follows the small scale (8–16px), ensuring it feels subtle and airy against the dark background.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow; it scrolls away naturally as the user moves down the page and does not stick to the top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The add-on summary header sits directly beneath the breadcrumb within the same centered grid container, acting as the primary focal block at the top of the content. It spans most of the content width, approximately 8–12 columns depending on screen size, with the main title left-aligned. Any key pricing or short meta info can be aligned to the right within the same horizontal band, creating a balanced left-to-right scan similar to the header’s logo-nav-CTA structure. There is a clear vertical gap (~16–24px) above it (from the breadcrumb) and a slightly larger gap (~24–32px) below it before the next element (usually the status notification bar). The height of this section is moderate: enough for a large, bold title on one or two lines with a single secondary line beneath, maintaining a spacious feel. The background remains the page’s dark tone, allowing any neon accent (e.g., small pill labels) to pop without overwhelming the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and anchored at the top of the main content area; it scrolls with the page and acts as the starting point of the main vertical content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status notification appears as a full-width horizontal bar directly below the add-on summary header, echoing the cart page’s light notification strip. It fills the entire width of the centered content container (all 12 columns within that container), creating a strong contrast against the surrounding dark background. The bar’s background is a light neutral tone, with a compact icon or status indicator anchored at the far left and the message text running to the right in a single or double line. Vertical padding within the bar follows the pattern of 8–16px, making it visually comfortable but not tall. There is a consistent vertical gap (~24px) between the bottom of the summary header and the top of this bar, and another gap (~24–32px) separating the bottom of the bar from the top of the description section. When no status is present, this bar disappears completely, and the description section moves up to occupy its space, preserving the linear flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the content column. It expands or collapses in place based on whether there is a message to show, but does not overlay other content or remain sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The description section is placed immediately below the status notification bar (or directly below the summary header when no status is shown), separated by a comfortable vertical gap of about 24–32px. Its content is left-aligned and limited to a readable width—roughly 6–8 of the 12 columns—to avoid overly long line lengths. On larger screens, this narrower text block sits centered within the main container, often starting at the same left alignment as the heading while leaving empty dark space to the right, which reduces visual density and maintains the airy feel. Vertically, the description can span multiple text lines or short paragraphs, with line spacing reflecting the medium density of the design. The bottom of this section is separated from the channel list by another 24–32px gap, creating clear hierarchy: summary at the top, then explanation, then detailed content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main flow, expanding vertically as needed to accommodate content while always respecting the centered grid and established vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channel list sits directly beneath the description section, forming the main body of detailed content in the central column. It spans a wider portion of the grid—typically 8–10 of the 12 columns—to match the sense of a full-width informational block, while still aligning to the same left edge as the description and summary. A vertical gap of about 24–32px separates its top from the description. Within this block, channels may be arranged in one or two text columns or grouped by category, but overall it reads as a vertically scrolling list. Line spacing and group spacing maintain medium density: channels are clearly legible but not overly spaced out, keeping the section informative yet manageable. The bottom of the channel list transitions into the CTA panel with a slightly larger gap (~32–48px), signaling a shift from information to action.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, vertically scrollable content block that expands based on the number of channels. It remains within the central container and does not introduce independent scrolling regions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan CTA panel is positioned at the bottom of the main content column, just below the channel list, separated by a clear vertical gap of approximately 32–48px to give it breathing room and indicate it as the next step. It spans the full width of the centered content container (all 12 columns), visually echoing the full-width notification bar but with a dark background and strong neon accents for buttons and key pricing. Internally, the panel is structured as a horizontal band: on the left, a concise summary of what is being added (add-on name, price, billing cadence); on the right, one or two CTA buttons, with the primary action (e.g., “Add to Plan & Continue”) in bright neon and a secondary action in a more subdued outline or muted tone. The panel has generous internal padding (~16px vertical, 24–32px horizontal), making it feel substantial yet clean. Below this panel, there is a comfortable vertical margin (~32–48px) before the start of the global footer, preserving the page’s top-to-bottom flow and ensuring the CTA is visually anchored as the last main decision point.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow. It sits near the bottom of the page content but scrolls naturally; it does not stick to the viewport edge. On shorter pages where content does not extend far, it visually sits roughly in the lower half of the viewport, just above the footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading and error states occupy the same central content area as the main sections, but they simplify or replace the usual layout to focus the user’s attention. In a page-level loading state, a compact loading indicator and short status message are centered within the visible portion of the central column, roughly in the middle of the viewport height, while the dark background remains visible around it. Space above and below is generous, keeping the presentation minimal and calm. In an error state (for example, if the add-on cannot be loaded), a prominent message block appears centered in the content column where the summary header and subsequent sections would normally begin. This block spans about 6–8 columns, is left-aligned within that span, and includes a succinct error title, optional description, and perhaps a single action button to retry or go back. When these states are active, the usual sections (breadcrumb, summary header, description, channel list, CTA panel) are hidden or visually suppressed, preserving a clean single-column vertical focus.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in place of the normal content within the central column, aligned to the same grid. Depending on implementation, a loading state may also work with the data loader to show a semi-opaque overlay, but all remain static relative to scrolling rather than fixed to the viewport.

name: Checkout
title: Checkout
description: Confirm and finalize subscription plan, add-on, and billing cycle changes, including pricing, effective date, and any applied promo codes.

core_features: Load a pending subscription change summary using getCheckoutSummary, including current vs new plan, add-ons, billing cycle and pricing totals.
Display a detailed pricing comparison (monthly and annual) including current charges, new charges, and any applied promo code savings.
Allow the user to select when the change becomes effective (e.g., immediately or on the next billing cycle) using the effective_date_options from getCheckoutSummary.

Show contextual confirmation copy and primary button label (e.g., Confirm, Confirm Upgrade, Update Plan, Confirm Billing Change) based on the URL parameters from and context plus the loaded change_type and pricing deltas.
Finalize the subscription change by submitting the selected effective date and context to confirmSubscriptionChange and handle success or error responses.

After a successful confirmation, present navigation options back to dashboard.html, my_plan.html, or billing.html, including context-specific primary actions like Go to My Account, View Updated Plan, or Return to Billing Overview.
Handle edge cases where there is no pending change (has_pending_change is false) by showing an appropriate message and navigation options without allowing confirmation.
user_workflows:
Standard plan or add-on change from My Plan (from=my_plan, context=plan_or_addon_change):
1) User initiates a plan or add-on change on my_plan.html and is navigated to checkout.html?from=my_plan&context=plan_or_addon_change.
2) On page load, the state manager component calls getCheckoutSummary and receives subscription_change, current_subscription, selected_plan, selected_addons, pricing_summary, and effective_date_options.
3) The checkout summary panel shows the current plan versus the new plan, current add-ons versus selected add-ons, and pricing comparison for monthly and annual totals.
4) If effective_date_options contains multiple options, the effective date selector defaults to subscription_change.effective_date_option if present, or the first option value, and lets the user change the selection.
5) Contextual copy and primary button label are determined. For example, if change_type is plan_change or combined and the new recurring price is higher than the current price, the primary button label is set to Confirm Upgrade; if lower or equal, it may be Update Plan or Confirm, depending on app rules.
6) The user clicks the confirm button, triggering confirmSubscriptionChange with the selected effectiveDateOption and the context from URL params.
7) On success, a success state is shown with the returned message and updated subscription summary, and the user is presented with navigation actions such as View Updated Plan (to my_plan.html?from=checkout) and Go to My Account (to dashboard.html?from=checkout).
8) On error, an error banner is displayed with the message from the API; the user can adjust the effective date or try again, or navigate back to my_plan.html.

Billing cycle change from Billing (from=billing, context=billing_cycle_change):
1) User initiates a billing cycle change (e.g., monthly to annual) on billing.html and is navigated to checkout.html?from=billing&context=billing_cycle_change.
2) On load, the state manager calls getCheckoutSummary and binds the returned data.
3) The summary panel focuses on current vs new billing cycle, highlighting changes in monthly and annual totals and any promo code that applies to the new cycle.
4) If effective_date_options are provided (for example, effective immediately vs next billing cycle), the effective date selector component lets the user choose; if only one option is provided, it is selected and shown as read-only.
5) The primary button label is set to Confirm Billing Change based on the context.
6) On click of the confirm button, confirmSubscriptionChange is called with effectiveDateOption and context=billing_cycle_change.
7) On success, a confirmation message is shown along with a primary navigation action Return to Billing Overview (to billing.html?from=checkout) and a secondary action Go to My Account (to dashboard.html?from=checkout).
8) On error, the error banner shows the message and the user can retry or navigate back to billing.html.

New plan purchase from Plans (from=plans, context omitted or treated as plan_or_addon_change):
1) User selects a new plan (and optionally add-ons) from plans.html and is navigated to checkout.html?from=plans. If context is not provided, the page treats it as a plan_or_addon_change by default.
2) On load, the state manager calls getCheckoutSummary and receives the pending change data.
3) The summary panel shows selected_plan and selected_addons versus the current subscription (if any), plus recurring pricing and promo savings.
4) If effective_date_options exist (e.g., start immediately or next billing cycle), the user selects one; if not, the system uses the provided default effective_date_option on subscription_change when present.
5) The primary button label is set contextually (for example, Confirm or Confirm Upgrade, depending on whether this is effectively an upgrade and pricing difference vs current subscription).
6) When the user confirms, confirmSubscriptionChange is called with effectiveDateOption and a derived context (plan_or_addon_change if context was not present in the URL).
7) On success, the page shows a confirmation message and offers Go to My Account (to dashboard.html?from=checkout) and View Updated Plan (to my_plan.html?from=checkout).
8) On error, display the error and allow retry or navigation back to plans.html (navigation to plans.html is handled by site-wide navigation, not a dedicated outgoing connection in this spec).

No pending change edge case:
1) The user reaches checkout.html with from and context parameters, but getCheckoutSummary returns has_pending_change=false.
2) The state manager sets a no-pending-change state, which disables the confirmation form.
3) An informational message is displayed indicating there is no change to confirm.
4) Based on context and from, navigation suggestions are shown, such as View Current Plan (to my_plan.html?from=checkout) and Return to Billing Overview (to billing.html?from=checkout) for billing contexts, or Go to My Account (to dashboard.html?from=checkout).
5) The user chooses a navigation option to continue.
interactions:
Page load / initialization:
- Trigger: Page loads at checkout.html, optionally with ?from=<origin>&context=<context> in the URL.
- Behavior: The checkout-state-manager component reads URL parameters from and context, normalizes them (e.g., defaults context to plan_or_addon_change if missing and from is plans or my_plan), then calls getCheckoutSummary. While awaiting the response, it sets a loading state.
- On successful response: State is updated with has_pending_change, subscription_change, current_subscription, selected_plan, selected_addons, pricing_summary, effective_date_options. Dependent components (summary panel, effective date selector, confirmation form) are rendered or updated.
- On error from getCheckoutSummary: The error banner component displays the error message, and the confirmation form is disabled.

Effective date selection:
- Trigger: User selects an effective date option in the effective-date-selector component (for example, a radio button change event).
- Behavior: The selected option value is stored in page-level state (e.g., selectedEffectiveDateOption). If the user does not change it manually, the default value is set from subscription_change.effective_date_option or the first entry in effective_date_options.
- Impact: The selectedEffectiveDateOption is passed as effectiveDateOption to confirmSubscriptionChange when the user submits the confirmation form.

Confirmation button click (form submission):
- Trigger: User clicks the primary confirmation button in the checkout-confirmation-form component (e.g., Confirm, Confirm Upgrade, Update Plan, or Confirm Billing Change).
- Behavior: The component validates that an effectiveDateOption is selected (if required) and that has_pending_change is true. It then calls confirmSubscriptionChange with:
  - effectiveDateOption: the value from the effective date selector or default.
  - context: the context value derived from the URL (or a default, e.g., plan_or_addon_change, when URL context is not provided).
- While waiting for the API response, the button enters a loading state and the form is disabled to prevent duplicate submissions.
- On success: The state manager updates the page to a completed state, replaces the confirmation form with a confirmation result panel showing the message returned by the API and the updated_subscription summary, and reveals post-checkout navigation options.
- On failure (success=false or network error): The error banner shows the message, the form is re-enabled, and the user can try again.

Context-sensitive button labeling and copy:
- Trigger: After loading getCheckoutSummary data or when pricing_summary changes (if refetched), the state manager computes whether the change is an upgrade, downgrade, or lateral change by comparing new vs current totals for the active billing cycle.
- Behavior: The label of the primary confirmation button is set by rules such as:
  - If context === 'billing_cycle_change': label = "Confirm Billing Change".
  - Else if change_type is plan_change or combined and the new recurring price is greater than the current price: label = "Confirm Upgrade".
  - Else if change_type is addon_change only or new recurring price is lower or equal: label may be "Update Plan" or "Confirm" according to application rules.
- Impact: The user sees clear, context-appropriate labeling without requiring additional input.

Viewing pricing and promo breakdown:
- Trigger: User opens the checkout page and the summary panel renders.
- Behavior: The checkout-summary-panel and billing-and-promo-summary components bind to pricing_summary fields such as current_monthly_total, new_monthly_total, current_annual_total, new_annual_total, promo_code, promo_savings_amount and the billing_cycle / next_billing_date from current_subscription.
- Optional interactions: The user may expand or collapse detailed pricing sections (e.g., toggling to show monthly vs annual totals or showing a breakdown by base plan and add-ons). These interactions only change visibility; they do not trigger API calls.

Post-confirmation navigation actions:
- Trigger: After confirmSubscriptionChange succeeds, the user clicks one of the navigation buttons in the post-checkout-navigation component.
- Behavior: Based on which button is clicked and the context:
  - Clicking Go to My Account navigates to dashboard.html?from=checkout.
  - Clicking View Updated Plan navigates to my_plan.html?from=checkout.
  - Clicking Return to Billing Overview navigates to billing.html?from=checkout.
- The component uses the context and from values to decide which button is primary (for example, Return to Billing Overview is primary in billing_cycle_change flows).

Handling no pending change:
- Trigger: getCheckoutSummary returns has_pending_change=false.
- Behavior: The state manager hides or disables the confirmation form, shows an informational message (e.g., there is no subscription change to confirm), and exposes navigation options similar to the post-confirmation navigation component to let the user return to my_plan.html, billing.html, or dashboard.html depending on context and from.
- No call to confirmSubscriptionChange is allowed in this state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is primarily driven by URL parameters and the responses from getCheckoutSummary and confirmSubscriptionChange.

1) URL parameters and initial state:
- from: The origin of the checkout flow. Expected values include 'my_plan', 'billing', and 'plans'. The checkout-state-manager reads this parameter from the URL (e.g., using query-string parsing) and stores it in page state as originFrom.
- context: The type of subscription change being confirmed. Expected values include 'plan_or_addon_change' and 'billing_cycle_change'. If context is missing and from is 'my_plan' or 'plans', the page defaults context to 'plan_or_addon_change'. If context is missing and from is 'billing', it may default to 'billing_cycle_change'. The normalized context is stored as changeContext.
- These values do not come from localStorage; they are parsed directly from the URL each time the page loads or when the URL changes.

2) Data loading logic via getCheckoutSummary:
- Once originFrom and changeContext are set, getCheckoutSummary is called with no parameters (as defined). The returned object is stored in state as checkoutSummary, including:
  - has_pending_change
  - subscription_change (including change_type, selected_plan_id, selected_billing_cycle, selected_addon_ids, removed_addon_ids, promo_code_code, effective_date_option, estimated_new_monthly_total, estimated_new_annual_total, status)
  - current_subscription
  - selected_plan
  - selected_addons
  - pricing_summary
  - effective_date_options
- If has_pending_change is false, the page enters a no-change state. The confirmation form is not rendered or is disabled, and navigation hints are shown.

3) Derived state and behavior:
- activeBillingCycle: Derived from current_subscription.billing_cycle. Used for comparing monthly vs annual totals and for selecting which price difference to emphasize.
- isUpgrade / isDowngrade / isNeutral: Derived by comparing pricing_summary.new_monthly_total vs pricing_summary.current_monthly_total (or annual totals, depending on activeBillingCycle and application rules). The result influences:
  - Primary button label (e.g., Confirm Upgrade vs Update Plan).
  - Optional explanatory copy in the summary panel (e.g., how much more or less the user will pay).
- hasMultipleEffectiveDateOptions: Derived from effective_date_options.length > 1, used to decide whether to show a selector or a single, read-only label.
- selectedEffectiveDateOption: Initialized using subscription_change.effective_date_option if provided; otherwise using the value of the first entry in effective_date_options when available. When the user interacts with the effective-date-selector, this value is updated and ultimately passed to confirmSubscriptionChange.

4) Confirmation logic via confirmSubscriptionChange:
- On confirm, the page calls confirmSubscriptionChange with:
  - effectiveDateOption: selectedEffectiveDateOption.
  - context: changeContext (derived from URL and defaults).
- While waiting for the response, a pendingConfirmation flag is set to true, disabling form interactions and updating button state.
- On success (success=true):
  - confirmationResult is stored with message, updated_subscription, and subscription_change from the response.
  - The page state changes to confirmed=true, which hides the confirmation form and shows the confirmation result panel.
  - The navigation component then uses originFrom and changeContext to render appropriate next-step links.
- On failure (success=false or error):
  - confirmed remains false.
  - errorMessage is set from the response message or the error, and the error banner displays it.
  - pendingConfirmation is set back to false, re-enabling the form.

5) Navigation state and URL propagation:
- After a successful confirmation, navigation buttons construct URLs with from=checkout to preserve the origin of the navigation for downstream pages:
  - dashboard.html?from=checkout
  - my_plan.html?from=checkout
  - billing.html?from=checkout
- No state is persisted in localStorage; all context for subsequent pages is provided via these URL parameters and the server-side subscription state.

6) Conditional rendering based on state:
- If loading: Only a loading indicator or skeleton for the summary panel is shown; the confirmation form is disabled.
- If has_pending_change=false: No confirmation form; show no-change messaging and navigation.
- If confirmed=true: Hide the main confirmation form and show confirmation result plus navigation options.
- If errorMessage is set and confirmed=false: Show the error banner while keeping the form visible for retry.

This state logic ensures all behavior is driven by URL parameters, interface responses, and derived state, with no reliance on localStorage or hardcoded business data.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter parsing, data loading from getCheckoutSummary, derived-state computation (upgrade/downgrade, button labels, default effective date), and overall page state transitions before and after confirmSubscriptionChange.
Displays a concise comparison of the current subscription vs the pending change, including plan details, add-ons, billing cycle, and pricing differences using pricing_summary from getCheckoutSummary.
Shows available effective date options (e.g., immediately or next billing cycle) returned in effective_date_options and lets the user choose which one applies to the subscription change.

Summarizes billing context and promo impact, including current billing cycle, next billing date, current vs new recurring totals, the applied promo code (if any), and promo savings based on pricing_summary.

Provides the main confirmation controls, including the primary confirmation button with context-aware labeling and optional secondary actions (such as cancel or go back). On submit, it passes the selected effective date and context to confirmSubscriptionChange.

Displays error or warning messages related to loading the checkout summary or confirming the subscription change (e.g., network errors, invalid state, or business-rule validation errors from the backend).
Shown when getCheckoutSummary indicates has_pending_change=false, explaining that there is no subscription change to confirm and suggesting navigation options.
Visible after confirmSubscriptionChange succeeds; presents the confirmation message, optionally shows a snapshot of the updated subscription, and indicates which effective date was applied.

Provides context-sensitive navigation buttons after successful confirmation (or in the no-pending-change case) to dashboard.html, my_plan.html, or billing.html, appending from=checkout as a URL parameter.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page retains a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container. Above and below this container, the full-width dark header and footer mirror the mockup. Within the checkout content column, the order is: page heading ('Checkout'), optional error banner OR info message, then the main checkout stack (summary panel → effective date selector → billing & promo summary → confirmation form). When the checkout is completed, the confirmation/result panel replaces the form stack, followed by post-checkout navigation links. A floating support/call action (from the overall site pattern) still anchors bottom-right but sits outside this checkout component list.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page retains a linear, top-to-bottom, single-column content flow inside a centered max-width container. Above and below this container, the full-width dark header and footer mirror the mockup. Within the checkout content column, the order is: page heading ('Checkout'), optional error banner OR info message, then the main checkout stack (summary panel → effective date selector → billing & promo summary → confirmation form). When the checkout is completed, the confirmation/result panel replaces the form stack, followed by post-checkout navigation links. A floating support/call action (from the overall site pattern) still anchors bottom-right but sits outside this checkout component list.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The central checkout content sits in a max-width column (roughly 960–1200px) centered on the page, with generous side gutters (~64–80px to viewport edges) mirroring the mockup. The header occupies a shallow band at the top; the checkout column takes the central vertical band, but individual components are not crammed, so a lot of dark background remains visible. The footer consumes roughly the bottom third of the viewport with multiple columns. Vertically, 32–48px gaps separate major blocks (heading, banners, main panels, footer), while related components within a block (summary + effective date + billing summary + form) are separated by 16–24px.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density is medium: each major component (summary, billing, form, result) spans the full content column but has ample internal padding (16–24px) and breathing room. The error/info banners and info panels resemble the airy notification strip from the mockup—full-width bands with clear text and icon alignment. The footer remains the densest area, while the central checkout state keeps content concise: no multi-column form fields or crowded tables—just stacked, readable sections.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts in the full-width dark header at the logo and the neon primary CTA on the right, then drops into the main checkout column. First stop is the left-aligned 'Checkout' heading, then immediately the highest-priority banner (error or state/info) as a high-contrast strip. From there the gaze flows downward through the checkout summary, effective date selector, billing & promo details, and finally rests on the confirm button at the bottom of the form (often right-aligned to echo the header CTA). After submission, attention is drawn to the checkout result panel and then to the post-checkout navigation links just below. The floating support/call button at the bottom-right remains a secondary attractor outside the main vertical narrative.

layout.overall_description:
The page sits on a deep black background with a centered, max-width content column that feels spacious and focused. A full-width dark header runs across the top with the IPTV logo on the left, minimal nav in the middle, and a neon green CTA on the right. Below this, the main checkout content begins: a large, left-aligned 'Checkout' heading anchors the column. Directly under it, a full-width, light-toned notification-style band appears whenever there’s an error or state message, echoing the mockup’s cart notification bar.

Beneath this, a vertical stack of dark panels guides the user through confirmation: plan summary first, then effective date selection, then billing and promo details, and finally the confirmation form with a prominent neon confirm button. Each panel spans most of the content column, with 32–48px of vertical air between major sections and 16–24px between related subcomponents, keeping the dark canvas visible and maintaining the site’s sleek, modern feel. When the checkout is complete, the central stack is replaced by a bold confirmation/result panel, followed by understated navigation links leading to post-checkout destinations. The bottom third of the page is occupied by the tall, multi-column dark footer, with brand logo and description on the left and link columns to the right. A floating circular call/support action hovers at the bottom-right, visually detached from the main flow but consistent with the overall tech/gaming aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This component is non-visual and does not occupy any explicit space in the layout. Conceptually, it sits behind the main checkout column, controlling which visible block appears immediately below the 'Checkout' heading. It toggles between three primary states: (1) a simple 'no pending change' info message displayed mid-column with ample dark background around it; (2) the main checkout stack (summary, selectors, billing info, and confirmation form) occupying the central vertical band of the page; and (3) the post-checkout state where the checkout-result panel plus post-checkout navigation links sit in that same central band. In all cases, the vertical alignment with the header and footer grid is preserved, and the state manager ensures only one of these major content stacks is visible at a time.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible controller; does not render UI elements and therefore has no direct spatial footprint, but orchestrates which visible components are mounted in the main vertical flow beneath the page heading.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout summary panel is the first substantive panel in the main column, positioned directly below any active error banner (or, if no banner, roughly 32–40px under the 'Checkout' heading). It spans the full width of the inner content column (about 80–90% of viewport width, capped by the max-width container), left-aligned with the heading. Its background is a slightly lighter dark tone than the page background, giving it a subtle card feel without visible shadows, and its corners are softly rounded in line with the design DNA. The panel has generous internal padding (24px top and bottom, 24–32px left and right), and its content stacks vertically: plan name and key price on the top line, with secondary details (add-ons, current vs new pricing) below. There is at least 24px of vertical space between the bottom of this panel and the next component (effective-date-selector), keeping the central area feeling open.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block in the normal document flow, anchored to the grid; it grows vertically with content but always maintains full-width alignment inside the max-width container.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The effective date selector sits directly below the checkout summary panel, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space to signal close relationship but distinct function. It appears as a single-row or short-stack horizontal band that also spans the full width of the content column, aligning flush with the left edge of the summary panel and heading. The overall height is modest—about the same as a tall form row—keeping it visually light. On the left, a label such as 'Effective date' is aligned with the summary’s text; on the right or just beneath, a segmented control, dropdown, or radio options allow the user to choose when the change takes effect. Internal padding matches other panels (16–24px), and the background is either the same dark card tone as the summary or a slightly lighter variant to subtly differentiate it without breaking the dark theme. A 16–24px gap separates it from the billing-and-promo-summary that follows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width row within the main column, flexing horizontally to accommodate content but not breaking into multiple columns; it expands vertically on smaller viewports as options wrap.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This panel appears below the effective date selector, separated by a comfortable 16–24px gap. It also spans the full width of the content column and uses a card-like dark background with subtle rounding, mirroring the checkout-summary-panel. Inside, billing breakdowns (next charge, proration, taxes) and any applied promo codes are stacked vertically with 8–12px between individual lines of text. Prominent numbers (totals, discounts) appear near the right edge or on a dedicated line, but the layout remains fundamentally single-column and left-aligned to respect the mockup’s simplicity. Internal padding is about 24px on all sides, making this section feel readable despite potentially dense financial information. There is a slightly larger vertical gap (24–32px) below this panel leading into the more action-oriented checkout-confirmation-form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the main column; height grows to fit content but maintains consistent left/right alignment with neighboring panels.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The confirmation form is positioned immediately beneath the billing-and-promo-summary panel, offset by roughly 24–32px of vertical spacing to clearly mark it as the final action zone. It spans the full width of the content column but feels like a focused card: dark background (slightly lighter than the page), subtle corner rounding, and generous padding (24–32px). Inside, form elements are arranged in a single vertical stack: any confirmation text, optional checkboxes (e.g., 'I agree to the terms'), and finally the primary confirm button. The confirm CTA is a bright neon green button located at the bottom of the form, aligned to the right edge of the form content area, echoing the neon CTA in the header and reinforcing the horizontal right-side emphasis. There is ample space below the button (24–32px) before the start of the footer area, so the form feels comfortably separated from the footer content even on shorter viewports.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, main call-to-action block in the normal flow; expands vertically as necessary for validation messages or additional fields but always keeps the CTA anchored inside the card, near its bottom-right.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When present, the error banner appears as a full-width notification strip directly beneath the 'Checkout' heading, pushing all other content (summary, selectors, form) further down. It mirrors the mockup’s cart notification bar: a light, high-contrast background (e.g., pale red-tinted or amber-tinted light band) on top of the dark page, spanning the width of the content column with rounded corners and no shadow. An icon or alert symbol sits on the left, followed by the error text on its right; content is vertically centered within a 48–56px high band. There is a modest 16–24px gap above (between heading and banner) and another 24–32px gap below before the checkout-summary-panel begins, ensuring the banner stands out and is easily scannable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static but conditional; it occupies a fixed position in the vertical stack directly under the page heading when active, and disappears entirely (collapsing the space) when there are no errors.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: In the state where the user has no pending subscription change to confirm, this message replaces the main checkout stack. It appears centered within the main content column, directly under the 'Checkout' heading (or error banner if one is present), leaving a large expanse of dark background above and below to echo the minimal, empty-cart feel from the mockup. The message itself is styled like a softer notification bar: a mid-tone dark or very subtle light strip, spanning about 60–70% of the content column width and centered horizontally within it, or left-aligned but shorter in width to create more side whitespace. Internal padding is generous (24–32px), with concise text and possibly a small info icon on the left. Below this message, there is extra vertical breathing room (48–64px) before the footer begins, reinforcing the sense that there is no action to take here.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with the main form/result stack; when this message is shown, the summary, selectors, billing panel, and confirmation form are hidden, so this element becomes the primary occupant of the central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: After the user completes checkout, the result panel occupies the same central position as the form and its supporting panels previously did. It sits approximately 32–48px beneath the 'Checkout' heading (or any error/success notice if used), spanning the full width of the content column like a large confirmation card. The background is a dark card tone with subtle rounding; internal padding is generous (32px) to accommodate a success or failure icon, a large status heading (e.g., 'Change Confirmed'), and explanatory text stacked vertically. The main status text is center-aligned within the card for a strong focal point, while details wrap beneath it with comfortable line spacing. Below this content, there is a clear separation (24–32px) and then the post-checkout-navigation links appear, making the result panel the visual anchor of the post-checkout state.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static but state-specific; replaces the summary/form stack in the main vertical flow once checkout completes, maintaining alignment to the same grid and keeping a similar overall footprint.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The post-checkout navigation sits immediately below the checkout-result-panel, separated by a modest 16–24px vertical gap. It consists of a small set of navigation links (e.g., 'Go to My Account', 'View Subscription', 'Back to Home') arranged in a simple horizontal row on larger screens, left-aligned to the same column edge as the heading and panels, and wrapping into a vertical list on smaller screens. The links themselves are medium-weight, neon-accented text or pill-style buttons on the dark background, but visually quieter than the primary CTA. The entire navigation block occupies only the lower portion of the central column, leaving substantial dark space below before the footer begins (32–48px), so it feels like a gentle next-step suggestion rather than a heavy block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, sits directly under the result panel when in the post-checkout state; adapts from a horizontal row to a vertical stack responsively but always remains within the main column alignment.

name: Channels
title: Channels
description: Browse all available channels, filter by attributes such as genre, language, and rating, sort the list, and manage favorite channels.

core_features: Load available filter and sort options dynamically using getChannelFilterOptions and render them as interactive controls.
Read optional URL parameters (genre, language, rating, sort) on load and apply them as initial filters and sort order.
Request and display a channels list using getChannelsForListing based on the currently applied filters and sort.
Indicate which channels are already marked as favorites using favorite_channel_ids returned by getChannelsForListing.
Allow users to toggle favorite status for a channel using addChannelToFavorites and removeChannelFromFavorites and update the page state accordingly.
Provide navigation to channel_detail.html when a channel row (or its logo/name) is clicked, passing channelId via URL.
Provide navigation to tv_guide.html when a "View Schedule" or similar action is clicked for a channel, passing channelId via URL.
Support deep-linking and back/forward navigation by keeping filters and sort state synchronized with URL parameters.
user_workflows:
Workflow: Initial page load with optional deep-link filters
1. User opens channels.html (optionally with genre, language, rating, and/or sort URL parameters).
2. Page reads URL parameters and constructs an initial filter/sort state.
3. Page calls getChannelFilterOptions to retrieve available genres, languages, ratings, and sort options.
4. Filter controls are initialized and pre-selected according to the URL parameters where applicable.
5. Page calls getChannelsForListing with filters derived from URL parameters and the selected sort option.
6. The channels list is rendered using the channels and favorite_channel_ids returned by getChannelsForListing.
7. Applied filters and sort are summarized in an "applied filters" area so the user can understand the current view.

Workflow: User adjusts filters and sort
1. User changes one or more filter controls (e.g., selects a different genre or rating).
2. Filter component updates the internal filter state and updates the URL parameters (genre, language, rating) accordingly using navigation APIs (push/replace state or querystring update).
3. Page calls getChannelsForListing again with the updated filters and the current sort option.
4. Channels list is refreshed with the new results and favorite statuses from the response.
5. User optionally changes the sort option; the component updates the sort URL parameter and triggers a new getChannelsForListing call.
6. Applied filters summary updates to reflect the new filters and sort order.

Workflow: Mark a channel as favorite
1. User identifies a channel in the list and activates the favorite toggle control for that channel.
2. The component checks if the channel is currently in favorite_channel_ids (from the latest getChannelsForListing response).
3. If the channel is not a favorite, the component calls addChannelToFavorites with the channelId.
4. On success, the component updates the local favorite state for that channel (e.g., adds the channelId to an in-memory favorite set) and may optionally refresh UI indicators (icon state, count badges) using total_favorites_count from the response.
5. If the channel is already a favorite, the component calls removeChannelFromFavorites with the channelId and updates local state similarly on success.
6. The favorite indicator on that channel row reflects the new state immediately, and any parent component that displays the favorites count is updated.

Workflow: Navigate to channel details
1. User clicks on a channel row, logo, or channel name within the channels list.
2. The channel-row component retrieves the associated channelId from its bound data.
3. The page navigates to channel_detail.html?channelId={channelId}.
4. Navigation uses standard URL-based routing so that the target page can fetch and display the selected channel’s details.

Workflow: Open channel schedule in TV guide
1. User clicks a contextual action such as "View Schedule" associated with a specific channel in the list.
2. The channel-row component uses the bound channelId to construct a navigation URL.
3. The page navigates to tv_guide.html?channelId={channelId}.
4. The Channels page does not fetch schedule data; it only passes channelId to tv_guide.html via URL parameters.

Workflow: Clear filters and reset view
1. User activates a "Clear Filters" control in the applied-filters-summary or filter-controls component.
2. The component resets the internal filter state to an unfiltered baseline (no genre/language/rating in URL) and selects a default sort option (e.g., first sort option from getChannelFilterOptions if no sort is set).
3. URL parameters for genre, language, rating are removed and sort is optionally reset or preserved according to design.
4. Page calls getChannelsForListing with the cleared filters and the chosen sort option.
5. Channels list refreshes to show a broader set of channels; applied filters summary shows an empty or default state.

Workflow: Use browser back/forward navigation with filters
1. User changes filters and sort multiple times, causing URL parameters to be updated each time.
2. User clicks the browser back or forward buttons.
3. On each popstate or equivalent event, the page re-reads the current URL parameters and reconstructs the filter/sort state.
4. Page calls getChannelsForListing with filters derived from the current URL parameters.
5. Filter controls, applied filters summary, and channels list are updated to match the state implied by the URL, ensuring consistent navigation behavior.
interactions: Selecting a genre, language, or rating option in filter controls triggers onFilterChange, which updates URL parameters and calls getChannelsForListing.
Changing the sort option triggers onSortChange, updating the sort URL parameter and calling getChannelsForListing.
Clicking a channel row, logo, or name triggers onChannelClick, navigating to channel_detail.html with the corresponding channelId as a URL parameter.
Clicking the "View Schedule" or equivalent control for a channel triggers onViewScheduleClick, navigating to tv_guide.html with channelId as a URL parameter.
Clicking the favorite toggle on a channel triggers onFavoriteToggle, which calls either addChannelToFavorites or removeChannelFromFavorites based on current favorite status and updates the UI state.
Clicking a "Clear Filters" control triggers onClearFilters, which resets filter-related URL parameters and reloads the channel list via getChannelsForListing.
Page load and URL change events trigger onPageInit or onUrlStateChange, which read current URL parameters, initialize filter and sort control states, and call getChannelsForListing.
page_functionality.state_logic: The Channels page uses URL parameters as the single source of truth for filter and sort state. On initial load and whenever the URL changes, the page parses the genre, language, rating, and sort parameters and maps them into the filter/sort state used by the components. If a parameter is absent, the corresponding state is left undefined or set to a default determined from getChannelFilterOptions (for example, selecting the first available sort option when no sort parameter is provided). After state initialization, the page calls getChannelsForListing with an object that includes filters.genre, filters.language, filters.rating, and the selected sort value. The response includes channels, favorite_channel_ids, and applied_filters. The applied_filters object is used to populate the applied filters summary and to confirm the effective filters currently applied by the backend. When the user changes any filter or sort option, the page updates the URL query parameters to reflect the new selections and then calls getChannelsForListing again with the updated values. No persistent client storage such as localStorage is used; all persistence across navigation and reloads is achieved via URL parameters and data fetched from the assigned interfaces. Favorite status is derived from favorite_channel_ids returned by getChannelsForListing and from the results of addChannelToFavorites/removeChannelFromFavorites calls. Local in-memory state may be used to optimistically update favorite indicators between API calls but is not persisted beyond the current page session.

components.functionality: Coordinates initial page setup: reads URL parameters, fetches filter options using getChannelFilterOptions, sets initial filter/sort state, and triggers the first getChannelsForListing call.

Displays dynamic filter controls for genre, language, and rating, as well as a sort selector. Initializes available options using getChannelFilterOptions and synchronizes user selections with URL parameters and the channels listing.
event_handlers: onFilterChange: When a genre, language, or rating input value changes, update the corresponding URL parameter and trigger getChannelsForListing with the new filters.
onSortChange: When the sort option changes, update the sort URL parameter and trigger getChannelsForListing with the new sort.
onClearFilters: When the user activates the clear filters action, remove genre, language, and rating parameters from the URL, optionally reset sort to a default, and trigger getChannelsForListing.

components.functionality: Summarizes the currently applied filters and sort order as confirmed by the backend via applied_filters and the selected sort option. Provides quick access to clear all filters.
event_handlers: onClearFiltersClick: Invokes the filter-controls.onClearFilters handler to reset filters and refresh the channels list.

components.functionality: Renders the list of channels returned by getChannelsForListing, delegating row-level interactions (favorites, navigation) to child components.
event_handlers: onChannelsDataUpdated: When new channels data is returned, re-render the list and propagate favorite_channel_ids to each channel-row.
onEmptyStateCheck: If channels array is empty, show the empty-state-message component.

components.functionality: Represents a single channel in the list, showing key metadata (such as name, logo, genre indicator, language, rating, and resolution badges) and offering actions for viewing details, viewing schedule, and toggling favorite status.
event_handlers: onFavoriteToggle: Delegates to favorites-toggle for add/remove favorite operations.

components.functionality: Manages favorite status for a single channel. Determines current favorite state from favorite_channel_ids and toggles it via addChannelToFavorites or removeChannelFromFavorites.
event_handlers: onFavoriteToggle: When activated, checks whether Channel.id exists in favorite_channel_ids; if not, calls addChannelToFavorites(channelId). If it does, calls removeChannelFromFavorites(channelId). On success, updates in-memory favorite set and re-renders the control.
onFavoriteUpdateSuccess: Updates any displayed favorites count using total_favorites_count from the API response.
onFavoriteUpdateError: Optionally reverts the optimistic UI state for this channel row and exposes an error state to the user.

components.functionality: Displays a functional message when getChannelsForListing returns no channels for the current filters, and offers a way to clear filters or adjust them.
event_handlers: onShow: Triggered when channels array length is zero; shows a message and possible guidance based on applied_filters.
onClearFiltersClick: Delegates to filter-controls.onClearFilters to reset all filters and refresh the list.

components.functionality: Optionally manages pagination or incremental loading if supported by the filters object passed to getChannelsForListing, while keeping page index in sync with URL parameters (if implemented).
event_handlers: onNextPage: Increments page parameter in URL (if used) and calls getChannelsForListing with updated filters including page information.
onPreviousPage: Decrements page parameter in URL (if used) and calls getChannelsForListing with updated filters.
onPageSizeChange: Updates pageSize parameter in URL (if used) and calls getChannelsForListing.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom structure mirroring the mockup: global header (already defined for the site) at the very top, then within the centered content column a left-aligned 'Channels' page heading, immediately followed by a prominent, full-width filter bar (using the same visual role as the notification strip in the mockup). Directly beneath that sits a slimmer applied-filters summary bar. Below this, the channels list occupies the main vertical portion of the page; if there are no results, the empty state replaces the list in that same region. At the bottom of the content column, pagination/load-more controls appear, followed by the tall, multi-column global footer. A floating call button remains anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport, visually independent from the document flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows a strictly linear, top-to-bottom structure mirroring the mockup: global header (already defined for the site) at the very top, then within the centered content column a left-aligned 'Channels' page heading, immediately followed by a prominent, full-width filter bar (using the same visual role as the notification strip in the mockup). Directly beneath that sits a slimmer applied-filters summary bar. Below this, the channels list occupies the main vertical portion of the page; if there are no results, the empty state replaces the list in that same region. At the bottom of the content column, pagination/load-more controls appear, followed by the tall, multi-column global footer. A floating call button remains anchored to the bottom-right of the viewport, visually independent from the document flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is a shallow strip at the top. Inside the main container, the 'Channels' heading plus filter controls area uses roughly the upper quarter of the central viewport height, with generous vertical spacing (24–32px gaps) to feel airy. The channels list region is the dominant area, taking roughly the middle half of the page height for a typical list, with substantial dark background visible around and between rows. The applied-filters summary bar is compact, about half the height of the main filter bar. Pagination/load-more controls occupy a narrow band (about 48–64px tall) near the bottom of the content column. The footer is tall and content-dense, roughly a third of the page height when in view, spanning full width with its own grid-aligned inner content. Horizontal content is constrained to a centered max-width (e.g., 1120–1280px) with generous left/right padding (~64–80px total from viewport edges).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains medium. The header and filter bar are airy, with clear padding and breathing room around elements. The applied-filters summary is slightly more compact but still readable, using chips or short lines with 8px vertical gaps. Channel rows in the list are moderately dense: each row has comfortable vertical padding (16–24px) and clear separation via spacing or dividers, but they stack closely enough to feel like a data list rather than large marketing cards. The footer is the densest region, with multiple link columns and tighter vertical rhythm (8–12px between lines), but still organized and legible. There are no heavy tables; instead, everything is presented as simple horizontal rows and short lists.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual attention begins at the global header: logo left, navigation center, neon CTA button right. The eye then drops to the 'Channels' heading, which anchors the page context. From there, focus moves immediately to the high-contrast, light filter bar beneath the heading (equivalent in emphasis to the notification strip in the mockup), inviting the user to refine or explore channels. Next, the eye falls to the applied-filters summary bar to understand what’s currently active. From there, attention flows down through the vertical stack of channel rows, scanning left-to-right within each row (logo/name left, meta in the middle, favorites toggle at the far right). At the end of this vertical path, the gaze reaches the pagination/load-more controls, then continues into the dense, multi-column footer. Throughout, the floating call button in the bottom-right corner acts as a persistent secondary attractor, visible as users scroll but not interrupting the main content flow.

layout.overall_description: The Channels page sits on a dark, modern canvas consistent with the rest of the IPTV site: a full-width black background with a neon green accent, minimal ornamentation, and content constrained to a centered, grid-aligned column. At the top, the site’s global header stretches edge-to-edge, with the brand logo on the left, navigation in the center, and a bright neon CTA button on the right. Just below, the main content container begins with a clean, left-aligned 'Channels' heading in large type, positioned with a generous vertical gap from the header. Immediately under this heading, a full-width, light-toned filter bar cuts across the dark page, creating strong contrast similar to the notification strip in the mockup. This bar houses genre, language, rating, and sorting controls, laid out horizontally within the 12-column grid. Beneath it, a slimmer applied-filters summary row runs left-aligned within the same column, showing active filters as chips and offering a quick way to clear them. Further down, the channels list occupies the largest vertical area—a vertical stack of horizontally stretched rows on a slightly lighter dark surface. Each row presents channel info from left to right, culminating in a neon-accented favorites toggle at the far right. If no channels match the current filters, a centered, high-contrast empty state panel replaces the list in this space, echoing the light-strip visual language but as a more compact card. At the bottom of the list area, pagination or a load-more control is centered, subtly framed by extra vertical spacing before the footer begins. The footer then fills the lower third of the page with a dark block containing the brand logo and description on the left and three link columns—Company, Product, Help—on the right, followed by a thinner copyright bar below. Floating above all this, a circular neon call button is anchored to the bottom-right corner of the viewport, slightly inset from the edges, maintaining presence without colliding with the footer or main content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channels-page-init component acts as the structural container for the main content area of the Channels page. It begins immediately below the full-width global header and ends just above the global footer. Horizontally, it is centered in the viewport and constrained to the site’s max-width (for example, around 1120–1280px), aligned to a 12-column grid. There is generous top padding—roughly 32–48px—from the bottom edge of the header to the 'Channels' page heading inside this container, maintaining the airy separation observed in the mockup. Left and right padding inside this container mirror the mockup’s margins, keeping content about 64–80px away from the viewport edges on large screens and scaling down proportionally on smaller viewports. All other components for this page—heading, filter-controls, applied-filters-summary, channels-list, empty-state, and pagination—are vertically stacked inside this container, maintaining a single-column, top-to-bottom flow within the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Serves as a standard, non-fixed, non-floating block container that establishes the centered max-width and grid alignment for all Channels page content; it scrolls with the page and inherits the site-wide dark background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-controls component is positioned directly below the 'Channels' page heading inside the main content container, with approximately 24–32px of vertical space separating it from the heading. Visually, it appears as a full-width horizontal strip spanning the entire 12-column grid of the content container but not touching the viewport edges, mirroring the notification bar from the mockup. Its background is a light, high-contrast color (e.g., light gray or very dark-on-light variant) against the otherwise dark page, making it a focal band. The strip height is moderate—around 56–72px—providing enough room for comfortable controls without feeling bulky. Internally, content is arranged left-to-right: on the far left is a short label such as 'Filters' or an icon, followed by grouped controls for Genre, Language, Rating, and possibly Sort. These controls align to the grid columns, with consistent 16–24px horizontal spacing between them. On the far right of the strip, there may be a compact 'Reset filters' text link or icon, aligned vertically with the controls. Padding inside the bar follows the component pattern from the mockup, around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. The bar’s edges are subtly rounded, consistent with the site’s minimal rounding style.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as a standard block-level band in the vertical flow. It scrolls away with the page (not sticky), but remains visually strong due to its contrasting light background and full-width horizontal presence within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary component sits immediately below the filter-controls bar, with a modest vertical gap of about 8–16px that visually groups it with the filters while still separating the elements. It spans the same max-width as the filter-controls, aligned exactly to the same 12-column grid, but its visual footprint is lower and more subtle. The background remains dark—either the main page background or a slightly lighter dark tone—to keep it visually secondary to the bright filter bar. The content is left-aligned and typically occupies the left half to two-thirds of the content width, leaving the right side mostly empty or with a single 'Clear all' action aligned to the far right. Within the left block, active filters are represented as horizontal pills or compact text labels with 8px vertical spacing between rows if they wrap, maintaining a medium-density feel. The vertical height is relatively small (around 32–48px when a few filters are active), expanding gracefully if many filters are applied. This summary remains visually in-line with the rest of the content and does not form a bold band like the filter-controls above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard, scrollable block that expands or contracts vertically based on the number of applied filters; it sits in the normal flow and maintains alignment with the filter-controls bar above and the channels-list below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channels-list component begins directly under the applied-filters-summary, with a generous vertical gap of about 24–32px to clearly separate the controls area from the content area, echoing the breathing space seen between the notification strip and content in the mockup. It spans the full 12-column width within the centered container, but the rows inside may use internal padding so the channel content feels slightly inset. The list occupies the main vertical body of the page, easily taking up half or more of the viewport height on a typical screen. Inside, channels are displayed as a vertical stack of channel-row items, each spanning nearly the full content width. There is consistent vertical spacing between rows—either 8–12px gaps or subtle dividers—preserving a medium density while avoiding a cramped table appearance. The background of the list area is the main dark page color; individual rows may use a slightly lighter dark shade to differentiate them from the background. If channels are present, this list pushes downward until it approaches the footer, with the pagination-or-load-more component anchored at its bottom. If there are no matching channels, the channels-empty-state component occupies the center of this list area instead of rows, still aligned to the same grid and width constraints.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: A scrollable, vertically expanding block that adapts to the number of channel-row items. It works as the main content region and naturally pushes pagination and the footer down as more items are loaded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each channel-row sits inside the channels-list as a horizontally oriented strip that extends across most of the content width, aligning to the grid columns. The row height is moderate—about 72–88px—reflecting a comfortably dense list view. The background for the row is a slightly lighter dark tone than the main page background, with subtle rounding on the corners (4–8px radius) and no visible shadow, consistent with the flat aesthetic. Within each row, content flows left-to-right: on the far left, a small square or horizontal rectangle area (one or two grid columns) houses the channel logo or thumbnail. Immediately to its right, the channel name is displayed prominently in bright or light text, with supporting metadata (e.g., language, genre, rating) stacked beneath or to the right in a smaller, dimmer text. This central information block might span roughly 6–8 grid columns. On the far right, aligned vertically center, the favorites-toggle component is placed as the primary action, separated from the meta info by generous horizontal spacing to keep the layout clean. Vertical padding of 16–20px and horizontal padding of 16–24px inside the row ensure breathing room around all elements. Rows either have 8–12px of vertical spacing between them or a single, subtle divider line that runs the width of the content area, avoiding visual clutter while clearly separating items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Repeating, scrollable list-item blocks arranged in a single column. They stretch to fit the list width, maintaining consistent height and spacing. On smaller screens, the internal layout can wrap so metadata moves under the channel name while the favorites-toggle remains right-aligned or drops below on its own line.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-toggle is nested inside each channel-row at the far right edge, acting as the main per-item action. It appears as a compact, high-contrast control—such as a neon-accented icon button (a star or heart) or a small pill-shaped button—standing out against the darker row background. The control’s size is roughly 32–40px in height and width, with at least 8–12px of padding around the icon inside the button. It is vertically centered within the row’s height and aligned to the right inner padding edge, typically occupying about one grid column. There is clear separation (around 24–32px) between the favorites-toggle and the nearest text content to its left, ensuring it forms its own distinct action zone without making the row feel crowded. When active, it fills with the neon green accent color; when inactive, it uses an outlined or muted variant that still reads as clickable. The control remains visually subordinate to the global header CTA but is the brightest element within each individual row.
