component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed position within each channel-row, anchored to the top-right or middle-right corner of the row’s content area. It scrolls along with the row as part of the list and does not float independently; on smaller screens it may shift to align right on its own line beneath the primary channel text while preserving right alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channels-empty-state component is displayed in place of channel-row items inside the channels-list when no channels match the current filters. It is positioned centrally within the list region, horizontally aligned to the same 12-column container but visually centered with additional surrounding whitespace. Vertically, there is a generous gap from the applied-filters-summary above—roughly 48–64px—so it feels like a distinct block, similar in emphasis to the notification strip in the mockup but more compact and card-like. The empty state appears as a light-background panel (echoing the high-contrast of the filter bar) with subtle rounded corners and no shadow, occupying roughly 50–70% of the content width on larger screens, centered within the grid. Inside this panel, an icon or simple illustration sits at the top center, followed by a clear, concise headline like 'No channels found' and a short explanatory line in smaller text. Below the text, a primary action (e.g., 'Clear filters' or 'Browse all channels') is centered and uses the neon accent color, mirroring the CTA style from the header but sized appropriately for inline content. Surrounding the panel, the dark page background is clearly visible, reinforcing the sense of an isolated, focused message area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional, occupying the same spatial slot that would otherwise be taken by the list of channel-row items. It remains a standard, scrollable block element; when it is visible, no channel rows are rendered, and pagination controls may be hidden or visually minimized below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The pagination-or-load-more component is placed at the bottom of the channels-list region, directly below the last channel-row or the empty state (if present), separated by a vertical gap of 24–32px. It is horizontally centered within the content container and typically occupies a narrow horizontal band, about 48–64px in total height. If implemented as pagination, page numbers, next/previous arrows, and the current page indicator are arranged in a single horizontal line, with the active page or primary action highlighted in the neon accent color. If implemented as a 'Load more' button, that button is centered and uses the neon accent, sized similarly to a medium primary button with 12–16px vertical and 24–32px horizontal padding. The background remains the dark page color, so the control reads as a discrete set of elements rather than a full-width strip. There is additional vertical space (32–48px) below this component before the start of the footer, creating a clear separation between interactive list controls and the static site footer content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow control anchored beneath the dynamic list. As the user scrolls and more items are loaded or pages are changed, this component naturally moves down the page. It does not stick to the viewport, maintaining the linear, top-to-bottom flow dictated by the mockup.

name: Channel Detail
title: Channel Detail
description: Displays detailed information and a mini schedule for a single channel, and lets users add or remove the channel from their favorites.

core_features: Load channel details and upcoming programs for the channelId in the URL using getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId).
Display channel metadata including name, logo, primary language, genre, rating, and a descriptive summary.
Show a mini schedule list of upcoming_programs for the channel, including program titles and times.
Allow the user to add the channel to favorites via addChannelToFavorites(channelId) when it is not yet a favorite.
Allow the user to remove the channel from favorites via removeChannelFromFavorites(channelId) when it is already a favorite.
Reflect current favorite status (is_favorite) in the UI (e.g., button label or heart icon state).
Update any favorites count indicator in the page header using total_favorites_count returned from addChannelToFavorites/removeChannelFromFavorites.
Provide a "Back to Channels" navigation action that sends the user to channels.html.
Provide a "View Full Schedule" navigation action that sends the user to tv_guide.html?channelId=<channelId>.
Provide a header or global navigation link to favorites.html (e.g., "Favorites" / "My Channels") so the user can move from this channel back to their overall favorites list.
user_workflows:
View a channel from the channel list:
1. User clicks a channel in channels.html, which navigates to channel_detail.html?channelId=<selectedChannelId>.
2. On load, the page reads channelId from the URL and calls getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId).
3. The page shows loading states while waiting for the response.
4. When data is returned, the page displays the channel information (name, language, genre, rating, description) and mini schedule (upcoming_programs).
5. The current favorite status is shown based on the is_favorite flag from the interface response.

Mark or unmark a channel as favorite:
1. After the channel details are visible, the user sees a favorite toggle control that reflects the current is_favorite state.
2. If is_favorite is false, the control appears in the "not favorite" state. The user clicks it, triggering addChannelToFavorites(channelId).
3. The control enters a busy/disabled state while waiting for the response.
4. On success (success = true), the component updates its local state to is_favorite = true and uses total_favorites_count to update any favorites counter in the header/nav.
5. If success = false, the control restores its previous state and may show the returned message as an error.
6. If is_favorite is true initially, clicking the control triggers removeChannelFromFavorites(channelId), and on success the component updates is_favorite = false and updates the favorites count indicator using total_favorites_count.

Inspect mini schedule and open full TV guide view:
1. User scrolls to the mini schedule section populated from upcoming_programs returned by getChannelDetailWithSchedule.
2. The user scans the list of upcoming programs; the page does not modify the schedule data but only displays it.
3. To see the full schedule, the user clicks a "View Full Schedule" control.
4. The page navigates to tv_guide.html?channelId=<currentChannelId>, letting the TV Guide page focus on this channel using the passed URL parameter.

Return to channel list or favorites after selecting favorites:
1. The user may open this page from channels.html, favorites.html, or tv_guide.html, always with channel_detail.html?channelId=<channelId>.
2. After adding or removing favorites on this channel, the user can click "Back to Channels" to navigate to channels.html and continue browsing or selecting other channels.
3. Alternatively, the user can use the header link (e.g., "Favorites" / "My Channels") to navigate directly to favorites.html.
4. Any favorites count indicator in this page’s header uses the latest total_favorites_count from the most recent favorite toggle response until the user navigates away.
interactions: Initial data load: On page initialization or whenever the URL channelId parameter changes, call getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId). Bind the returned channel object to the channel information components, is_favorite to the favorite toggle component, and upcoming_programs to the mini schedule list.

Favorite toggle (add): When the user clicks the favorite control while is_favorite is false, call addChannelToFavorites(channelId). If success is true, set the in-page is_favorite state to true and update any favorites count indicator using total_favorites_count. If success is false, keep is_favorite as false and surface message as an error state.

Favorite toggle (remove): When the user clicks the favorite control while is_favorite is true, call removeChannelFromFavorites(channelId). If success is true, set the in-page is_favorite state to false and update the favorites count indicator using total_favorites_count. If success is false, keep is_favorite as true and surface message as an error state.

View full schedule navigation: When the user clicks "View Full Schedule", read the current channelId from page state (derived from URL) and navigate to tv_guide.html?channelId=<channelId> using standard link or client-side routing.
Back to channels navigation: When the user clicks "Back to Channels", navigate to channels.html without additional query parameters, so the user can continue browsing the channel list.

Navigate to favorites page: When the user clicks the header "Favorites" / "My Channels" link, navigate to favorites.html. If a favorites count badge is displayed, it reflects the most recent total_favorites_count from the last successful addChannelToFavorites or removeChannelFromFavorites call.

Error handling: If getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId) fails or returns no channel, show an error state in the page (e.g., a functional message) and optionally disable the favorite toggle and schedule actions, since they depend on a valid channelId.

Reload on channel change: If the application allows navigation directly to another channel by changing the channelId URL parameter (e.g., via router links), detect this change, clear previous channel state, and re-run getChannelDetailWithSchedule with the new channelId.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver for this page is the channelId URL parameter (e.g., channel_detail.html?channelId=<id>). On load, the page reads channelId from the query string. If channelId is present, it triggers getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId) to populate all content. If channelId is missing or invalid, the page shows an appropriate error state and does not attempt to call addChannelToFavorites or removeChannelFromFavorites. The in-page representation of is_favorite is initially taken from the getChannelDetailWithSchedule response and is then updated only in response to successful favorite toggle API responses. The page does not store state in localStorage; instead, any navigation to other pages (channels.html, tv_guide.html, favorites.html) uses URL-based navigation. The link to tv_guide.html always includes the current channelId as a query parameter so that the TV Guide page can focus on this channel’s schedule. The link to channels.html and favorites.html does not require parameters as per the navigation specification, but the page may keep its internal state (is_favorite, total_favorites_count) only until the user navigates away. If the user returns later to the same channel_detail.html with the same channelId, the page will refresh the state by calling getChannelDetailWithSchedule again, ensuring consistency with server-side favorites and schedule data.

components.functionality: Coordinates the overall Channel Detail page behavior: reads channelId from the URL, invokes getChannelDetailWithSchedule(channelId), manages loading and error states, and passes channel, is_favorite, and upcoming_programs data down to child components.

Displays static properties of the selected channel such as name, logo, primary_language, genre, rating, channel_number, is_hd / is_4k indicators, and description using the channel object provided by the controller.

Shows an Add/Remove Favorites control whose state reflects the is_favorite value from getChannelDetailWithSchedule. When clicked, it calls addChannelToFavorites or removeChannelFromFavorites with the current channelId and updates the UI and any favorites count indicator based on the API response.

Renders the mini schedule for the channel using upcoming_programs from getChannelDetailWithSchedule, listing each program’s title, start_time, end_time, and rating, and includes a control to navigate to the full schedule in tv_guide.html for the same channel.

Provides navigation controls to move away from the Channel Detail page: a "Back to Channels" link to channels.html, and a header link to favorites.html (e.g., labeled "Favorites" / "My Channels"). It can display a favorites count badge that is updated by the favorite-toggle-control component using total_favorites_count from the last favorite API response.

Optionally displays the current total number of favorite channels in the header or navigation area. It listens to the results of addChannelToFavorites and removeChannelFromFavorites calls and updates its displayed count using total_favorites_count.

Shows functional error or empty states when getChannelDetailWithSchedule fails, when channelId is missing, or when no channel data is returned, and disables interactive elements (favorite toggle, schedule link) when appropriate.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar at the very top; below it, a centered single-column content area controlled by the page controller. At the top of this content area, the channel-info-panel sits left-aligned as the main heading block, with the favorite-toggle-control aligned to the right on the same horizontal band. Directly under this band sits a full-width notification-style strip for the channel-error-state when needed, visually echoing the cart notification bar from the mockup. The mini-schedule-list then fills the rest of the main content area below. The favorites-count-indicator lives inside the navigation-bar on the right side. All of this sits within a dark full-page background, with header and potential global footer spanning full width and inner content aligned to a centered max-width grid.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark navigation bar at the very top; below it, a centered single-column content area controlled by the page controller. At the top of this content area, the channel-info-panel sits left-aligned as the main heading block, with the favorite-toggle-control aligned to the right on the same horizontal band. Directly under this band sits a full-width notification-style strip for the channel-error-state when needed, visually echoing the cart notification bar from the mockup. The mini-schedule-list then fills the rest of the main content area below. The favorites-count-indicator lives inside the navigation-bar on the right side. All of this sits within a dark full-page background, with header and potential global footer spanning full width and inner content aligned to a centered max-width grid.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The navigation-bar occupies a shallow band at the top (roughly 64–80px high), spanning the full viewport width with inner content constrained to a wide, centered max-width container and generous horizontal padding (~64–80px from viewport edges). Below it, the channel-detail-page-controller defines a vertical content slice that takes up the central 50–60% of the viewport height on first load, with ample dark space around. Within this, the channel-info-panel plus favorite-toggle-control occupy the upper portion, roughly the top 1/4 of the scrollable content area. The channel-error-state (when visible) is a low, full-width strip that spans the content width but is visually light and compact. The mini-schedule-list takes up the remaining lower portion, analogous to the footer’s vertical weight in the mockup, potentially filling about the bottom third to half of the viewport with rows of schedule items.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium density overall. The navigation-bar is clean with clearly separated logo, nav links, favorites-count-indicator, and CTA. The channel-info-panel is visually airy, acting like a hero heading with ample breathing room above and below, no dense blocks of text. The favorite-toggle-control is a single, prominent control rather than a complex form, so it reads light but bold. The channel-error-state mimics the cart notification bar: a single, clear message on a lighter band with an icon and no clutter. The mini-schedule-list is the most content-dense area, similar to the footer in the mockup, with stacked rows and consistent spacing between items, but with readable line height and clear separation between rows so it never feels cramped.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the navigation-bar: logo on the left and neon CTA on the right set up a left-right scan, with the favorites-count-indicator near the CTA as a small status touchpoint. From there, attention drops down into the main content column to the channel-info-panel heading on the left, immediately followed by the favorite-toggle-control on the top-right of this band, reinforcing a primary action at the end of the initial horizontal scan. Below that, if present, the light-background channel-error-state strip acts as a high-contrast checkpoint before the main content, just like the cart notification bar. The gaze then continues downward into the mini-schedule-list, scanning vertically through upcoming programs. A global floating support/call button (outside these components but part of the pattern) would lightly attract attention at the bottom-right edge of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s secondary attractor at the termination of the vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a solid black, full-bleed background that gives the entire experience a streaming/gaming feel. A slim, full-width dark navigation bar hugs the very top, with its inner content aligned to a centered max-width grid. On the left, the IPTV brand logo anchors the layout; in the middle, a compact horizontal navigation; on the right, a favorites count indicator and a bright neon-green CTA button create a strong focal area. Beneath this, the main channel detail content lives inside a centered column that respects the same 12-column grid, starting with a generous dark gap after the header. At the top of this column, a bold channel information panel stretches most of the width, with the channel name and details left-aligned and a neon-accented favorite toggle aligned to the right in the same row, visually echoing the CTA energy from the header. Just below, a notification-style strip appears on a light background when there’s a channel error, spanning the full content width and standing out crisply against the dark page. The rest of the space is occupied by a compact, vertically stacked mini schedule list—rows of upcoming programs that read like neatly spaced bands, creating a denser but still breathable block similar in visual weight to the multi-column footer in the mockup. The overall composition is linear and calm: top nav, focused channel summary band, optional notification strip, then schedule content, all framed by comfortable dark margins on the sides and top/bottom, with a subtle sense that a global floating action button anchors the bottom-right corner beyond this page’s main flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channel-detail-page-controller defines the main content column between the global navigation at the top and the global footer area below. It starts approximately 48px–64px beneath the bottom edge of the navigation-bar, leaving a clear dark gap that visually separates the header from the content. Horizontally, it sits centered in the viewport and is constrained to a max-width that aligns with a 12-column layout (e.g., ~1120–1200px), with roughly 64–80px of dark margin between the content and the viewport edges on large screens. Inside this controller, content is stacked in a single vertical flow: first the channel-info-panel and favorite-toggle-control sharing the top horizontal band, then (when present) the channel-error-state as a full-width strip, followed by the mini-schedule-list occupying the lower portion. Vertical spacing follows an 8px-based scale, with about 32px separating the info/toggle band from the error strip, and another 24–32px between the error strip and the mini-schedule-list. On smaller screens, the controller maintains the same centered column concept but allows components to stack (e.g., favorite-toggle-control moves below the channel-info-panel) while preserving similar vertical gaps.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as a standard flow container that sets the max-width and vertical stacking order for all channel detail components. It scrolls with the page and does not fix or pin anything by itself; instead, it ensures consistent alignment with the global grid system.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channel-info-panel occupies the top portion of the content area within the page controller, functioning like a hero heading for the channel. It is positioned directly at the top of the controller’s column, with about 48px of dark space separating it from the navigation-bar above. The panel spans nearly the full width of the inner grid (e.g., 10–12 of 12 columns), but its content is left-aligned, giving it a strong visual anchor on the left side of the column. The channel name appears in a large, bold type near the top-left of this panel, similar in visual impact to the 'Cart' heading from the mockup, with secondary details (e.g., genre, category, quality, language) stacked beneath it with 8–12px of vertical spacing between lines. Horizontally, the channel-info-panel shares its top row with the favorite-toggle-control: the channel title and details occupy the left two-thirds of the width, while the toggle aligns to the right edge, leaving a clear gap between them. The bottom edge of the panel is separated from the next component (error strip or schedule) by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, keeping the panel feeling airy and singular. On smaller viewports, the panel retains full-width but allows its textual content to wrap; the favorite-toggle-control then drops below the text while still aligning to the right edge of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard in-flow block element. It remains at the top of the main content area and scrolls normally with the page, serving as the stable anchor for the rest of the channel details.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorite-toggle-control is positioned within the same horizontal band as the top of the channel-info-panel, aligned to the far right edge of the main content column. Visually, it sits at the same vertical level as the channel name’s baseline or slightly lower, giving the impression that it is directly associated with the channel heading. It occupies a relatively small horizontal footprint—approximately 2–3 grid columns wide or about 15–20% of the content width—leaving sufficient empty space between it and the channel text on the left. The control is styled as a prominent neon-accented element (e.g., a pill toggle or a button with an icon and label like 'Add to Favorites'), echoing the bright CTA in the navigation-bar. There is a comfortable 16–24px padding inside the control and about 8–16px of space above and below it within the band. Below this shared band, a vertical gap of around 24–32px separates the toggle from the next component, ensuring it reads as a top-level action rather than part of a dense block. On narrower screens, when the layout collapses, the favorite-toggle-control drops below the channel-info-panel text but remains right-aligned within the column with a small top margin (16–24px), still clearly associated with the channel heading.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the channel-info-panel at large widths and part of the normal document flow. It does not float or fix to the viewport, but its right alignment within the grid keeps it visually tied to the content’s top edge as users scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The mini-schedule-list sits below the channel-info-panel (and below the channel-error-state when that strip is visible), occupying the full width of the inner content column. There is a clear vertical separation from the content above—approximately 32px from the info/toggle band or 24px from the bottom of the error strip—maintaining the airy layout seen in the mockup. At the top of this section, a smaller heading such as 'Upcoming Schedule' is left-aligned, aligned with the left edge of the channel-info-panel, and followed by 16–24px of space before the first list item. The list itself presents schedule items as horizontally stretching rows across the content width, each row roughly 48–56px tall with 12–16px of vertical padding. Within each row, time is aligned toward the left, the program title occupies the central area, and optional tags (like 'Live' or duration) align to the right, all following the same 12-column grid. Thin dividers or subtle contrasting row backgrounds separate each item, giving a rhythm similar to stacked footer links but more horizontally structured. The list generally fills the lower half of the main content area, providing visual weight in the bottom portion of the page akin to the multi-column footer from the mockup. On scroll, the list simply continues downward; no sticky headers are needed, and the vertical spacing remains consistent with the site’s 8px-based scale.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrollable content within the main flow. It extends vertically as needed based on the number of schedule items, pushing the bottom of the page down while preserving the max-width and grid alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-bar forms a full-width horizontal band pinned to the very top of the page, sitting flush against the top edge of the viewport. It has a dark background that blends with the site’s overall black theme but is slightly differentiated (e.g., a subtle tint or border) to read as a distinct header. Inside, content is centered within the same max-width grid as the rest of the page, with approximately 64–80px of padding from the left and right viewport edges on large screens. The leftmost portion contains the site/brand logo, aligned to the left grid edge. The central portion holds the main navigation links, arranged inline and horizontally, with comfortable spacing (16–24px) between items. On the right side, near the far edge of the max-width container, the favorites-count-indicator sits as a compact pill or icon+number, and to its immediate right is the primary neon-green CTA button (e.g., 'Subscribe' or 'Start Watching'), which is the brightest element in the bar. Vertical height of the bar is relatively shallow, around 64–72px, echoing the mockup’s slim header. The bottom of the navigation-bar directly touches the dark background of the content area, with a small visual separation created by the content’s top margin rather than by heavy borders.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Stays anchored at the top of the page. It can be implemented as sticky so that it remains visible as users scroll, mirroring the persistent, global nature of the header in the mockup. Its internal elements are fixed within the bar and align to the global 12-column grid.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites-count-indicator is a small, unobtrusive status element located inside the navigation-bar on the right side. It sits immediately to the left of the bright CTA button, aligned vertically with the center of the bar. Horizontally, it occupies a narrow footprint—perhaps a compact pill or icon plus number—spanning about 1 grid column or roughly 5–8% of the header’s inner width. There is a small horizontal gap (8–16px) between the indicator and the CTA button to keep them visually separated. The indicator uses a lighter or neon-accented border or text to be readable against the dark header background, but it is intentionally less visually intense than the CTA. It remains right-aligned with the header’s inner grid, in line with the overall tech/streaming aesthetic. On smaller screens, as navigation condenses (e.g., into a menu icon), the favorites-count-indicator can remain visible to the right of the logo or next to the CTA, keeping its right-aligned bias where possible.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed within the navigation-bar’s layout. It moves only as the header moves (e.g., remains visible if the header is sticky) and does not alter the flow of the main content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The channel-error-state is rendered as a full-width notification strip inside the main content column, visually modeled after the light notification bar from the cart mockup. It sits directly beneath the channel-info-panel and favorite-toggle-control band, separated by approximately 24–32px of vertical space, and spans the entire width of the page controller’s max-width area from left to right. The strip has a lighter, high-contrast background (e.g., light gray or desaturated tone) that clearly stands out against the black page, with subtle rounding on its corners. Inside, content is arranged horizontally: on the far left, an error or warning icon is placed with a small margin (8–12px) from the left padding; to its right, the main error message text runs in a single line or wraps into two lines, occupying the majority of the bar. Vertical padding inside the strip is around 12–16px, yielding a total height of roughly 48–64px depending on text. The right side of the bar can optionally hold a small inline action (e.g., 'Retry' or 'Refresh') if needed, but the primary emphasis remains on the message, not on controls. Below this bar, there is another 24–32px gap before any subsequent component—the mini-schedule-list when partial data is available, or nothing when the error is blocking—so the strip remains visually isolated and easy to scan. When the error-state is not active, this area collapses entirely, letting the mini-schedule-list move up directly under the info/toggle band.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears inline in the main document flow, pushing other content up or down as it becomes visible or hidden. It is not sticky and does not overlay other elements; instead, it temporarily occupies the notification position analogous to the cart page’s full-width notification bar.

name: Favorites
title: Favorites
description: Page for viewing and managing the user’s favorited channels, including quick access to watch or view each channel in the TV guide.

core_features: Load and display the user’s favorite channels in a single ordered list using getFavoriteChannelsList.
Allow users to reorder their favorite channels and persist the new order using reorderFavoriteChannels.
Allow users to remove channels from their favorites list using removeChannelFromFavorites.
Provide direct navigation from each favorite channel to channel_detail.html with the appropriate channelId parameter.
Provide direct navigation from each favorite channel to tv_guide.html with the appropriate channelId parameter.
Offer navigation to channels.html to discover and add additional channels to favorites when desired or when the list is empty.
user_workflows: View favorites: User opens favorites.html (optionally with ?from=channels or ?from=dashboard) -> page calls getFavoriteChannelsList -> favorites list is rendered in the order returned by sort_order -> user can scroll and click any channel to access more details or the TV guide.

Open channel details from favorites: User clicks on a favorite channel’s name or logo -> browser navigates to channel_detail.html?channelId={channel_id} (preserving other unrelated query parameters if needed).
Open channel in TV guide: User clicks the "View in Guide" action or EPG icon for a favorite channel -> browser navigates to tv_guide.html?channelId={channel_id}.

Reorder favorites: User clicks a "Reorder" control -> page switches into reorder mode (reflected by updating the URL to include mode=reorder) -> user drags and drops favorite items to a new order -> user clicks "Save order" -> page constructs channelOrder based on the new sequence of channel identifiers -> page calls reorderFavoriteChannels with channelOrder -> upon success, page updates internal list order and removes mode=reorder from the URL.

Cancel reordering: User clicks "Reorder" to enter reorder mode -> user changes positions of channels -> user clicks "Cancel" instead of "Save order" -> reorder changes are discarded and the list reverts to the last saved order without calling reorderFavoriteChannels, and mode is removed from the URL.

Remove a channel from favorites: User clicks a "Remove" action on a specific favorite channel -> a confirmation interaction is shown -> on confirmation, page calls removeChannelFromFavorites with that channel’s channelId -> on success, the channel is removed from the in-memory favorites list and the total_favorites_count returned can be used to update any count display on this page -> if the list becomes empty, the empty state is displayed with a "Find more channels" link.

Navigate back to origin: User opens favorites.html?from=dashboard (or ?from=channels or ?from=channel_detail) -> breadcrumbs component displays a context-aware link (e.g., "Back to Dashboard" or similar label determined by the from value) -> user clicks the breadcrumb link -> browser navigates back to the inferred origin page (e.g., dashboard.html, channels.html, or channel_detail.html) without changing favorites.
interactions: Click on channel logo or name in the favorites list to navigate to channel_detail.html with ?channelId set to the clicked channel’s channel_id.
Click on "View in Guide" or an equivalent EPG icon to navigate to tv_guide.html with ?channelId set to the clicked channel’s channel_id.
Click on the "Reorder" toggle to enter or exit reorder mode; in reorder mode, drag-and-drop interactions on list items update the local channel sequence used to build the channelOrder payload.

Click on a "Save order" button to submit the new favorites order via reorderFavoriteChannels; handle the response by either confirming success (keeping the new order) or showing an error and restoring the previous order.
Click on a "Cancel" button while in reorder mode to exit reorder mode without persisting changes and to restore the original order in the UI.
Click on a "Remove" or delete icon for a favorite channel to initiate a confirmation; if confirmed, call removeChannelFromFavorites and, on success, remove the channel from the displayed list.
Click on the "Find more channels" link at the bottom of the page or within the empty state to navigate to channels.html.

Load event for the page triggers getFavoriteChannelsList once, populating the favorites list; any subsequent reordering or removals update this list in memory based on API responses without reloading the page.
page_functionality.state_logic: The favorites.html page reads and manages its state using URL parameters and in-memory data. The supported URL parameters are: (1) from: an optional string indicating the origin page (e.g., 'channels', 'dashboard', or 'channel_detail'). This value is used exclusively for UI breadcrumbs and contextual 'Back to' navigation. It does not affect which favorites are loaded or any API calls. (2) mode: an optional string managed by this page to reflect whether the user is in normal view mode or in reorder mode. When mode is set to 'reorder' in the query string (favorites.html?mode=reorder), the page renders drag-and-drop handles and shows 'Save order' and 'Cancel' controls. When mode is absent or not equal to 'reorder', the page is in normal browsing mode, and the list is not draggable. Toggling reorder mode updates the browser URL query string by adding or removing mode=reorder without reloading the page. The getFavoriteChannelsList interface is always called once on initial load to fetch the favorites; its response is bound to the favorites list in memory, ordered by the sort_order field. When reorderFavoriteChannels succeeds, the in-memory order is updated and considered the new canonical order; the mode parameter is cleared. When removeChannelFromFavorites succeeds, the corresponding item is removed from the in-memory list; if total_favorites_count becomes zero, the page automatically switches to displaying the empty state. No persistent state for this page is stored outside of the backend via the provided interfaces; local UI state (such as the current drag order before saving) is transient and not stored in localStorage or other persistent client storage.

components.functionality: Displays the current location ('Favorites') and, when the from URL parameter is present, shows a contextual back link to the originating page (such as channels.html, dashboard.html, or channel_detail.html). This component is purely navigational and does not invoke any of the favorites interfaces.

On initial page load, calls getFavoriteChannelsList once, receives the favorites array, and stores it in the page’s in-memory state. It normalizes and sorts the data by sort_order before passing it to child components such as the favorites list and toolbar.

Provides global controls for the Favorites page, including a 'Reorder' toggle button that switches between normal and reorder modes, and optionally displays the total number of favorites. When entering reorder mode, it updates the page URL to include mode=reorder; when exiting reorder mode, it removes that parameter. It also listens for changes in the favorites list (e.g., length changes after removals) to update any count display.

Renders the ordered list of favorite channels, one row per favorite. Each row displays key channel attributes from getFavoriteChannelsList (name, logo, genre, primary_language, rating, is_hd, is_4k, channel_number) and offers actions: (1) click on the channel logo or name to go to channel_detail.html with the channelId of that channel; (2) click on a 'View in Guide' action to go to tv_guide.html with channelId set; (3) click on a 'Remove' action to initiate removal via removeChannelFromFavorites. When the page is in reorder mode (mode=reorder), this component enables drag-and-drop or equivalent position controls. It maintains a temporary ordered list of channel identifiers reflecting the user’s adjustments.

Encapsulates the per-row actions for a single favorite channel. It receives a single favorite item and exposes three actions: (1) navigate to channel_detail.html when the channel name or logo is clicked; (2) navigate to tv_guide.html when 'View in Guide' or an EPG icon is clicked; (3) open a confirmation and, on confirmation, call removeChannelFromFavorites with the channelId. On successful removal, it notifies the parent list to remove the row and update any counts.

Manages the reorder workflow for the favorites list when mode=reorder. It receives the current temporary order of favorite channel identifiers from the favorites-list component. When the user clicks 'Save order', it constructs the channelOrder parameter from this sequence and calls reorderFavoriteChannels. On a successful response (success=true), it commits the new order to the page’s canonical favorites state and updates the URL to exit reorder mode. If the call fails, it exposes the message to the error-display component and resets the list to the last known good order. A 'Cancel' control lets users exit reorder mode without making an API call.

Displays when there are no favorites in the current list. It listens to the favorites data from getFavoriteChannelsList and updates also when removeChannelFromFavorites indicates that total_favorites_count is zero. The component shows a concise explanatory message and provides a 'Find more channels' action that navigates to channels.html so users can add new favorites.

Shows a persistent 'Find more channels' link at the bottom of the Favorites page whenever there is at least one favorite. The link navigates to channels.html to help users discover additional channels to favorite.

Displays error messages related to favorites operations, such as failures from reorderFavoriteChannels or removeChannelFromFavorites. It listens to the message and success flags returned by these interfaces and shows non-intrusive, dismissible errors when success is false.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. Above everything is the full-width dark global header (logo left, nav center, neon CTA right). The 'Favorites' page content sits in a centered max-width container beneath the header. Inside this container, the sequence is: small breadcrumbs at the very top, then the large left-aligned 'Favorites' heading (part of the page shell), then an optional error banner, then the favorites toolbar, then either the favorites list (with per-row actions and reorder controls) or an empty-state panel, followed by a subtle 'find more channels' footer link. Below this main container, a tall dark multi-column site footer spans full width, with a thin copyright bar at the very bottom. A floating call button sits anchored at the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of this flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page follows the same linear, top-to-bottom flow as the mockup. Above everything is the full-width dark global header (logo left, nav center, neon CTA right). The 'Favorites' page content sits in a centered max-width container beneath the header. Inside this container, the sequence is: small breadcrumbs at the very top, then the large left-aligned 'Favorites' heading (part of the page shell), then an optional error banner, then the favorites toolbar, then either the favorites list (with per-row actions and reorder controls) or an empty-state panel, followed by a subtle 'find more channels' footer link. Below this main container, a tall dark multi-column site footer spans full width, with a thin copyright bar at the very bottom. A floating call button sits anchored at the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of this flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The global header remains a shallow band at the top. The main favorites content sits in a relatively tall, airy middle section, but can become more filled if the list is long. Horizontal content is constrained to a wide, centered column with roughly 64–80px margins from the viewport edges, aligned to a 12-column grid. Vertical spacing follows the mockup: ~32–48px from header to breadcrumbs; 8–16px between breadcrumbs and heading; ~16–24px between heading, error banner, and toolbar; ~24px between toolbar and the first list row or empty state; and ~32–40px between the end of the list/empty state and the 'find more channels' footer link. The footer again occupies a significant vertical portion near the bottom, roughly the last third when content is short.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density is medium. The breadcrumbs and toolbar feel airy with generous horizontal breathing room. The list itself is moderate density: full-width rows with clear vertical separation and comfortable padding. The empty state is sparse and visually light, echoing the single notification bar feel from the mockup. The error banner and any reorder strip are denser strips but still use readable line spacing. The footer remains the most content-dense area, with multiple link columns but sufficient spacing.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual path mirrors the mockup: the eye catches the bright neon CTA in the header and the logo, moves across navigation, then drops to the 'Favorites' heading. From there, attention moves directly to any high-contrast strip (error banner or empty state) or the toolbar. Then the gaze flows downward through the favorites list rows, guided by left-aligned channel information and right-aligned neon inline actions. At the bottom of the main content, the subtle ‘find more channels’ link acts as a soft call to continue exploring, before the eye finishes on the footer’s logo and columns. The floating call button in the bottom-right acts as an optional secondary attractor once the user has scanned the main content.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep black background, framed by a full-width dark header at the top and a tall dark footer at the bottom. Inside a centered, max-width content column, the 'Favorites' page opens with small, subdued breadcrumbs sitting comfortably below the header, followed by a bold, left-aligned 'Favorites' title. Below this, a pale, high-contrast strip may appear when there’s an error, mirroring the light notification bar from the mockup. Next comes a sleek toolbar—a single horizontal band with filters and sort controls spaced across the width, its primary actions picked out in neon green. Under the toolbar, the main favorites area either shows a vertical list of channels or a minimalist empty-state panel. The list rows stretch across the content width, each row a dark band with channel details on the left and crisp, neon-accented inline actions on the right; a slim reorder strip sits just above the rows when reordering is enabled. When there are no favorites, a light-background empty-state card floats in the middle of this dark field instead, with a brief message and a prominent neon CTA. Beneath the list or empty state, a low-emphasis, centered ‘find more channels’ link gently pulls users toward discovery. Finally, the page flows into a substantial footer with the brand block on the left and three link columns on the right, capped by a thin copyright bar. Throughout, spacing is generous and linear, with minimal ornamentation, and a floating call button hovers at the bottom-right corner of the viewport, independent of scrolling content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The breadcrumbs navigation appears at the top of the main content column, directly beneath the global header and aligned with the left edge of the 12-column grid. It occupies the full width of the content container but visually reads as a small, single-line element. There is a generous vertical gap of about 32–40px from the bottom of the header to the breadcrumbs, giving them breathing room. Below the breadcrumbs, a smaller gap of about 8–16px leads into the large 'Favorites' page heading. The breadcrumbs text is small and subdued, sitting quietly against the dark background, ensuring it doesn’t compete with the main heading but still anchors the user’s location in the site structure.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow, always appearing at the top of the favorites content area just below the header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The data loader occupies the same horizontal footprint as the favorites list within the centered content column. When active, it appears directly beneath the favorites toolbar (and any reorder controls) with a vertical gap of about 24px, aligning with the left and right grid edges. Visually, it can present as either a centered neon-accented spinner or a stack of 2–4 horizontal skeleton bars that mimic the width and approximate height of list rows. These skeleton bars run nearly the full content width with subtle spacing (8–16px) between them, reinforcing the expectation of a list. The surrounding dark background remains visible above and below, preserving the airy feel from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Overlays or replaces the list region within the normal flow: it pushes the footer downward but temporarily hides the actual list or empty state. It scrolls with the page and is not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites toolbar is a full-width horizontal band inside the main content container, positioned below the 'Favorites' heading and any error banner. There is roughly 16–24px of vertical space above and below it, separating it from the heading (or error strip) above and the list/empty region below. It spans nearly the entire content width, aligning to the left and right grid margins, with internal horizontal padding of about 16–24px. Controls are arranged in a single row: primary controls (like 'Watch All' or a key filter) are left-aligned, secondary filters or search sit toward the center, and a sort or 'Reorder' toggle anchors the right edge. The toolbar’s background remains dark, slightly differentiated from the page background via a subtle tint or hairline bottom border, and its primary buttons use the neon green accent to echo the header CTA.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow. It scrolls away with the content and does not stick to the top, maintaining the simple linear flow from the mockup.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The favorites list fills the main central portion of the page beneath the toolbar (and any reorder controls), beginning about 24px below and extending downward as a vertical column of rows. It spans the full width of the content container, with each row aligned to the 12-column grid. Each row is a dark tile slightly lighter than the page background, with a comfortable row height around 64–80px and horizontal padding of 16–24px. Rows are separated by subtle vertical gaps of about 8–12px, preserving medium density. Inside each row, channel identity (logo, name, category) is left-aligned and vertically centered. To the right edge of each row, a horizontal cluster of inline actions is aligned, leaving a small margin to the right grid edge. The list continues down the page, pushing the ‘find more channels’ footer link and main footer further down; there is no internal scrolling separate from the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, occupying the central vertical column of the page and growing/shrinking with the number of favorites. It scrolls with the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline actions for each favorite channel sit at the far right of each list row, forming a compact horizontal cluster. They are vertically centered within the row’s height and aligned with the right boundary of the grid, leaving a small inner margin (16–24px) to the row’s edge. The actions are arranged from left to right by importance—for example, 'Watch Now' as the leftmost and most prominent, styled with a neon green pill-shaped button, followed by a lower-emphasis 'Guide' button and an icon-only 'Remove' control farther to the right. Spacing between action items follows the 8–16px rhythm, ensuring they feel deliberate but not cramped. On wider screens, they sit on a single line; on narrower viewports, they may wrap to a second line beneath, still right-aligned and attached visually to their row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Embedded within each list row as part of the static layout. They move with their row as the list scrolls and do not float independently.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reorder controls appear as a slim horizontal strip positioned between the favorites toolbar and the list, only visible when reordering is enabled. It spans the full width of the content container with a slight vertical gap of roughly 8–16px above the first list row. The strip uses a slightly lighter or tinted dark background than the rest of the page, subtly echoing the notification bar pattern from the mockup but without being as bright as the error strip. On the left side, brief instructional text such as 'Drag channels to reorder your favorites' is left-aligned; on the right, a small neon-accented 'Done' or 'Save order' button anchors the strip. Within each list row, drag handles (if used) align toward the far left, keeping a clean left-to-right ordering: handle, channel info, then inline actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in the normal flow between the toolbar and list, but may become sticky within the favorites content area when the user scrolls past its initial position—remaining pinned just beneath the header until reordering is complete. When not in reorder mode, it is absent and no vertical space is reserved for it.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When the user has no favorited channels, the empty state replaces the list area entirely. It is a centered, card-like panel sitting about 24px below the toolbar, occupying roughly 60–70% of the content width on desktop and aligning to the center of the grid. The panel uses a light background similar to the notification strip in the mockup, standing out sharply against the dark page, with rounded corners and internal padding of about 32–48px. At the top of the panel, a simple icon or illustration sits centered, followed by a bold, center-aligned headline like 'No favorites yet', with a short explanatory line beneath using relaxed line spacing. At the bottom, a primary neon green CTA button (‘Browse channels’ or similar) is centered within the panel. Generous dark space surrounds the panel on all sides, maintaining the airy, minimal feel of the empty cart-like state from the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element occupying the same vertical region that the list would. It pushes subsequent elements, including the 'find more channels' footer link and the global footer, downwards and scrolls naturally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The 'find more channels' footer link acts as a subtle intra-page footer for the favorites section. It appears beneath the list or empty-state panel, with a vertical gap of about 32–40px above it and roughly 48px below it before the main site footer begins. The element is horizontally centered within the content container and relatively compact—typically a single line of text such as 'Looking for more channels?' followed by a neon-accented link or small button. The typography is smaller and lower contrast than primary CTAs, so it reads as a soft nudge rather than a dominant call to action. The surrounding dark background and ample vertical spacing make it feel like a natural endpoint for the favorites section, analogous to the quiet space above the footer in the mockup.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and placed at the bottom of the favorites content area, just above the global footer. It scrolls with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error display is a full-width notification strip inside the main content container, positioned between the 'Favorites' heading and the favorites toolbar. It sits about 8–16px beneath the heading and 16–24px above the toolbar, following the mockup’s pattern of a light notification bar following the main title. The strip uses a light background that strongly contrasts with the dark page, making it immediately noticeable. On the left, an error icon or status indicator is aligned near the edge; to its right, error text spans most of the width, and a small neon-accented 'Retry' or 'Refresh' button may appear aligned to the far right. Internal padding is around 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally. The strip feels like a sibling of the mockup’s cart notification bar: flat, slightly rounded corners, no visible shadows, and a clean, single-line or two-line text layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the flow: when present, it pushes the toolbar and main content downward; when dismissed or absent, the toolbar moves up to sit closer to the heading. It scrolls with the page and does not stay fixed.

name: TV Guide
title: TV Guide
description: Interactive electronic program guide showing live channel schedules by date and time, with filters, program details, and recording controls.

core_features: Load and display a time/channel grid for a given date and optional genre and time window using getTvGuideGrid(date, genre, startTime, endTime).
Allow users to change the visible date, time range, and genre, updating URL parameters and reloading the guide grid.
Provide quick navigation to a specific channel row when a channelId URL parameter is present or chosen in the UI.
Enable users to select a program block from the grid to open a program detail panel populated via getProgramDetails(programId).
Show key program and channel metadata (title, description, timing, genre, rating, channel name) in the detail panel using Program and Channel data.
Offer recording controls in the detail panel to schedule a recording for the selected program via scheduleRecording(programId, keepDays).
Provide a simple keep-days setting, including a 'Keep for 7 days' option, and reflect the recording status using the Recording returned from scheduleRecording.
Allow users to navigate from a channel logo or name in the grid to channel_detail.html with the appropriate channelId URL parameter.
user_workflows:
Browse the TV guide for a specific date:
1) User navigates to tv_guide.html (optionally with a date URL parameter).
2) The page reads date, genre, startTime, and endTime from the URL.
3) The page calls getTvGuideGrid(date, genre, startTime, endTime).
4) The tv-guide-grid component renders the returned time_slots and channel/program rows.
5) User scrolls vertically through channels and horizontally through the time axis as needed.

Filter the guide by genre (e.g., sports) and time window:
1) User opens tv_guide.html, the initial grid is loaded via getTvGuideGrid with current URL params.
2) In the guide-filter-bar, the user selects a genre from the genre control.
3) The url-state-manager updates the genre query parameter, preserving date and time window.
4) The page calls getTvGuideGrid(date, updatedGenre, startTime, endTime) and re-renders the grid.
5) Optionally, the user adjusts the visible startTime and endTime; URL parameters are updated and getTvGuideGrid is called again with the new time window.

Deep-link into a specific channel and date from another page:
1) User clicks a link on channels.html or favorites.html that points to tv_guide.html with date and channelId URL parameters.
2) On load, url-state-manager reads date and channelId from the URL.
3) The page calls getTvGuideGrid(date, genre, startTime, endTime).
4) After the grid is rendered, tv-guide-grid scrolls or focuses to the row corresponding to channelId.
5) User can then browse that channel’s schedule in the context of the full guide.

View program details from the grid:
1) User finds a program block of interest in tv-guide-grid.
2) User clicks the program block; tv-guide-grid emits a programSelected event with the programId.
3) program-detail-panel receives programId and calls getProgramDetails(programId).
4) The detail panel opens, showing detailed Program and Channel information from the response.
5) User can close the panel to return to full grid view.

Schedule a recording with a 'Keep for 7 days' setting:
1) User opens program-detail-panel for a future program from tv-guide-grid.
2) In recording-controls, the programId is already bound from the opened program.
3) User selects a keep-days option (for example, a preset labeled 'Keep for 7 days', which sets keepDays to 7).
4) User clicks the schedule recording button; recording-controls calls scheduleRecording(programId, keepDays).
5) On a successful response (success = true), recording-controls updates its local state with the returned Recording and displays confirmation and current status.
6) tv-guide-grid is optionally notified to visually indicate that the program is scheduled for recording.

Navigate from the guide to a channel details page:
1) In tv-guide-grid, each channel row header shows the channel name and optionally a logo.
2) User clicks the channel name/logo.
3) tv-guide-grid triggers navigation to channel_detail.html with the clicked channel’s id as channelId in the query string.
4) The user is taken to the channel_detail.html page to view more information about that channel.
interactions: On page load, url-state-manager reads date, genre, startTime, endTime, and channelId from the current URL query string and triggers an initial getTvGuideGrid call.
Changing the date in the guide-filter-bar updates the date URL parameter and triggers getTvGuideGrid with the new date.
Changing the genre filter in the guide-filter-bar updates the genre URL parameter (removing it if the user clears the filter) and triggers getTvGuideGrid.
Adjusting the time window (startTime and/or endTime) in the guide-filter-bar updates those URL parameters and triggers getTvGuideGrid with the new window.
Clicking a program block in tv-guide-grid fires an onProgramClick handler that passes programId to program-detail-panel, which then calls getProgramDetails(programId) and opens the panel.
Clicking the close icon or a backdrop in program-detail-panel triggers onClose to hide the panel without modifying URL parameters.
Selecting a keep-days option in recording-controls triggers onKeepDaysChange, updating an internal keepDays value bound to the scheduleRecording call.

Clicking the schedule recording button in recording-controls triggers onScheduleRecording, which calls scheduleRecording(programId, keepDays) and updates UI state based on success, message, and recording.
Clicking the channel name or logo in tv-guide-grid triggers onChannelClick, navigating to channel_detail.html?channelId={channel.id}.

Horizontal scrolling or time navigation controls in tv-guide-grid can emit onTimeWindowChange events; when used as discrete navigation (e.g., jumping to the next time block), url-state-manager updates startTime/endTime parameters and triggers getTvGuideGrid.
When a channelId parameter is present in the URL, tv-guide-grid listens for an onGridRendered event to then scroll or focus to the specified channel row.

If getTvGuideGrid or getProgramDetails returns an error state through the implementation, components display non-hardcoded, dynamically provided error messages where available (without introducing additional interfaces).
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is fully derived from and synchronized with URL query parameters and API responses. On initial load and on every relevant user interaction, the url-state-manager component reads and writes the following parameters: date, genre, startTime, endTime, and channelId. If date is missing, the implementation may derive a suitable default (such as the current date) without hardcoding any specific value. If genre, startTime, or endTime are missing, the guide-filter-bar reflects that by showing an 'all genres' or 'full day' state in a non-hardcoded way. Whenever any of these parameters change via the UI, url-state-manager updates the query string (e.g., using history APIs) and triggers a new call to getTvGuideGrid(date, genre, startTime, endTime). The tv-guide-grid component renders based on the latest getTvGuideGrid response; it does not store independent copies of date, genre, or time window beyond what is necessary for rendering. The optional channelId parameter influences which channel row is scrolled into view after each render but does not change the data fetched. The program-detail-panel is opened and closed based on component-internal state (selectedProgramId and associated Program/Channel data) that is driven by UI events and getProgramDetails(programId) responses; it does not modify URL parameters. Recording state for the currently selected program is managed within program-detail-panel and recording-controls using the Recording object returned from scheduleRecording and is not persisted via URL parameters. The page does not use localStorage or any other client-side persistence for guide state; URL parameters and server responses from getTvGuideGrid, getProgramDetails, and scheduleRecording are the single sources of truth.

components.functionality: Centralizes reading and writing of URL query parameters (date, genre, startTime, endTime, channelId) and orchestrates calls to getTvGuideGrid based on those parameters. It notifies other components when the effective guide state changes.
event_handlers: Reads existing URL parameters, determines effective date/genre/time window, and calls getTvGuideGrid(date, genre, startTime, endTime).
Triggered when URL query parameters are updated (e.g., via history APIs); re-calls getTvGuideGrid with the new parameters and broadcasts the new state.
Receives filter changes from guide-filter-bar (date, genre, startTime, endTime), updates the URL query parameters accordingly, and triggers getTvGuideGrid.
Receives time window changes from tv-guide-grid (e.g., time navigation controls), updates startTime and endTime in the URL, and triggers getTvGuideGrid.
Receives the result of getTvGuideGrid and publishes the guide data (date, time_slots, channels/programs) to tv-guide-grid and other interested components.

components.functionality: Provides controls for selecting the guide date, time range, and genre filter. It reflects current values derived from URL parameters and sends changes back to url-state-manager.
event_handlers: Subscribes to url-state-manager to receive current date, genre, startTime, and endTime, initializing its UI controls accordingly.
Triggered when the user selects a different date; emits a filter-change event containing the new date while preserving existing genre and time window where applicable.
Triggered when the user selects or clears a genre; emits a filter-change event with the updated genre value.
Triggered when the user changes the starting time of the guide window; emits a filter-change event with the new startTime.
Triggered when the user changes the ending time of the guide window; emits a filter-change event with the new endTime.
Optional interaction that clears genre and/or time-range selections; emits a filter-change event that removes those parameters from the URL.

components.functionality: Displays the time/channel grid for the selected date and filters, using the time_slots and channels/programs returned by getTvGuideGrid. Allows users to select programs, navigate in time, and click channel headers to navigate to channel details.
event_handlers: Triggered when url-state-manager provides new getTvGuideGrid data; re-renders the grid with the latest date, time_slots, and channel/program rows.
After rendering, checks whether a channelId parameter is active in the URL; if so, scrolls or focuses the view to the corresponding channel row.
Triggered when the user clicks a program block; emits a programSelected event with the programId to program-detail-panel.
Triggered when the user clicks a channel name or logo; performs navigation to channel_detail.html?channelId={channel.id}.
Triggered by user interactions that move the time window (e.g., next or previous time blocks); calculates a new startTime/endTime and emits a timeWindowChange event to url-state-manager.
Triggered when user scrolls the channel list; used internally for UX (no API calls) and may notify other components about visible channels if needed.

components.functionality: Shows detailed information about a selected program and its channel, retrieved via getProgramDetails(programId). Hosts the recording-controls for scheduling a recording of the selected program.
event_handlers: Listens for programSelected events from tv-guide-grid, stores the selected programId, calls getProgramDetails(programId), and opens the panel with loading state until data arrives.

Triggered when getProgramDetails returns; binds Program and Channel data to the UI (title, description, start/end time, genre, rating, channel name) and passes programId and timing information to recording-controls.
Triggered when the user closes the panel; hides the panel and clears any transient state specific to the selected program, without modifying URL parameters.
Triggered when recording-controls successfully schedules a recording; updates local display (e.g., shows scheduled status and keep-days information using the Recording object).

components.functionality: Provides recording options (including a 'Keep for 7 days' selection) for the program currently shown in program-detail-panel and calls scheduleRecording(programId, keepDays) to schedule a recording.
event_handlers: Invoked when program-detail-panel loads a program; receives programId and relevant timing information (start_time, end_time) from the Program and resets recording state for that program.
Triggered when the user selects or adjusts the keep-days setting (e.g., choosing a preset labeled 'Keep for 7 days' which sets keepDays to 7); updates the internal keepDays value.
Triggered when the user confirms scheduling; calls scheduleRecording(programId, keepDays) and manages loading/disabled states while waiting for the response.

Handles the result of scheduleRecording; if success is true, stores the returned Recording object, surfaces the message to program-detail-panel, and may emit an event back to tv-guide-grid to mark the program as scheduled.
Triggered when the user dismisses an error or informational message related to scheduling; clears transient error state without affecting the underlying Recording data.

components.functionality: Optional controls adjacent to the grid to quickly jump between portions of the day or adjust the granularity of the time view; works by emitting updated startTime and endTime values.
event_handlers: Subscribes to the current startTime and endTime from url-state-manager to initialize its controls.
When the user chooses to view an earlier time segment, calculates an earlier time window and triggers timeWindowChange to url-state-manager with updated startTime/endTime.
When the user chooses to view a later time segment, calculates a later time window and triggers timeWindowChange to url-state-manager with updated startTime/endTime.
If the user changes the time granularity, adjusts how much startTime/endTime shift per interaction; the resulting window is always propagated to url-state-manager without hardcoded absolute times.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header with logo, nav, and neon CTA at the very top; below that, within a centered max-width container, a large left-aligned “TV Guide” page title; directly under the title, a single, full-width light strip that combines the guide-filter-bar on the left and time-zoom-controls on the right (playing the same role as the notification bar in the mockup); beneath this strip, the tv-guide-grid fills the main central area; the program-detail-panel sits directly below the grid as a full-width section within the same content column; recording-controls live inside the program-detail-panel’s upper area; finally, a tall multi-column dark footer and a thin copyright bar sit at the bottom, with a floating support/call button anchored at the bottom-right outside the main flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The page keeps the mockup’s linear, top-to-bottom structure: a full-width dark header with logo, nav, and neon CTA at the very top; below that, within a centered max-width container, a large left-aligned “TV Guide” page title; directly under the title, a single, full-width light strip that combines the guide-filter-bar on the left and time-zoom-controls on the right (playing the same role as the notification bar in the mockup); beneath this strip, the tv-guide-grid fills the main central area; the program-detail-panel sits directly below the grid as a full-width section within the same content column; recording-controls live inside the program-detail-panel’s upper area; finally, a tall multi-column dark footer and a thin copyright bar sit at the bottom, with a floating support/call button anchored at the bottom-right outside the main flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header and light filter/zoom strip remain shallow, preserving plenty of vertical space for the tv-guide-grid as the dominant content block (roughly the central half to two-thirds of the viewport). The program-detail-panel below is allocated a moderate but clearly secondary vertical band, roughly the lower quarter of the content area when expanded. Left and right gutters inside the main content container sit around 64–80px from the viewport edges, mirroring the mockup. Footer height is substantial, occupying roughly the bottom third of the visible page when scrolled to the bottom, consistent with the dense multi-column footer observed in the mockup.
layout_strategies.content_density: Density stays medium overall. The header and filter/zoom strip are airy with clear gaps and comfortable padding. The tv-guide-grid is visually denser due to many channels and time slots, but grid rows remain tall enough for readability and maintain consistent 8–16px internal padding. The program-detail-panel and recording-controls cluster are medium density: stacked info with clear headings, 8–12px between lines, and no crowding of controls. Footer columns remain the densest area text-wise but still follow the mockup’s open line spacing and column separation.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first hits the neon CTA in the header’s top-right and the logo on the left, then scans across the navigation. From there, attention drops down to the large “TV Guide” heading, then immediately to the high-contrast light strip containing filters and time zoom. The user then moves into the tv-guide-grid, scanning left-to-right along the time axis and top-to-bottom across channels. Selecting a program pulls focus downward into the program-detail-panel, where the recording-controls cluster in the panel’s top-right becomes the primary CTA focus. At the end of the vertical journey, the user reaches the footer columns. Throughout, a floating support/call button in the bottom-right acts as a secondary attractor outside the main content flow.

layout.overall_description: The TV Guide page sits on a dark, modern canvas with a full-width black header at the top and a tall dark footer at the bottom, both stretching edge-to-edge. Inside, a centered max-width content column is aligned to a 12-column grid, inset from the viewport edges by wide 64–80px gutters. Under the header, a bold, left-aligned “TV Guide” title anchors the page. Directly beneath it, a single pale strip spans the width of the content column—this is the functional equivalent of the mockup’s notification bar, housing filters on the left and time zoom controls on the right, standing out sharply against the dark background. Below this strip, the tv-guide-grid dominates the central area: a horizontally scrollable, dark timeline grid of channels and programs, visually occupying most of the vertical space above the fold. When a program is selected, a dark program-detail-panel appears immediately under the grid, full-width, with descriptive text on the left and a tight cluster of neon-accented recording-controls in the panel’s upper-right corner. The entire content stack—title, light control strip, grid, and detail panel—reads as a single vertical column, preserving the simple linear layout from the mockup. At the bottom, a multi-column footer with logo and link groups fills a tall band, followed by a slim copyright bar. A circular floating support/call button is anchored to the bottom-right corner of the viewport, hovering above the grid and footer, independent of the main document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-manager is a non-visual component that lives logically at the page level, spanning the entire TV Guide experience but without any visible footprint. Conceptually, it sits behind the main content column (TV Guide title, filter/zoom strip, grid, and detail panel), keeping the URL in sync with filters, time zoom, and selected program state. It does not occupy any width or height in the layout and does not affect the spacing of visible components; instead, it connects the guide-filter-bar, time-zoom-controls, tv-guide-grid, and program-detail-panel so that changes in those areas are reflected in the browser’s URL.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; no rendered box in the UI. Conceptually bound to the full page as an invisible layer that observes and updates state but does not alter layout flow or consume space.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The guide-filter-bar is positioned within the main content column directly below the large left-aligned “TV Guide” heading, mirroring the notification bar’s location in the mockup. It appears as a single, full-width horizontal strip on a light background (e.g., charcoal text on a light grey or muted dark-on-light), sharply contrasting with the surrounding dark canvas. The bar spans nearly the entire width of the content container, leaving equal left and right gutters (~64–80px from viewport edges). Vertically, it has about 16–20px padding top and bottom, making the strip roughly 56–72px tall. Inside the bar, filter elements (such as date selector, channel group dropdown, and category chips) are aligned to the left, starting flush with the internal 16–24px padding from the bar’s left edge. Items are arranged horizontally with 8–16px gaps between them; on narrower screens they wrap to a second line while still maintaining the bar’s airy feel. The right side of this strip is visually reserved for the time-zoom-controls, giving the bar a balanced left–right composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the document flow. It sits between the TV Guide heading and the tv-guide-grid, keeping its position as the user scrolls the grid; it does not stick or float by default, though it can be made sticky at the top of the content column on taller pages without breaking the linear layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The time-zoom-controls are embedded within the same light horizontal strip as the guide-filter-bar, occupying the right-hand side of that bar. They are right-aligned, sitting flush with an internal padding of about 16–24px from the bar’s right edge, and vertically centered within the strip’s height so they align nicely with the filters on the left. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal cluster (e.g., zoom-in/zoom-out buttons, a time-range slider, and shortcuts like “Now” or “Prime Time”) arranged left-to-right with small 8–12px gaps. Their buttons use the neon accent color for key interactive elements (like the active zoom level or “Now” state), making this right side of the strip visually active but not overpowering. On wide screens, the left filters and right zoom controls share the single strip with a clear empty buffer in the middle; on narrower screens, the time-zoom-controls break onto a second row within the same light area, still right-aligned, keeping the entire control zone as one cohesive block above the grid.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the guide-filter-bar’s container. It moves with the bar as part of the normal document flow. If the bar is made sticky at the top of the content area, the time-zoom-controls also become effectively sticky, always visible while the user scrolls the tv-guide-grid beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The tv-guide-grid sits directly below the light filter/zoom strip, occupying the full width of the main content column and acting as the dominant visual element in the central portion of the page.
