It is bounded on the left and right by the same wide gutters used across the layout, so the grid appears as a large, dark, horizontally scrollable panel framed within the central container. Vertically, there is a clear 24–32px gap between the bottom of the filter/zoom strip and the top of the grid, and another similar gap between the grid’s bottom edge and the top of the program-detail-panel. The grid’s height is designed to take up most of the viewport’s remaining height above the fold—roughly 50–60% of the overall page height on a typical desktop—so that multiple channels and time slots are visible at once without feeling cramped. Within the grid, a fixed-height top row displays time labels, and a leftmost sticky column lists channel names; the rest of the grid area shows program blocks aligned in a timeline. Rows are of consistent height with 8–12px internal padding and subtle dividing lines, keeping the density readable. The grid scrolls vertically to reveal more channels and horizontally to reveal later times while leaving the header, filter strip, and eventual detail panel in their respective vertical positions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow but internally scrollable. The grid occupies its allotted area below the filter/zoom strip and above the program-detail-panel; within that area, it can have both vertical and horizontal scrollbars while the rest of the page remains fixed. The left channel column and top time row can remain sticky inside the grid’s scrollable container to aid navigation, without affecting the rest of the page layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The program-detail-panel is positioned immediately below the tv-guide-grid within the same central content column, maintaining the mockup’s single-column structure. When a program is selected, the panel expands into a full-width horizontal band on the dark background, matching the grid’s width and alignment. There is a vertical gap of about 24–32px separating the bottom of the grid from the top of this panel, giving it room to breathe. The panel’s background is slightly lighter than the page’s base black (e.g., a deep charcoal) to distinguish it subtly, with a top border or subtle color shift but no visible shadows. Its height is flexible based on content but is typically around 25–30% of the viewport height, enough to show a title, time range, channel, description, and metadata comfortably with 16–24px of padding on all sides. Internally, content is arranged left-to-right: textual details (program name, episode info, schedule, synopsis) are aligned to the left, starting after the left padding; on the right, within the top portion of the panel, space is reserved for recording-controls. When no program is selected, this panel can collapse to a minimal bar (e.g., a slim “No program selected” strip) or disappear, allowing the grid to visually flow closer to the footer while still maintaining a clean vertical rhythm.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static in the document flow but conditionally rendered/expanded. When opened, it pushes the footer down and occupies its own vertical space under the grid. On longer pages it may become partially off-screen; optionally, it can be configured to stick to the bottom edge of the viewport once opened (a bottom-docked drawer that remains visible while the grid scrolls behind it), still full-width and shadow-free, preserving the flat aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The recording-controls live inside the program-detail-panel and are visually anchored to its top-right area. Within the panel’s inner 12-column grid, they typically span the rightmost 3–4 columns, starting at the same top padding line as the program title on the left. There is about 16–24px padding from the panel’s right edge to the outermost control. The controls themselves form a compact horizontal cluster of buttons and toggles—such as “Record”, “Record Series”, and maybe a dropdown for recording options—with the primary Record action in the neon accent color, making this corner of the panel the key CTA focus. Buttons share consistent height, with 8–12px gaps horizontally between them and 8–16px of vertical breathing room above and below. On narrower screens, the recording-controls stack beneath the main program text within the panel, spanning nearly the full width but still separated by a 16–24px vertical gap, effectively becoming a full-width button row at the bottom of the panel. This keeps the overall layout single-column while preserving clear hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Positioned relative to and moving with the program-detail-panel. It does not float independently; when the panel scrolls or sticks to the bottom, the recording-controls remain anchored in the panel’s top-right (or lower stacked area on small screens). It does not change the document flow outside the panel.

name: Settings
title: Settings
description: Account settings page focused on configuring and managing parental controls, including PIN creation, content rating limits, and purchase restrictions.

core_features: Load and display the current parental controls configuration using getParentalControlsSetting.
Allow users to create or update a 4-digit parental control PIN and confirmation, then save using updateParentalControlsSetting.
Allow users to set the maximum allowed content rating for playback using a dropdown or slider bound to the ParentalControlsSetting.max_content_rating enum.
Provide a toggle to require the parental control PIN before completing purchases and save this preference via updateParentalControlsSetting.
Highlight and auto-open the relevant settings subsection (e.g., parental controls) based on the incoming URL parameter section.
Provide navigation links back to dashboard.html and to help.html with the appropriate URL parameters reflecting the origin and context.
user_workflows:
View current parental controls:
1. User opens settings.html optionally with a section URL parameter.
2. On page load, the page reads the section parameter to determine which settings subsection to focus (e.g., parental_controls).
3. The page calls getParentalControlsSetting.
4. If has_parental_controls_configured is true, the retrieved settings object is bound to the parental controls form fields in a view-only or masked way (PIN is not shown in plain text; form is prepared for update).
5. If has_parental_controls_configured is false, the form is shown in an initial setup state prompting the user to create a PIN and choose preferences.

Set up parental controls for the first time:
1. User navigates to settings.html?section=parental_controls from dashboard.html or help.html.
2. The page calls getParentalControlsSetting and detects has_parental_controls_configured is false.
3. The parental controls form displays empty PIN fields and uninitialized preference controls.
4. User enters a 4-digit PIN in the PIN field and repeats it in the confirmation field.
5. User selects a maximum allowed content rating from the dropdown or slider populated from the ParentalControlsSetting.max_content_rating enum options.
6. User toggles whether purchases require the PIN.
7. User clicks the Save/Update button.
8. The page validates that PIN and confirm PIN match and that the PIN meets format rules before sending.
9. The page calls updateParentalControlsSetting with pin, confirmPin, maxContentRating, and blockPurchasesWithoutPin.
10. On success, the component displays a success message from the response and updates the local view state with the returned settings object.

Update existing parental controls:
1. User opens settings.html (with or without section=parental_controls in the URL).
2. The page calls getParentalControlsSetting and receives has_parental_controls_configured as true with a settings object.
3. The parental controls form initializes with the existing max_content_rating and block_purchases_without_pin values; the PIN fields show masked or empty inputs prepared for change.
4. User optionally enters a new PIN and confirmation, and/or changes the maximum content rating and/or toggles the purchase blocking switch.
5. User clicks Save/Update.
6. The page validates the submitted values (e.g., PIN match, required fields present).
7. The page calls updateParentalControlsSetting with the form values.
8. On success, the success message is shown, and the returned settings are rebound to the form state; on failure, an error message from the response is displayed.

Navigate back to the dashboard after updating settings:
1. After a successful update via updateParentalControlsSetting, the page surfaces a navigation link or button labeled to go back to the dashboard.
2. User clicks this link.
3. The browser navigates to dashboard.html with the URL parameter from=settings appended.
4. dashboard.html can use this parameter to optionally highlight that the user arrived from Settings, without further interaction on settings.html.

Request help from Settings context:
1. On settings.html, the user clicks a contextual Need Help? link within the parental controls section or page header.
2. The browser navigates to help.html with the URL parameter section=settings.
3. help.html can use this parameter to display help content related to settings or parental controls.

Deep-link to parental controls via URL parameter:
1. Another page, such as dashboard.html or help.html, links to settings.html with section=parental_controls.
2. On load, settings.html reads the section parameter.
3. The parental controls subsection is set as active, ensuring the view scrolls to or focuses on the parental controls form.
4. The page calls getParentalControlsSetting and renders the form based on the returned settings.
interactions:
On page load:
- Event: onLoad (document or main settings container).
- Behavior: Read the section URL parameter; if it matches parental_controls (or is absent and parental controls is default), set internal activeSection state accordingly.
- Behavior: Trigger getParentalControlsSetting and handle the response to populate the parental controls form state.

Change active settings section via internal navigation:
- Event: onClick on a section tab or link (e.g., Parental Controls, other settings sections if present).
- Behavior: Update the activeSection internal state.
- Behavior: Update the URL (e.g., settings.html?section=parental_controls) using navigation or history without reloading core data unnecessarily.
- Behavior: Ensure the parental controls form is visible when parental_controls is the selected section.

Edit PIN fields:
- Event: onChange on the PIN input.
- Behavior: Update local form state for pin.
- Event: onChange on the Confirm PIN input.
- Behavior: Update local form state for confirmPin.
- The PIN values are not pre-filled with any concrete numbers from the backend; only user-entered values are held in the form state.

Change maximum content rating:
- Event: onChange on the dropdown or slider used for selecting maxContentRating.
- Behavior: Update local form state for maxContentRating.
- Options displayed are derived from the ParentalControlsSetting.max_content_rating enum definition provided by the backend or a schema, not from a hardcoded arbitrary list.

Toggle purchase blocking:
- Event: onChange on the switch or checkbox controlling blockPurchasesWithoutPin.
- Behavior: Update local form state for blockPurchasesWithoutPin.

Submit parental controls form:
- Event: onSubmit on the parental controls form or onClick on the Save/Update button.
- Behavior: Run client-side validation (e.g., PIN match, required fields, format checks) using only user-entered values and current enum options.
- Behavior: If validation passes, call updateParentalControlsSetting with pin, confirmPin, maxContentRating, and blockPurchasesWithoutPin.
- Behavior: Disable the submit button while the request is in progress and optionally show a loading indicator.
- Behavior: On success (response.success is true), show response.message in a success component and update local settings state with response.settings.
- Behavior: On failure (response.success is false), show response.message as an error and do not override existing settings state.

Navigate back to the dashboard:
- Event: onClick on the Back to Dashboard link or button.
- Behavior: Navigate to dashboard.html?from=settings without modifying parental control data.
- This navigation does not call any additional interfaces from settings.html.

Open Help from Settings:
- Event: onClick on the Need Help? link.
- Behavior: Navigate to help.html?section=settings.

Handle URL parameter changes while on the page:
- Event: onURLParamChange (e.g., via popstate or link clicking that changes query parameters).
- Behavior: If section changes, update activeSection to match the new value.
- Behavior: If section transitions to or from parental_controls, show or hide the parental controls form accordingly without re-calling getParentalControlsSetting unnecessarily, unless a full page reload occurs.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page uses the URL query parameter section to determine which subsection of Settings is currently active. On initial load of settings.html, the application parses window.location.search to obtain the section parameter value. If section equals 'parental_controls', the parental controls subsection is set as active and its components are rendered or focused. If section is absent or does not match a known subsection key, the application applies a default behavior (which can include selecting parental controls as the default section) without assuming any static data.

The page maintains internal state for:
- activeSection: a string that mirrors the current section parameter and controls which settings subsection content is visible.
- parentalControlsSettings: an object reflecting the latest ParentalControlsSetting returned from getParentalControlsSetting or updateParentalControlsSetting.
- parentalControlsForm: a transient form state object containing the in-progress values for pin, confirmPin, maxContentRating, and blockPurchasesWithoutPin.
- requestStatus: flags for the loading, success, or error state of the last updateParentalControlsSetting call, and any message returned from the backend.

State transitions:
- On page load, after reading section, the application calls getParentalControlsSetting. The result populates parentalControlsSettings and initializes parentalControlsForm fields (except PIN fields, which are not pre-filled with any hardcoded or backend PIN values for security; they remain empty until the user enters input).
- When the user interacts with form controls, only parentalControlsForm is updated; parentalControlsSettings remains unchanged until a successful updateParentalControlsSetting response is received.
- On successful updateParentalControlsSetting, parentalControlsSettings is replaced with response.settings, parentalControlsForm may be reset or synchronized with this new state, and requestStatus is updated using response.success and response.message.
- When navigation links update the URL (e.g., to dashboard.html?from=settings or help.html?section=settings), the current settings page does not alter its internal data models and allows the browser navigation to occur.

All dynamic behavior is driven by the two assigned interfaces (getParentalControlsSetting and updateParentalControlsSetting) and URL parameters. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client-side storage, and it does not rely on any static, hardcoded content or example data for the parental control values.

components.functionality: Manages the selection of the currently active subsection within the Settings page (e.g., parental controls). It reads the section URL parameter on initialization, sets the activeSection state, and allows the user to switch sections via tab or link clicks. When parental_controls is active, it ensures the parental controls components are visible.
event_handlers: onLoad (read URL parameter 'section' and set activeSection)
onClick (tab or section link changes activeSection and updates URL query)

components.functionality: On page load or when the parental controls section first becomes active, this component invokes getParentalControlsSetting and stores the result in page state. It also interprets has_parental_controls_configured to determine whether to render the parental controls form in initial setup or edit mode.
event_handlers: onSectionActivated (when activeSection becomes 'parental_controls', trigger getParentalControlsSetting if not already loaded)
onLoad (if 'parental_controls' is the default or initial section, trigger getParentalControlsSetting)

components.functionality: Provides the main parental controls configuration interface. It allows the user to enter or update a PIN and confirmation, choose a maximum allowed content rating, and toggle whether purchases require the PIN. On submission, it validates the inputs and calls updateParentalControlsSetting. It adapts its presentation depending on whether parental controls were previously configured.
event_handlers: onChange (update local form state when the PIN field value changes)
onChange (update local form state when the Confirm PIN field value changes)
onChange (update local form state when the max content rating dropdown or slider value changes)
onChange (update local form state when the block purchases without PIN toggle changes)
onSubmit (validate form data and call updateParentalControlsSetting with pin, confirmPin, maxContentRating, and blockPurchasesWithoutPin)
onSuccess (on successful response from updateParentalControlsSetting, update bound ParentalControlsSetting state and clear or sync form state)
onError (display error message from updateParentalControlsSetting response.message without mutating existing settings state)

components.functionality: Displays success, error, and loading feedback related to parental controls operations. It reacts to the status of getParentalControlsSetting and updateParentalControlsSetting calls and shows the message returned by the updateParentalControlsSetting interface when applicable.
event_handlers: onLoadStatusChange (when getParentalControlsSetting is loading, succeeded, or failed, update displayed status)
onUpdateStatusChange (when updateParentalControlsSetting returns, show response.message and reflect success or error visually)

components.functionality: Provides contextual navigation actions for the Settings page, including a Back to Dashboard link and a Need Help? link. The links construct their target URLs with the required query parameters: dashboard.html with from=settings and help.html with section=settings.
event_handlers: onClick (navigate to 'dashboard.html?from=settings' when the Back to Dashboard link is clicked)
onClick (navigate to 'help.html?section=settings' when the Need Help? link is clicked)

components.functionality: Coordinates which components are rendered based on the active settings section. It listens to the settings-section-tabs component's activeSection state and the URL parameter 'section', and conditionally renders the parental-controls-loader and parental-controls-form only when the parental_controls section is active.
event_handlers: onActiveSectionChange (when activeSection is 'parental_controls', mount or reveal parental controls components)
onURLParamChange (if 'section' changes, re-evaluate which components should be rendered)

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width content column: global dark header (already shared site-wide), then a left-aligned 'Settings' page heading, immediately followed by the settings-section-tabs as the main in-page navigation. Below that, the settings-section-router occupies a single-column area that vertically stacks the parental-controls-status-messages (as a prominent notification strip), the parental-controls-form (as the main interactive block), and finally the settings-navigation-links (as low-emphasis secondary navigation). The footer remains a full-width dark band at the bottom, outside this component set.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Follow the mockup’s linear top-to-bottom structure inside a centered max-width content column: global dark header (already shared site-wide), then a left-aligned 'Settings' page heading, immediately followed by the settings-section-tabs as the main in-page navigation. Below that, the settings-section-router occupies a single-column area that vertically stacks the parental-controls-status-messages (as a prominent notification strip), the parental-controls-form (as the main interactive block), and finally the settings-navigation-links (as low-emphasis secondary navigation). The footer remains a full-width dark band at the bottom, outside this component set.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a shallow header and a generous central content area with ample dark background breathing room. The settings content column sits within a centered max-width container (roughly 60–70% of viewport width on desktop) with ~64–80px horizontal padding from the viewport edges. The section above the footer is visually taller than the header but shorter than the footer, echoing the mockup where the footer occupies the bottom third. Vertical spacing between major blocks (heading, tabs, status strip, form, navigation links, footer top edge) follows 32–48px gaps, with tighter 16–24px internal padding inside each component.
layout_strategies.content_density: Use medium density overall. The settings-section-tabs and parental-controls-status-messages are visually airy with clear padding, similar to the mockup’s notification bar. The parental-controls-form is denser but still uses comfortable vertical spacing between field groups. The settings-navigation-links are compact and text-heavy, closer in feeling to the footer’s denser link columns but on a smaller scale, and they remain clearly separated from the form by a distinct vertical gap.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands on the global header’s neon CTA and logo, then drops to the left-aligned 'Settings' heading. From there, the user’s gaze moves horizontally across the settings-section-tabs, then vertically down to the high-contrast parental-controls-status-messages bar (mirroring the mockup’s notification strip). The flow continues into the parental-controls-form fields from top to bottom, and finally into the lower-emphasis settings-navigation-links before transitioning into the global footer. A floating call button in the bottom-right of the viewport stays visually available but secondary to the main vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep black background with a full-width dark header and footer framing a centered, single-column settings content area. Inside this central column, a left-aligned 'Settings' heading anchors the page, followed immediately by a sleek row of section tabs that control which settings subsection is visible. Below the tabs, a pale, high-contrast notification strip spans the width of the content column, clearly separated from the tabs above and the form below by generous dark space. The parental controls form sits beneath this strip as the main focus: a vertically stacked group of PIN, rating, and purchase restriction controls contained in a moderately wide, invisible card-like column with subtle rounding on inputs and neon accents on key actions. Below the form, a small cluster of low-profile settings navigation links provides cross-links to other settings, visually echoing the footer’s textual density but occupying far less space. The footer then takes over the bottom portion of the page with multi-column links and branding. Throughout, everything aligns to an invisible 12‑column grid within the central container, and a floating neon call button rests in the lower-right corner of the viewport, hovering above both the content and footer, matching the site’s tech/gaming aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-section-tabs are positioned near the top of the main settings content column, directly below the left-aligned 'Settings' page heading and any brief subheading like 'Parental Controls'. They span the full width of the centered content container (roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop), but the individual tabs themselves are grouped toward the left, aligning with the heading to preserve a strong left rail. Vertically, there is about 24–32px of dark space above the tabs (from the heading) and 24px below the tabs before the next element. Each tab is a horizontally oriented pill or flat button with subtle corner rounding, low height, and neon green accent for the active tab. The tabs form a single horizontal row, each taking up a relatively small fraction of the width, leaving generous negative space to the right and preventing the row from feeling crowded. On narrow viewports, the row remains at the top of the content column, with tabs wrapping into a second line if necessary, but always maintaining clear 8–16px spacing between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow, anchored to the top of the settings content column. It does not scroll independently or stick, but its full-width container remains aligned to the same max-width grid as the rest of the page content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parental-controls-loader occupies the same region as the parental controls content inside the settings-section-router. When active, it sits centrally within the router’s content area, roughly mid-way down the viewport, with equal left and right margins matching the main content column width. Visually, it appears as a minimal spinner or progress indicator with a short status text directly below, both center-aligned horizontally. There is ample dark space surrounding it on all sides (at least 48px top and bottom), so it feels isolated and clearly distinct from the header, tabs, and footer. If the form content is already rendered, the loader can appear as a translucent overlay centered over the form area, dimming the underlying content very slightly without introducing heavy shadows, maintaining the flat, modern aesthetic.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the content within the settings-section-router. When overlaying, it uses absolute positioning within the router container to center itself both vertically and horizontally over the form, without affecting surrounding layout or pushing other elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parental-controls-form is the main vertical block within the settings-section-router and is positioned directly below the parental-controls-status-messages strip, with approximately 32px of vertical gap separating them. It is aligned to the left edge of the central content column but does not span its entire width: the form’s interactive fields and labels occupy roughly 60–70% of the content width, leaving comfortable dark margins on the right for visual breathing room. The form is organized top-to-bottom into clearly separated sections—such as 'Parental PIN', 'Content Rating Limits', and 'Purchase Restrictions'—each section separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. Within each section, fields are stacked in a single column, with labels aligned left and inputs spanning most of the form width. Primary actions (like 'Save Changes' or 'Update PIN') appear as neon green buttons aligned left under the last field, with 16–24px vertical padding inside and about 24px margin above them. The overall height of the form typically occupies the central third to half of the page, depending on the amount of content and viewport height, leaving visible dark background above and below before reaching the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the settings-section-router, growing vertically as needed. It respects the max-width grid and remains centered with the rest of the content. On smaller screens, the form expands to nearly the full width of the content column (minus minimal side padding) while preserving vertical spacing between groups.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The parental-controls-status-messages component is positioned immediately below the settings-section-tabs and above the parental-controls-form, echoing the mockup’s notification strip. It spans nearly the full width of the centered content column, with slight horizontal padding (16–24px) on both left and right inside the strip itself. The bar is a low-height horizontal rectangle with a light or mid-tone contrasting background against the dark page, subtle rounding on the corners, and no visible shadow. Inside the strip, a status icon or importance indicator sits on the far left, followed by a single or multi-line message block that flows to the right, both left-aligned with 8–16px spacing between icon and text. There is roughly 16–20px of vertical padding within the bar, making it taller than a standard line of text but still sleek and unobtrusive. Above the strip, there is about 24px of space from the tabs; below it, another 24–32px separates it from the form, strongly tying it into the main content flow while still feeling like a distinct, scannable element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, full-width bar within the content column. It appears or hides based on state but does not overlap other elements; instead, it pushes the form down when visible, maintaining the linear flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-navigation-links component sits at the bottom of the main settings content area inside the settings-section-router, directly below the parental-controls-form with a clear vertical gap of about 32–40px. It occupies the same max-width column as the form but feels visually lighter and less emphasized. The links are arranged as a compact group aligned to the left edge of the form fields, mirroring that alignment. Depending on the number of items, they can appear either as a short vertical stack (one link per line with 8–12px between) or as a horizontal row of text links with moderate spacing (16–24px) between them. The typography is smaller and lower contrast than the main form labels—closer to footer link styling—making this section feel like supportive navigation. The overall height of this block is relatively small, occupying only a thin band above the start of the global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and integrated into the normal flow at the bottom of the settings-section-router. It does not fix or float; instead, it scrolls off-screen naturally as the user moves up or down.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The settings-section-router functions as the main container for the settings subsection content and sits directly below the settings-section-tabs, spanning the full width of the central content column from left to right. Its top edge is aligned with the left edge of the tabs, with about 24–32px of vertical separation. Within this container, the sequence is strictly vertical: first the parental-controls-status-messages strip (when present), then the parental-controls-form as the central interactive area, and finally the settings-navigation-links at the bottom. The router’s height adjusts to fit its content, but on a typical viewport, it occupies most of the middle portion of the page between the tabs and the beginning of the global footer. The background within the router matches the page’s dark base, so the perception of boundaries comes from the aligned child components and their internal paddings rather than from a visible card outline.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, block-level container aligned to the global 12-column grid and centered in the viewport. It manages which subsection’s content appears (here, parental controls) but does not itself scroll independently; the entire page scrolls as a single document.

name: Billing
title: Billing
description: Manage your IPTV billing overview, switch billing cycles, apply promo codes, review eligible annual plans under a set price, and start subscription updates that route through checkout or inline confirmation.

core_features: Display a billing overview using getBillingOverview, including current billing cycle, next charge date, current amount, currency, payment method summary, and any last promo code.
Provide a billing cycle toggle (monthly vs annual) that updates the draft subscription change via updateDraftBillingCycle.
Allow users to enter and apply a promo code to the current draft subscription change using applyPromoCodeToDraft, and show feedback based on the response.
List annual-eligible plans for selection using getPlansForBillingSelection, sorted low-to-high by estimated annual total and filtered so only plans under the configured annual maximum are shown.
Allow the user to select one of the available annual plans and create/update a draft subscription change via startPlanChange.
Show a change summary from the current SubscriptionChange and provide an 'Update Subscription' action that navigates to checkout.html with the appropriate URL parameters.
Offer a 'Return to Billing Overview' navigation action that reloads billing.html with a fresh overview state, and a 'Return to Dashboard' link back to dashboard.html.
user_workflows:
1) View current billing overview: 
- User opens billing.html, optionally with ?from=dashboard or ?from=checkout.
- On page load, the page calls getBillingOverview.
- The billing overview panel shows billing cycle, next charge date, current amount, currency, payment method summary, and related subscription details.
- If from=checkout, the page also shows a non-blocking confirmation context (e.g., that a billing change may have just occurred), while still reflecting the latest data from getBillingOverview.

2) Switch to annual billing and review eligible plans: 
- From the billing overview, the user interacts with the billing cycle toggle.
- When the user selects the annual option, the page calls updateDraftBillingCycle with billingCycle='annual'.
- If updateDraftBillingCycle succeeds, the page updates its draft billing cycle state using the returned SubscriptionChange.
- The page calls getPlansForBillingSelection with billingCycle='annual', sort set to ensure low-to-high ordering, and maxAnnualTotal set to the required annual-cap threshold.
- The annual plan list component displays all returned plans (via Plan + estimated_annual_totals) that meet the criteria, highlighting which plan (if any) matches the current subscription or draft selection.

3) Apply a promo code and re-evaluate eligible annual plans: 
- The user enters a promo code into the promo-code form and submits.
- The page calls applyPromoCodeToDraft with the promoCode string.
- If applyPromoCodeToDraft fails (success=false), the form shows the message returned without altering draft state.
- If it succeeds, the page updates its SubscriptionChange and displays promo details (e.g., discount type and description, via the returned PromoCode and SubscriptionChange fields).
- After success, the page calls getPlansForBillingSelection again using the currently selected billing cycle and the same maxAnnualTotal threshold so that estimated_annual_totals reflect the applied promo and the plan list stays filtered to under the annual cap.

4) Select a new annual plan under the annual cap and start checkout: 
- With annual billing selected and eligible plans visible, the user selects a plan from the list (e.g., a radio button or 'Select' control per plan).
- When the user selects a plan, the page calls startPlanChange with planId set to the selected plan’s id, billingCycle='annual', and changeContext='billing_cycle_change'.
- If startPlanChange succeeds, the page updates its current SubscriptionChange, including estimated_new_annual_total and any applied promo.
- The change summary component now reflects the selected annual plan, the effective billing cycle, and the estimated totals after discount.
- If the SubscriptionChange indicates an estimated_new_annual_total that exceeds the annual cap (for safety), the page disables or hides the 'Update Subscription' button and shows a validation message.
- Otherwise, when the user clicks 'Update Subscription', the page navigates to checkout.html with URL parameters from=billing and context=billing_cycle_change.
- The checkout.html page then handles confirmation and payment based on the draft created by startPlanChange.

5) Return from checkout and refresh billing overview: 
- After completing checkout, the user is redirected (by the checkout flow) to billing.html?from=checkout.
- On load, the billing page reads from=checkout, calls getBillingOverview to fetch the updated billing state, and shows the billing overview as the primary view.
- Optionally, a transient confirmation state can be shown (e.g., indicating the change was completed) based solely on the navigation context (from=checkout), without assuming any static result data.
- The user can click 'Return to Billing Overview' at any time to reload billing.html?from=billing_overview, which again calls getBillingOverview and focuses on the overview panel.
- From the overview, the user can navigate back to the dashboard via a 'Return to Dashboard' link to dashboard.html?from=billing.
interactions:
Page load: 
- Event: window.onload or equivalent lifecycle hook.
- Action: Read URL parameter from (if present).
- Action: Call getBillingOverview.
- Result: Populate the billing overview component with BillingSettings and Subscription data.
- Conditional: If from=checkout, set a page-level flag to show post-checkout context (e.g., a compact confirmation banner) while still using live data from getBillingOverview.

Billing cycle toggle interaction: 
- Component: billing-cycle-toggle.
- Event: onBillingCycleChange when the user selects 'monthly' or 'annual'.
- Action: Call updateDraftBillingCycle with the selected billingCycle.
- Result (success): Update the draft SubscriptionChange in state and reflect the selected cycle in UI.
- If the selected cycle is 'annual', trigger a call to getPlansForBillingSelection with billingCycle='annual', sort configured for low-to-high, and maxAnnualTotal set to the annual cap. Bind returned plans and estimated_annual_totals to the annual-plan-list component.
- Result (failure): Show the returned message and keep the previous billing cycle selection.

Promo code application: 
- Component: promo-code-form.
- Event: onSubmitPromo when the form is submitted.
- Action: Call applyPromoCodeToDraft with promoCode from the input field.
- Result (success): Update SubscriptionChange, store the returned PromoCode for display, and show confirmation feedback.
- Follow-up: Call getPlansForBillingSelection using the current draft billing cycle and the annual cap, so the annual-plan-list reflects discounted estimated_annual_totals.
- Result (failure): Show an error message from the response; do not modify existing SubscriptionChange or plan list.

Annual plan selection: 
- Component: annual-plan-list.
- Event: onLoadAnnualPlans triggered when annual billing is active or when the promo code changes.
- Action: Call getPlansForBillingSelection with billingCycle='annual', sort for low-to-high, maxAnnualTotal set to the cap.
- Result: Bind the returned array of Plan entities and the corresponding estimated_annual_totals to the list; only plans with an estimated_annual_total at or under the cap (per API filtering) are shown.
- Event: onSelectAnnualPlan when a user chooses a specific plan from the list.
- Action: Call startPlanChange with planId equal to the selected plan id, billingCycle='annual', changeContext='billing_cycle_change'.
- Result (success): Update the page-level SubscriptionChange; highlight the selected plan and update the change-summary component.
- Result (failure): Show the returned message and keep any previous selection.

Update subscription and navigation to checkout: 
- Component: plan-change-summary.
- Event: onClickUpdateSubscription when the user activates the 'Update Subscription' button.
- Precondition: SubscriptionChange is present, success from startPlanChange is true, and estimated_new_annual_total is at or under the annual cap.
- Action: Navigate to checkout.html with URL parameters from=billing and context=billing_cycle_change.
- Effect: No additional billing API calls are made on click; the checkout page uses the current draft SubscriptionChange already established via startPlanChange.

Return to Billing Overview and Dashboard navigation: 
- Component: navigation-links and/or confirmation panel.
- Event: onClickReturnToBillingOverviewLink (e.g., when the user clicks 'Return to Billing Overview').
- Action: Navigate to billing.html?from=billing_overview.
- On reload: Call getBillingOverview and focus the overview panel; any previously selected draft state is not assumed unless reconstructed from SubscriptionChange.
- Event: onClickReturnToDashboard when the user clicks 'Return to Dashboard'.
- Action: Navigate to dashboard.html?from=billing.
page_functionality.state_logic:
URL parameters and interface responses govern page state. The primary URL parameter is 'from':
- from=dashboard: Indicates the user reached Billing from the dashboard. On load, the page calls getBillingOverview and shows the overview as the primary focus with default controls collapsed or neutral. No special confirmation state is shown.
- from=checkout: Indicates a likely return from checkout.html. On load, the page calls getBillingOverview to reflect any recent changes. A page-level flag (derived purely from the from parameter) can show a non-persistent confirmation context (e.g., that billing may have been updated), but all displayed amounts and cycles come from the fresh BillingSettings and Subscription returned by getBillingOverview.
- from=billing_overview: Indicates the user explicitly requested to refresh or return to the billing overview. On load, the page calls getBillingOverview, emphasizes the overview section, and can treat any prior draft SubscriptionChange as outdated unless a fresh SubscriptionChange is created via new API calls.

Additional state is derived entirely from API responses and not persisted via localStorage:
- The current billing overview state (billing cycle, next charge date, current amount, currency, payment method) is always taken from getBillingOverview and stored in memory for binding to the overview and summary components.
- Draft change state (selected billing cycle, selected plan, applied promo code, and estimated totals) is represented by the latest SubscriptionChange object returned by updateDraftBillingCycle, applyPromoCodeToDraft, or startPlanChange. The page keeps a single current SubscriptionChange instance in memory and updates it after each relevant API call.
- The annual plan list state is derived from getPlansForBillingSelection. The billing cycle and maxAnnualTotal parameters passed into this interface are driven by the current draft billing cycle and the fixed business rule for the annual cap. Sorting order is provided via the 'sort' parameter; the page does not hardcode any list order beyond what the interface returns.
- Navigation actions (back to checkout, billing overview, or dashboard) are represented only via URL changes, so the browser history and bookmarking behaviors reflect the user's location. No client-side persistence (e.g., localStorage) is used to store plans, promos, or subscription details; these are always re-fetched through the assigned interfaces when needed.

components.functionality: Fetches and displays the current billing overview, including billing cycle, next charge date, current amount, currency, payment method summary, and main subscription metrics. It reacts to page-load and 'Return to Billing Overview' navigation by calling getBillingOverview and rebinding data.

Allows the user to switch the draft billing cycle between monthly and annual. On change, it updates the draft SubscriptionChange via updateDraftBillingCycle and, when set to annual, triggers loading of annual plans for selection.

Provides an input for a promo code and applies it to the current draft subscription change. On submission, it calls applyPromoCodeToDraft, displays success or error messaging from the response, updates the SubscriptionChange and PromoCode bindings on success, and then prompts a refresh of eligible plans to reflect revised estimated totals.

Shows annual plans that are eligible under the configured annual price cap. When annual billing is selected or a promo code changes, it calls getPlansForBillingSelection with billingCycle='annual', a low-to-high sort configuration, and the maxAnnualTotal cap. It binds the returned Plan array and estimated_annual_totals, rendering each plan with its key attributes and estimated annual total. User selection of a plan calls startPlanChange to update the current SubscriptionChange.

Summarizes the current draft subscription change based on SubscriptionChange and the existing BillingSettings, including selected plan, billing cycle, applied promo (if any), and estimated new totals. It validates that the estimated_new_annual_total is within the allowed range before enabling the 'Update Subscription' action. When the action is triggered, it navigates to checkout.html with from=billing and context=billing_cycle_change.

Optionally displays an inline confirmation or contextual message when the user returns from checkout (based on the from URL parameter). It does not assume static outcome data but can show state derived from the latest BillingSettings and Subscription returned by getBillingOverview. It includes a 'Return to Billing Overview' action that reloads billing.html with from=billing_overview.

Provides navigation controls for leaving the billing page or resetting its state. It includes a 'Return to Billing Overview' link (navigates to billing.html?from=billing_overview) and a 'Return to Dashboard' link (navigates to dashboard.html?from=billing). It does not call any billing APIs directly, but navigation results in getBillingOverview being triggered by the overview panel on reload.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the mockup’s single-column cart page. At the very top, a full-width dark header bar contains the logo on the left, navigation links centered (navigation-links component), and a bright neon CTA on the right. Below the header, the main content column begins with a large left-aligned 'Billing' page title. Under that, the billing-overview-panel comes first, followed by the billing-cycle-toggle, then a full-width promo-code-form styled like the mockup’s notification strip. The annual-plan-list sits next as a vertical stack of plan items. Near the bottom of the main content, the plan-change-summary panel appears, and when needed, the inline-confirmation-panel shows immediately beneath it as a light, high-contrast strip. Below the main content, a tall, multi-column footer (out of scope for components) fills the lower third of the page, with a thin copyright bar at the very bottom. A global floating call/support button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport, outside normal flow, echoing the mockup’s floating action pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a linear, top-to-bottom layout inside a centered max-width container, mirroring the mockup’s single-column cart page. At the very top, a full-width dark header bar contains the logo on the left, navigation links centered (navigation-links component), and a bright neon CTA on the right. Below the header, the main content column begins with a large left-aligned 'Billing' page title. Under that, the billing-overview-panel comes first, followed by the billing-cycle-toggle, then a full-width promo-code-form styled like the mockup’s notification strip. The annual-plan-list sits next as a vertical stack of plan items. Near the bottom of the main content, the plan-change-summary panel appears, and when needed, the inline-confirmation-panel shows immediately beneath it as a light, high-contrast strip. Below the main content, a tall, multi-column footer (out of scope for components) fills the lower third of the page, with a thin copyright bar at the very bottom. A global floating call/support button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport, outside normal flow, echoing the mockup’s floating action pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header is a relatively shallow band (~64px–80px high) spanning full width, with its inner content constrained to the central grid and padded horizontally by ~64–80px from the viewport edges. The main billing content uses a single centered column within this grid, occupying roughly 70–80% of the max-width container on desktop, with consistent left alignment of section titles and components. Vertical spacing between major sections (overview, toggle, promo bar, plan list, summary panel) uses 24–40px gaps (multiples of 8px), preserving the airy feel of the mockup’s cart page. The promo-code-form and inline-confirmation-panel each span the full width of the content column, visually similar to the mockup’s full-width notification strip. The footer occupies a significant vertical space (about the bottom third of the page), with generous top padding and clearly separated link columns. The floating call button uses a fixed size (~56–64px) and stays inset ~24px–32px from the bottom and right edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains medium overall, matching the mockup. The header and the billing-overview-panel are relatively airy, with comfortable padding and limited text. The billing-cycle-toggle row is light and uncluttered, containing only a label and the toggle control. The promo-code-form and inline-confirmation-panel are slim, high-contrast strips with a concise message and one action, reflecting the cart notification bar’s simplicity. The annual-plan-list is the densest region, presenting stacked plan items with clear vertical spacing and readable line height, similar to the footer’s denser but still legible columns. The plan-change-summary panel groups a bit more information (current vs new plan, pricing change, and a CTA) but uses clear hierarchy and spacing to avoid visual crowding.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Visual flow follows the mockup’s pattern: the eye is first drawn to the neon CTA in the top-right of the header and the logo at top-left, then moves horizontally across the navigation-links in the center. From there, attention drops down to the large 'Billing' heading, then immediately into the billing-overview-panel. After scanning the overview, the eye moves slightly right to the billing-cycle-toggle, then down into the high-contrast promo-code-form bar, echoing the cart page’s notification bar emphasis. Below, the gaze travels through the annual-plan-list from top card to bottom. Finally, attention is pulled toward the plan-change-summary near the bottom of the main content and, when present, to the inline-confirmation-panel’s bright strip just under it. The vertical flow then continues naturally into the footer logo and link columns. Throughout, the floating call/support button in the bottom-right acts as a secondary attractor at the periphery of vision, similar to the mockup’s floating action button.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep black background with a centered max-width content column, framed by a full-width dark header at the top and a tall, multi-column footer at the bottom. The header is slim and modern, with the brand logo on the far left, navigation-links neatly centered across the middle, and a bright neon-green CTA button anchored on the far right, all aligned to a 12-column grid and padded generously from the viewport edges. Below this, the main content begins with a bold, left-aligned 'Billing' title with ample breathing room above and below, establishing a clean entry point. Immediately under the title, the billing-overview-panel appears as a dark, slightly rounded block that spans about three-quarters of the content width, containing the essential billing summary in a calm, medium-density layout. Just beneath, the billing-cycle-toggle sits in its own horizontal row, slightly right-weighted within the same column, providing a clear, minimal control for switching between monthly and annual plans. The promo-code-form follows as a full-width, lighter-toned strip—visually similar to the mockup’s cart notification bar—with an icon and label on the left and the input plus 'Apply' button on the right, standing out crisply against the dark backdrop. Below this band, the annual-plan-list stacks plan options in vertically separated blocks, each aligned to the left within the main column, creating a simple scroll-down narrative of eligible annual plans. Near the bottom of the page, just before the footer, the plan-change-summary appears as a prominent dark panel with neon-accented CTA, summarizing changes and prompting confirmation. When a change is submitted, the inline-confirmation-panel appears directly beneath that summary as a slim, high-contrast bar that feels like a positive notification banner. All of this sits above a substantial, dark footer that mirrors the mockup: logo and description on the left, three link columns on the right, and a thin, low-contrast copyright bar at the very bottom. A neon-accented floating call/support button hovers in the lower-right corner of the viewport, independent of the document flow and always visible, reinforcing the tech-forward, streaming-service aesthetic.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links component resides inside the global header bar at the very top of the page. The header itself is full-width, with a dark background and a height of roughly 64–72px. Within this header, content is constrained to a centered max-width container (approximately 1120–1200px) aligned to a 12-column grid. The logo occupies the leftmost columns and the primary neon CTA button sits in the rightmost columns; the navigation-links sit between them, horizontally centered in the middle of the header. The nav links form a horizontal row of text items, evenly spaced with about 24px–32px between each link, aligned vertically to the header’s midline. There is approximately 16px–20px of padding above and below the nav text inside the header band, following the mockup’s airy header style. On wider screens, the navigation-links region spans roughly the central 50%–60% of the header’s inner width, ensuring a strong, centered focal line. On scroll, the header (and thus the navigation-links) remains fixed at the top if the overall site pattern supports a sticky nav, preserving constant access without interfering with the main billing column below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within a full-width header bar that may be sticky/fixed to the top of the viewport; otherwise static in the document flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing-overview-panel appears in the main content area, just below the large 'Billing' page title. The back-to-back spacing between the title and the panel is generous—about 32px–40px—creating a clear visual separation. The main content column is centered and constrained to the same max-width as the header’s inner content; within this, the billing-overview-panel is left-aligned and spans about 70%–80% of the content width on desktop, maintaining a single-column feel. The panel is a dark, slightly lighter-than-background rectangle with subtly rounded corners (4–8px radius), no visible shadow, and internal padding of roughly 24px–32px on all sides. The content inside may be organized into two or three short text blocks and key values, but the overall density remains medium with generous line spacing and 8–16px gaps between lines. There is an ample vertical gap of about 24px–32px between the bottom edge of this panel and the next element (billing-cycle-toggle), ensuring the overview feels like a distinct, first-stop section in the billing flow. On smaller screens, the panel expands to nearly full width of the content column with maintained padding, keeping the same vertical stacking pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the main content column, participating in normal vertical flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The billing-cycle-toggle sits directly below the billing-overview-panel, separated by a vertical gap of approximately 24px–32px. It occupies a single horizontal row within the main content column. The row spans the full width of the column, but the actual toggle control is slightly right-weighted to echo the mockup’s top-right CTA emphasis: a label or small text is aligned to the left half of the row, while the toggle itself is aligned toward the right side, roughly in the rightmost third of the column. The overall height of this row is relatively slim, with about 12px–16px of vertical padding above and below the control, keeping it visually light. There is clear whitespace above and below to prevent the toggle from feeling cramped between the overview panel and the promo-code-form. On mobile, the label may stack above the toggle but still remain centered within this same row area, preserving the sense of a single, self-contained control strip.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static inline-row element within the main content column, aligned within the grid but not fixed or sticky.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The promo-code-form appears beneath the billing-cycle-toggle, with a vertical gap of roughly 24px–32px between them. It visually echoes the mockup’s notification strip by presenting as a full-width, horizontally oriented bar spanning the entire width of the main content column. The background is a lighter tone compared to the dark page background (e.g., a dark gray or desaturated panel), creating a strong contrast band through the center of the page. The bar has slightly rounded corners (4–8px) and uses generous horizontal padding of about 16px–24px on each side and vertical padding of 12px–16px, keeping it slim but clearly tappable/clickable. Inside the bar, the left side (roughly 60% of the bar’s width) contains an icon or small indicator followed by a label like 'Have a promo code?' and perhaps a short explanatory text. The right side (about 40% of the bar) holds the text input for the code and a compact neon-accented 'Apply' button, aligned to the right edge. The content is horizontally centered within the bar’s height, mirroring the centered icon/text composition of the mockup’s cart notification. There is at least 32px of spacing between the bottom of this bar and the top of the annual-plan-list to maintain clear sectional separation.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static strip-like element spanning the main content column; behaves like a horizontal notification bar within the normal scroll flow.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The annual-plan-list sits below the promo-code-form, separated by about 32px–40px of vertical space, and forms the main body of the billing page. This component occupies the full width of the main content column but is visually composed of vertically stacked plan items (cards) that each span roughly 70%–80% of the column’s width, left-aligned with the other content for a cohesive single-column feel. Each plan card has a dark background slightly lighter than the page background, subtle corner rounding, and internal padding of around 16px–24px. Cards are separated from each other by vertical gaps of 16px–24px, echoing the spacing between footer link lines in the mockup. Within each card, text lines (plan name, description, price, features) are separated by 8px–12px of vertical space, creating a medium content density that remains easily scannable. Primary plan actions (e.g., 'Switch to this plan') are usually aligned to the right of each card, possibly using neon-accented buttons or links. The entire list creates a clear vertical reading path down the page, and there is substantial space (about 40px–48px) below the final card before the plan-change-summary panel, visually indicating a transition from browsing to confirming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list within the main content column; all items scroll naturally in order without fixed positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-change-summary component appears near the bottom of the main content area, immediately after the annual-plan-list, with a vertical gap of approximately 40px–48px to signal its importance as a final decision step. It spans the same width as the billing-overview-panel and promo-code-form, left-aligned within the main column and covering about 70%–80% of the column width on desktop. The panel has a dark, slightly elevated-looking background (without shadows), neon accent elements (such as a border line on the top edge or accented CTA button), and rounded corners of 4–8px. Internal padding is generous—about 24px–32px on all sides—to comfortably house a summary of the current plan, the newly selected plan, pricing differences, and key terms. Information is arranged in a vertical stack with clear labels and values, separated by 8px–16px line gaps, culminating in a prominent neon CTA button (e.g., 'Confirm plan change') aligned to the right or full-width on mobile. There is at least 24px–32px of space below this panel before any inline-confirmation-panel appears, or before the footer when the confirmation is hidden, ensuring that the summary feels visually anchored but not cramped against the page’s bottom elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Primarily a static block in the normal flow; optionally may become sticky near the bottom of the viewport on large screens to remain visible while scrolling, but still aligned within the main content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline-confirmation-panel is an inline, notification-like strip that appears only after a successful billing action, directly related to the plan-change-summary. When visible, it is inserted immediately below the plan-change-summary panel, with a modest vertical gap of about 16px–24px between them. Visually, it strongly references the mockup’s cart notification bar: it spans the full width of the main content column, uses a noticeably lighter background (e.g., a light gray or desaturated tinted bar) to contrast against the dark page, and has subtle rounding (4–8px) with no shadow. Inside this strip, an icon or status indicator (such as a checkmark) is placed toward the far left, followed by a concise confirmation message. If a secondary link or button (like 'View receipt' or 'Undo') is present, it sits on the far right of the bar, aligned horizontally with the text. Padding inside the bar is about 12px–16px vertically and 16px–24px horizontally, making it tall enough to feel substantial without overwhelming the main layout. The panel aligns perfectly with the left and right edges of the promo-code-form and plan-change-summary components, reinforcing the sense of a structured, single-column spine. Below this confirmation strip, there is ample space (32px–40px) before the footer begins, ensuring it reads as part of the main billing content rather than blending into the footer area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears inline within the normal document flow, pushing surrounding content up or down; no fixed or floating behavior.

name: Search Results
title: Search Results
description: Displays IPTV on-demand content search results based on a text query with resolution, content type, genre, and sorting controls, and allows navigation to a specific series detail page.

core_features: Read URL parameters (query, resolution, content_type, genre, sort) and construct a search request using the searchSeries interface.
Display search results returned from searchSeries as a list/grid of content items, focusing on series and other on‑demand content types defined in the Series model.
Provide filter controls for resolution, content type, and genre that update the URL parameters and re-trigger searchSeries when changed.
Provide sorting control (e.g., most_popular, top_results) that updates the sort URL parameter and re-triggers searchSeries.
Show the currently applied filters and sort state, synchronized with URL parameters and searchSeries.applied_filters.
Handle empty or missing query parameters by showing a controlled empty/search-prompt state without calling searchSeries until a valid query is provided.
Handle no-results cases from searchSeries by showing a functional no-results state with options to clear or adjust filters (through URL changes).
On clicking a series result tile, navigate to series.html with seriesId and fromQuery URL parameters derived from the selected Series record and current query.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1 – Run a search from the global header:
1. User enters a search term in the global header search field on any page and submits the form.
2. The site navigates to search.html with a URL containing at least the query parameter.
3. On load, the Search Controller component reads the URL, passes query and derived filters/sort to searchSeries, and receives a list of Series results.
4. The Results Grid component renders the returned Series items as clickable tiles.
5. User reviews the results and optionally clicks a tile, navigating to series.html with seriesId and fromQuery set.

Workflow 2 – Refine results with filters on search.html:
1. User arrives on search.html with an existing query in the URL and sees initial results.
2. User interacts with the filter controls (e.g., selects a resolution, content type, or genre).
3. Filter components update the URL parameters (resolution, content_type, genre) and push a new URL state without a full page reload.
4. The Search Controller observes the URL change, re-calls searchSeries with updated filters, and updates the Results Grid.
5. Applied Filters Summary updates to show the active filters; user can remove individual filters, which again updates the URL and triggers a new search.

Workflow 3 – Change sorting of results:
1. User views a set of search results on search.html.
2. User selects a different sort option in the Sort Control component.
3. Sort Control updates the sort URL parameter and pushes the new URL state.
4. The Search Controller re-invokes searchSeries with the new sort value and updates the Results Grid when the response arrives.

Workflow 4 – Handle no results and adjust search:
1. User submits a query and filters that return zero results from searchSeries.
2. No Results State component displays a functional message and actions like clearing filters or editing the query.
3. If user opts to clear filters, the component removes resolution, content_type, genre, and sort parameters from the URL (leaving query intact) and pushes the new state.
4. The Search Controller calls searchSeries again using only the query and application-level default sort, and Results Grid updates accordingly.

Workflow 5 – Adjust query directly on search.html:
1. User is on search.html and wants to refine the text query itself.
2. User edits the query in the inline search input or the global header search and submits.
3. The page navigates (or updates) to search.html with the new query in the URL and any existing filters/sort either preserved or reset according to product rules.
4. Search Controller reads updated URL parameters, calls searchSeries, and displays updated results.
interactions:
Interaction – Page load / URL change:
- Trigger: search.html loads or the URL query string changes.
- Behavior: Search Controller parses query, resolution, content_type, genre, and sort parameters from the URL. If query is present and non-empty, it builds the searchSeries request: query maps directly, filters maps resolution, content_type (to contentType), and genre, and sort maps to sort. It then calls searchSeries and updates bound components with the response.

Interaction – Filter change (resolution/content_type/genre):
- Trigger: User selects or deselects a filter option.
- Behavior: Relevant filter component updates the corresponding URL parameter in lowercase_snake_case (resolution, content_type, genre). It uses history.pushState or equivalent to update the URL without full reload. The URL change triggers Search Controller to re-call searchSeries with updated filters.

Interaction – Sort change:
- Trigger: User selects a different sort option in the Sort Control.
- Behavior: Sort Control updates the sort URL parameter and pushes a new URL state. Search Controller detects the URL change, calls searchSeries with the same query and filters but new sort, and updates Results Grid and Applied Filters Summary.

Interaction – Clear individual filter:
- Trigger: User clicks a remove/clear control on an applied filter chip in Applied Filters Summary.
- Behavior: Component removes that filter’s parameter from the URL, pushes updated URL state, and thereby triggers a new search via Search Controller.

Interaction – Clear all filters:
- Trigger: User clicks a 'clear filters' action (if available) in Applied Filters Summary.
- Behavior: Component removes resolution, content_type, genre, and sort parameters from the URL (query remains unchanged unless product rules specify otherwise). Search Controller re-runs searchSeries with only query (and application-configured default sort) and updates results.

Interaction – Update query from inline search input:
- Trigger: User types a new query in an inline search field on search.html and submits.
- Behavior: Inline Search Form updates the query parameter in the URL (optionally resetting filters/sort according to rules) and navigates or pushes new state. Search Controller calls searchSeries with updated query and filters/sort derived from the URL.

Interaction – Click search result tile:
- Trigger: User clicks on a result tile representing a series in Results Grid.
- Behavior: The tile’s click handler reads the Series.id and current query from state/URL, then navigates to series.html with URL parameters seriesId set to the selected Series.id and fromQuery set to the current query parameter value.

Interaction – Handle search error:
- Trigger: searchSeries returns an error or fails.
- Behavior: Search Controller sets an error state but does not mutate URL parameters. An Error State component displays a functional error message and a retry control that re-calls searchSeries with the current URL-derived parameters when activated.
page_functionality.state_logic: The canonical state of the Search Results page is encoded in the URL query parameters, not in localStorage. On every initial load or history navigation event, the Search Controller reads the following parameters from the URL: query (string), resolution (string), content_type (string), genre (string), and sort (string). If query is missing or empty, the page should not invoke searchSeries and should instead show a prompt or idle state. When query is present, the controller constructs the searchSeries call as: searchSeries({ query, filters: { resolution: resolution || undefined, contentType: content_type || undefined, genre: genre || undefined }, sort: sort || application_configured_default_sort }). The mapping between URL parameters and searchSeries parameters is: query maps directly; resolution maps to filters.resolution; content_type (lowercase_snake_case) maps to filters.contentType (camelCase); genre maps to filters.genre; and sort maps to the sort argument. If any parameter contains an unsupported value, the controller may ignore that parameter when building the filters/sort, while leaving the URL unchanged or normalizing it on the next push. All filter and sort UI changes update the URL parameters first (via pushState/replaceState) and do not directly set backend-related state; the subsequent URL change is what triggers re-execution of searchSeries. The applied_filters object returned from searchSeries is considered authoritative for what is actually applied on the backend and is used to render the Applied Filters Summary. However, URL parameters remain the single source of truth for client-side state; discrepancies (e.g., backend rejecting an invalid filter) should result in the UI adjusting to match applied_filters while optionally normalizing the URL in a subsequent navigation. Navigation to series.html uses URL parameters and does not alter any search.html state: series.html?seriesId={Series.id}&fromQuery={current_query}.

components.functionality: Central orchestration component that parses URL parameters, calls the searchSeries interface, manages loading/error/no-results states, and distributes results and applied filter data to child components.

Provides an optional inline query input on search.html (in addition to the global header search) to let the user adjust the search term without leaving the page. It updates the query URL parameter and triggers a new search via Search Controller.

Manages resolution, content type, and genre filter selections. On change, it updates the corresponding URL parameters (resolution, content_type, genre) and allows Search Controller to re-run searchSeries with the updated filters.

Allows the user to select a sorting method (e.g., most_popular or top_results). It updates the sort URL parameter, which triggers Search Controller to re-call searchSeries and refresh the result ordering.

Displays the currently applied query, filter, and sort state based on URL parameters and searchSeries.applied_filters, and provides controls to remove individual filters or clear all filters, updating the URL and indirectly triggering a new search.

Renders the list or grid of Series records returned by searchSeries.results. Each item is passed to a Result Tile component and is clickable to navigate to series.html with the appropriate URL parameters.

Represents a single Series search result. It displays key metadata from the Series entity and handles click events to navigate to series.html with seriesId set to Series.id and fromQuery set to the current query parameter.

Displays when searchSeries.results is empty but a query is present. Provides actions to clear filters or modify the search query, which update URL parameters and cause Search Controller to re-run searchSeries.

Displays when there is no query parameter on search.html, prompting the user to enter a search term via the inline search form or global header search. It does not call searchSeries until a query is provided.
Shows a loading state while a searchSeries request is in progress and hides once a response or error is received.
Displays error information when searchSeries fails and provides a retry action that re-calls searchSeries with the current URL-derived parameters.

Utility component that listens for browser navigation events (e.g., back/forward, pushState changes) and notifies the Search Controller of URL changes so that searchSeries is always executed with the latest query and filter parameters.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom flow from the mockup. Global header at the very top (logo left, nav center, neon CTA right), then the main "Search Results" content in a single centered column constrained by a 12‑column grid, then a tall multi-column footer at the bottom. Inside the main content: inline-search-form sits near the top, followed by a horizontal row combining filter-bar (left) and sort-control (right), then a full-width applied-filters-summary strip, then the main results area which shows either results-grid (with result-tile cards) or one of the empty/status states. The floating call CTA in the bottom-right remains above everything, independent of this flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use the same linear, top-to-bottom flow from the mockup. Global header at the very top (logo left, nav center, neon CTA right), then the main "Search Results" content in a single centered column constrained by a 12‑column grid, then a tall multi-column footer at the bottom. Inside the main content: inline-search-form sits near the top, followed by a horizontal row combining filter-bar (left) and sort-control (right), then a full-width applied-filters-summary strip, then the main results area which shows either results-grid (with result-tile cards) or one of the empty/status states. The floating call CTA in the bottom-right remains above everything, independent of this flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Maintain a shallow header band, a visually lighter middle content band, and a vertically substantial footer that occupies roughly the bottom third on typical desktop heights. Within the main content, allocate a modest vertical slice for the search form and controls (about the top 1/4 of the content area), and reserve the bulk (about 1/2 of the viewport height or more) for the results-grid / empty states. Horizontal content stays within a centered max-width container (e.g., 1120–1200px) with generous side padding (64–80px from viewport edges). Vertical gaps between major sections (search form vs filters, filters vs results, results vs footer) sit around 32–48px, with smaller 8–16px gaps between related controls.
layout_strategies.content_density: Keep density medium and breathable, mirroring the mockup. The search form and filter/sort row are airy with clear separation. The applied-filters-summary behaves like a single, strong, high-contrast notification strip with concise content. The results-grid introduces higher density via multiple tiles but maintains comfortable 16–24px gutters between items and 8–12px line spacing inside each tile. Empty and error states appear as singular, centered blocks with plenty of dark negative space around them. No cramped tables or overly complex layouts.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Guide the eye from the bright neon CTA in the header and logo at the top, down to the inline-search-form near the top-left of the content area, then across the horizontal filter-bar and sort-control row. From there, the eye drops into the high-contrast applied-filters-summary strip (similar to the mockup’s notification bar), then down into the results-grid where tiles create a scanning pattern from left to right, row by row. If there are no results, the centered empty state message becomes the focus. The footer logo and columns anchor the bottom of the page, with the floating call button in the bottom-right acting as a secondary attractor once the user scrolls through the results.

layout.overall_description: The page sits on a deep black background with a centered max-width content column aligned to a 12‑column grid. A full-width dark header spans the top, with the IPTV brand logo on the left, navigation links in the center, and a neon green CTA button on the right. Below this, the main content begins with an inline search section positioned in the upper-left of the content column, giving users an immediate way to refine their query.
