Directly beneath it runs a horizontal control band: filter options on the left and a sort control aligned to the right, echoing the header’s left/right tension. A light, full-width applied-filters-summary strip follows, standing out against the dark background in the same way the mockup’s notification bar did. Below this strip, a large vertical space is allocated to either a multi-column grid of dark, minimal result tiles or a single, centered empty/error state, depending on the search state. The page then transitions into a tall dark footer with the brand block on the left and three link columns on the right, capped by a thin bottom copyright bar. Throughout, elements breathe with consistent 8px-based spacing, neon accents emphasize interactive elements, and a floating call button hovers at the bottom-right corner above the content, not affecting layout but always visible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The search-controller is an invisible orchestration layer that conceptually wraps the entire main content area between the global header and footer. It spans the full width of the centered content column, starting roughly 32–48px below the header. While it does not render any visible UI, it determines which visual child component occupies the main results region (results-grid, no-results-state, no-query-state, loading-indicator, or error-state) and controls the state of inline-search-form, filter-bar, sort-control, and applied-filters-summary. From a spatial perspective, it defines a vertical stack: search form at the top, filters/sort directly beneath, applied filters strip next, and the dynamic results/status region filling the remainder of the available vertical space above the footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual controller anchored at the top of the main content column; it does not consume visible space but manages which child components are rendered in the central content stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline-search-form sits near the top of the main content column, approximately 32–48px below the bottom edge of the header. It is left-aligned within the centered max-width container and spans about 60–70% of the content width on desktop, leaving comfortable dark space to its right. The form presents as a single, prominent search field with a subtle rounded rectangle shape (4–8px radius) on the dark background, with a neon-accented border or caret and a search icon aligned to the right inside the input. Vertical padding inside the input is about 12–16px, making it feel substantial but clean. There is 16–24px of vertical space below the form before the filter-bar begins. On narrower screens, the search-form expands to nearly full width of the content column and remains positioned at the top of the scrollable content region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element in the main flow, responsive in width (60–70% of content width on desktop, approaching 100% on mobile) and always anchored directly below the header within the content column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter-bar is positioned directly beneath the inline-search-form, separated by roughly 16–24px of vertical space. It spans the full width of the centered content column but visually occupies the left two-thirds of a horizontal control row, with the sort-control sharing the same row on the right. The filter-bar consists of compact segmented controls or dropdowns for resolution, content type, and genre, laid out horizontally from left to right. Each control has 8–12px of horizontal space between it and the next, with 8–12px internal padding, keeping the row airy and easy to scan. The controls sit on the same dark background as the page, with subtle outlines or underlines and neon green accents for active or hovered states. The overall height of the filter-bar row is relatively shallow (around 40–48px), in keeping with the mockup’s lightweight header and notification elements.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static horizontal toolbar, aligned to the left within the content column and sharing a flex row with the sort-control; it wraps into multiple lines beneath the search form on smaller screen widths.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort-control sits on the same horizontal line as the filter-bar but is right-aligned within the content column. It occupies roughly the rightmost 20–25% of the control row on desktop, with an auto-expanding gap between it and the filter-bar to maintain separation and echo the header’s right-aligned CTA. The control appears as a compact dropdown or segmented toggle with a label such as "Sort by" and the current sort option. It shares the same visual language as the filter-bar (dark background, subtle border, neon accent for the active state) and aligns vertically with the filter controls, forming a clean, single-height band. There is only a small 8–12px bottom padding before the applied-filters-summary begins beneath this row.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, right-aligned element within the filter/sort row; on smaller screens it can drop below the filter-bar, becoming a second row while still maintaining 8–16px vertical spacing from the filters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The applied-filters-summary appears directly below the filter-bar and sort-control row, separated by around 8–16px of vertical space. It presents as a full-width, horizontally spanning strip inside the centered content column, with a noticeably lighter background compared to the rest of the dark page—visually mirroring the mockup’s notification bar. The strip has slightly rounded corners (4–8px radius) and 12–16px vertical padding and 16–24px horizontal padding. The left side holds a concise text summary of active filters (e.g., "Showing 24 results • 1080p • Series • Action"), while the right side can contain compact pill-shaped filter tags or a "Clear all" control. The bar runs nearly the entire width of the content area, stopping only at the column padding, and serves as a strong horizontal anchor immediately above the results region.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width strip within the content column; it appears only when there are active filters or a meaningful summary to show, otherwise it can collapse entirely, allowing the results region to move up.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The results-grid occupies the main body of the page below the applied-filters-summary, starting approximately 24–32px beneath it (or beneath the filter/sort row when the summary is hidden). It fills the width of the centered content column and uses a multi-column grid aligned to the 12‑column system: on large desktops, result-tile cards typically appear in 4 columns (each tile about 25% of the content width minus gutters), on medium screens 3 columns, and on mobile 1–2 columns. Horizontal gaps between tiles are about 16–24px, and vertical gaps between rows are about 24–32px, creating an evenly spaced, breathable layout. The grid extends downward, occupying most of the vertical space between the filter region and the top of the footer, with a final 48–64px bottom margin to visually separate it from the footer. The underlying background remains dark, so the tiles themselves provide the main visual texture.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content region; it grows vertically with the number of results and scrolls under the fixed header and above the floating call button. On smaller viewports the grid automatically reflows to fewer columns while retaining consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each result-tile is a vertically oriented card within the results-grid. It occupies one grid column, with a width determined by the column layout (roughly 20–25% of the content width on desktop in a 4-column configuration). The tile has a dark, slightly lifted appearance without heavy shadows, using subtle borders or a faint neon edge on hover to stand out against the black background. The top portion of the tile is dominated by the content artwork or poster, maintaining a consistent aspect ratio (e.g., 16:9 or 2:3) across tiles. Beneath the artwork, there is a compact block with the series title on the first line, followed by a secondary line or two for resolution, content type, and genre, with 4–8px spacing between lines. Vertical padding inside the card is about 12–16px, and border radius matches the overall system (4–8px). The tile’s total height is visually balanced so that rows look cohesive, and on hover or focus, a neon green outline or subtle glow signals interactivity without adding heavy shadows.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the grid; tiles reflow responsively into more or fewer columns depending on viewport width, maintaining consistent padding and aspect ratio of artwork.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-results-state occupies the same region that the results-grid would, starting at the same vertical position beneath the applied-filters-summary or filter/sort row. Instead of a grid, it displays a single, centered message block within the content column. The block is horizontally centered and restricted to roughly 50–60% of the content width, with generous dark space on either side. There is 48–64px of vertical space above it (from the filters) and at least as much below before the footer, creating a quiet, focused state. The message can include a simple icon or glyph at the top, a clear headline like "No results found", and a short explanation or suggested actions below, with 8–12px spacing between lines. The background stays dark; the emphasis is on typography and possibly subtle neon accent text or buttons for "Clear filters". This layout ensures the empty state feels intentional and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with results-grid; it appears in the same slot in the vertical stack when there is a query but no matching results, pushing the footer down as needed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The no-query-state also occupies the main results region but is used when the user first lands on the page or before any search has been executed. It is a centered, welcoming block positioned beneath the inline-search-form and filter controls, with more pronounced whitespace than the no-results-state to emphasize the starting point. The block sits about 40–56px below the controls, centered within the content column and limited to roughly 50–60% of its width. It may contain an illustration or abstract icon, a headline inviting the user to "Search for on-demand content", and a brief line describing what can be found (e.g., "Movies, series, live channels by genre, resolution, and more"), with 8–16px spacing between lines. The surrounding dark background remains largely empty, creating a calm, open feel that draws the eye back up to the search field.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static and mutually exclusive with results-grid and no-results-state; it occupies the same central slot and disappears as soon as a valid query triggers loading or results.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading-indicator appears over the main results region whenever a search is in progress. It is centered horizontally within the content column and vertically within the visible portion of the results area (typically starting just below the applied-filters-summary). The indicator can be a neon-accented spinner or progress glyph on the dark background. Depending on implementation, it can sit alone on the dark background or on top of a slightly dimmed overlay above any existing grid or empty content. The visual footprint is compact (e.g., a 48–64px icon plus short label), but the surrounding area is intentionally left uncluttered so the loading state is immediately obvious without feeling heavy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlays or replaces the results content area; it is centrally positioned within that area and is mutually exclusive with the visible grid, empty states, and error-state while active. It scrolls with the page if the user has scrolled past the top of the results region.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error-state appears as a prominent, full-width alert bar in the main content column, positioned directly beneath the filter-bar and sort-control row, effectively taking the place of the applied-filters-summary. It spans nearly the entire width of the content column with 16–24px horizontal padding and 12–16px vertical padding. The background is a lighter tone relative to the dark page but tinted toward a warning/error color, with high-contrast text describing the issue (e.g., network error) and a small action like "Retry" on the right. The bar has subtle rounding (4–8px radius) similar to the notification strip in the mockup. When the error-state is visible, it sits above any results-grid or empty state; in severe errors, the results area below can be empty, leaving the error-state as the primary focus above a large dark void.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width bar within the content column, pushing the results region downward. It is mutually exclusive with the applied-filters-summary strip and is anchored just below the filter/sort controls.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-sync component is non-visual and exists purely as a state management layer tied to the page URL. It does not render any UI or occupy physical space. Conceptually, it tracks and updates the query, filters, sorting, and page of results so that the current search state is reflected in the URL and restored on reload or navigation. From a layout perspective, it simply sits alongside the search-controller at the top of the content logic without affecting any visual alignment or spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-rendering utility component; it has no spatial behavior in the layout and does not alter the visual stacking order of any elements.

name: Series Detail
title: Series Detail
description: Displays detailed information for a selected on-demand series, including metadata, seasons, and episodes, and allows starting playback of any episode with navigation back to search results when applicable.

core_features: Read the seriesId (and optional fromQuery) URL parameters and load full series details plus all seasons and episodes using getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes.
Display series metadata including title, synopsis, genre, resolution, and tags from the Series model returned by getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes.
Organize episodes by season_number and episode_number (or episode_order when available) and present them in ordered lists per season.
Provide a selectable season control so the user can switch between seasons; the currently selected season is reflected in the URL (e.g., season parameter) for shareable state.
Render each episode with a clearly labeled episode index (e.g., "Episode " + episode_number) and a Play button that calls startEpisodePlayback for the selected episode.
Offer a "Play from beginning" action that starts playback of the earliest available episode (first season, first episode in order) via startEpisodePlayback.
If the page was accessed from search (fromQuery present), show a "Back to search results" navigation that links to search.html with the query parameter restored.
Expose deep links to other series (e.g., related content) purely via navigation to series.html with a different seriesId, letting that target page handle its own data loading.
user_workflows: Viewing a series from search results: 1) User clicks a series in search.html; browser navigates to series.html?seriesId={id}&fromQuery={originalSearch}. 2) On page load, the controller reads seriesId and calls getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes(seriesId). 3) While loading, a loading state is shown. 4) On success, the page shows series metadata, selects a default season (either from the season URL parameter or the earliest season), and lists its episodes. 5) A "Back to search results" link is available and sends the user back to search.html?query={fromQuery}.

Selecting a season and browsing episodes: 1) User sees a season selector populated from the seasons returned by getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes. 2) User chooses a different season. 3) The controller updates the selected season state and rewrites the URL to include season={season_number} while keeping seriesId (and fromQuery if present). 4) The episodes list re-renders to show episodes for the newly selected season in order.

Starting playback of a specific episode: 1) In the episodes list, the user clicks the Play button for a particular episode. 2) The click handler calls startEpisodePlayback(episode.id). 3) If the response indicates success, the controller navigates the browser to the returned playback_url (or initializes the player using that URL, depending on the global playback implementation). 4) If success is false, an inline error message from the response message is displayed near the episode or globally.

Playing from the beginning of the series: 1) The user clicks a "Play from beginning" control in the series header area. 2) The controller computes the earliest episode across all seasons using the seasons and episodes array from getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes. 3) The controller calls startEpisodePlayback with that episode's id. 4) On success, the browser navigates to the playback_url; on failure, an error message is displayed.

Returning to search results: 1) When fromQuery is present in the URL, a "Back to search results" link is shown. 2) User clicks this link. 3) The browser navigates to search.html?query={fromQuery}, restoring the previous search query context.

Navigating to another series (e.g., related link): 1) User clicks a link to another series (for example, from a list of related titles rendered by the server or parent context). 2) Browser navigates to series.html?seriesId={otherSeriesId}. 3) The new instance of the page reads the new seriesId and repeats the loading and rendering workflow.
interactions: On page load, the controller reads URL parameters (seriesId is required, season and fromQuery are optional) and triggers getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes(seriesId).
If seriesId is missing or getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes fails, an error state interaction allows the user to retry loading (re-calling getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes) or navigate away.

Season selector interaction: when a user selects a season, selectedSeasonNumber state is updated and the URL is rewritten to include season={season_number} while preserving seriesId and fromQuery; episodes for that season are displayed.
Episode row interactions: clicking on an episode row can highlight or expand it (optional), while clicking the Play button on that row calls startEpisodePlayback for that episode.
Play from beginning interaction: clicking this control triggers a computed selection of the first episode in series order and calls startEpisodePlayback for that episode.
Back to search results interaction: clicking the back link navigates directly to search.html with query set from the fromQuery URL parameter.
Error handling interaction for playback: when startEpisodePlayback returns success=false, the user sees the provided message and may attempt playback again or choose another episode.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary state driver is the URL. The required parameter seriesId identifies which Series to load; the page controller will not attempt to call getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes without a valid seriesId. On initial load and on any URL change, the controller reads seriesId, fromQuery, and season (optional). It calls getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes(seriesId) exactly once per seriesId change and stores the returned Series and seasons/episodes in memory for rendering. The selected season is determined as follows: if a numeric season parameter exists and matches a season_number from the API response, that season is selected; otherwise, the earliest season_number in the response is selected. When the user changes seasons through the UI, the controller updates the selected season in memory and adjusts the URL using history APIs to include or replace the season parameter, keeping seriesId and fromQuery untouched. The fromQuery URL parameter acts purely as navigation state: if present, a back link to search.html is shown, and clicking it creates a navigation to search.html?query={fromQuery}; if absent, no such back link is rendered. Episode playback does not alter URL parameters on this page; instead, clicking Play triggers startEpisodePlayback, and the returned playback_url is used to navigate to or initialize the player. No localStorage or other client storage is used; all shareable state (which series and which season is active, and search context) is encoded in URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates URL parameter reading, data loading via getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes, selection of the active season, and delegation of episode playback requests to startEpisodePlayback. Manages global loading and error states for the page.
Displays core series information including title, synopsis, genre, resolution, and tags, and exposes a 'Play from beginning' control that plays the earliest available episode of the series.

Provides a control (e.g., dropdown or list) for selecting which season's episodes to view. Options are derived from the seasons array returned by getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes. When a season is selected, it notifies the page controller, which updates the URL season parameter and the currently displayed episode list.

Renders an ordered list of episodes for the currently selected season. For each Episode, shows a label constructed from episode_number (e.g., 'Episode ' + episode_number), the episode title, and optional metadata such as duration_minutes or release_date if available, and includes a Play button that initiates playback of that episode.

Abstracts the interaction with startEpisodePlayback. Given an episodeId, calls startEpisodePlayback, interprets the response, and either navigates to playback_url on success or surfaces an error message on failure. This component is used by both the episode list Play buttons and the 'Play from beginning' action.

When fromQuery is present in the URL, renders a 'Back to search results' link. Clicking the link navigates to search.html with query set to the fromQuery value so search results can be reconstructed there.

Displays navigational links to other series (such as related or recommended titles) if such data is provided by the surrounding context or server-side rendering. Each link navigates to series.html with the corresponding seriesId; no additional interfaces are called by this component.
Shows a loading indicator or message while getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes is in progress. It is controlled by the page controller's loading state.

Displays an error message when getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes fails or when seriesId is missing or invalid. May expose a retry action that instructs the page controller to call getSeriesDetailWithEpisodes again.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a full-width dark page with a fixed-height global header at the top, a single main content column centered on a 12-column grid, and a tall multi-column footer at the bottom. Within the main column, the vertical order is: (1) a subtle back-to-search text link above the series content, (2) a prominent series metadata header acting as the page hero, (3) a horizontal season selector immediately below the hero, (4) a vertically stacked episode list as the main body content, (5) a compact related-series section near the bottom as a secondary discovery area. Loading and error states appear as high-contrast notification strips near the top of this content column. A floating playback control button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of scroll, echoing the mockup’s floating call button pattern.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a full-width dark page with a fixed-height global header at the top, a single main content column centered on a 12-column grid, and a tall multi-column footer at the bottom. Within the main column, the vertical order is: (1) a subtle back-to-search text link above the series content, (2) a prominent series metadata header acting as the page hero, (3) a horizontal season selector immediately below the hero, (4) a vertically stacked episode list as the main body content, (5) a compact related-series section near the bottom as a secondary discovery area. Loading and error states appear as high-contrast notification strips near the top of this content column. A floating playback control button sits at the bottom-right of the viewport, independent of scroll, echoing the mockup’s floating call button pattern.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Header and footer span full width with their inner content constrained to the same centered max-width (aligned to the 12-column grid) and padded from viewport edges by ~64–80px. The main series detail content column shares this width. The header remains relatively shallow; the hero-like series metadata header occupies roughly the upper third of the content area above the fold. The season selector and episode list consume the central vertical band, with the episode list taking the majority of scroll height. The related series section is compact and positioned just above the tall footer. Notification strips (loading and error) span the inner content width but are only about the height of a standard banner (roughly 56–72px). The floating playback controller occupies a small footprint in the bottom-right corner, about the size of a medium FAB, offset ~24–32px from the edges.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium density overall. The header, back-to-search link, series metadata header, and season selector are spacious with clear breathing room (32–48px gaps between major sections). The episode list is denser but still breathable, with each episode row padded (16–24px vertical) and separated by modest gaps (8–16px). The footer remains text-dense with multi-column links but uses consistent line spacing and column separation to stay readable. Notification strips are visually clear and uncluttered, with a single line or short paragraph of text and an icon or loader on a light background.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts at the bright neon CTA in the global header and the logo, then drops into the main column to the series title in the metadata header. From there it moves naturally rightward across key metadata and the primary 'Play' or 'Continue watching' CTA. The gaze then flows down to the season selector, and further down through the vertically stacked episodes. After episodes, attention moves to the related series suggestions and finally to the footer’s brand/logo and link columns. The floating playback controller at the bottom-right acts as a persistent secondary focal point that becomes most prominent during or after playback. When present, loading/error notification strips briefly intercept attention just below the top of the content column due to their contrasting light background.

layout.overall_description: The Series Detail page sits on a dark, full-width canvas with a neon-accented, streaming-inspired aesthetic. A shallow global header stretches across the top, with the logo on the left, centered navigation, and a bright fluorescent green CTA button on the right, all constrained within a wide, centered grid. Below this, the main content is a single, centered column with generous horizontal margins (~64–80px from the viewport edge). At the top of this column, a subtle back-to-search link anchors the user’s context; beneath it, a roomy series metadata header presents the series title, imagery, and key metadata as a hero-like block, but still within the flat, minimal style. Immediately under this hero, a horizontal season selector runs partway across the column, and below it, a vertically stacked list of episodes forms the main scrollable content. When applicable, a light notification strip appears near the top of this column to communicate loading or error states, standing out strongly against the dark background. Near the bottom of the page, before the footer, a compact related-series section offers a few alternative titles in a simple row or small grid. The page ends in a tall, multi-column footer with the brand and three link columns, echoing the mockup, plus a thin bottom bar for copyright. Throughout, the layout is airy at the top and more content-dense toward the footer, with a floating, neon-accented playback controller button fixed to the bottom-right corner of the viewport, hovering above content and footer as a constant, action-focused anchor.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller spans the full viewport, setting a dark background from edge to edge. It arranges the page vertically in three main bands: a full-width header at the very top, the central content column in the middle, and a full-width footer at the bottom. The central column is centered and constrained by a max-width grid (12 columns), with left and right margins of approximately 64–80px from the viewport edges on large screens and responsive reduction on smaller devices. All series-related components (back-to-search link, metadata header, season selector, episodes list, related series, and any status strips) are stacked within this central column with vertical gaps of 24–48px, maintaining a clear linear top-to-bottom reading order. The floating playback control component is anchored above this flow, positioned at the bottom-right of the viewport, remaining visible as the user scrolls, and never overlapping the header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Defines overall structure and grid; central content scrolls normally, header and footer are static at top and bottom of the document, while the floating playback controller is positioned fixed relative to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Within the main content column, the series metadata header sits near the top, directly below the back-to-search link with a vertical gap of about 24–32px. It spans the full inner width of the column (roughly 80–90% of overall page width, depending on breakpoint) but preserves the dark background, using layout and typography rather than heavy containers. Visually, it behaves like a hero band occupying approximately the top third of the above-the-fold area on desktop. The content inside aligns left: on the left half to left two-thirds, a vertical stack contains the series title (large, bold, the most visually dominant text on the page), followed by smaller metadata chips or lines (genre, year, rating, number of seasons), and then a brief description paragraph. Optionally, a poster thumbnail or key art can sit to the far left or slightly offset, occupying about one-quarter to one-third of the width, with text wrapping to the right on larger screens. A primary neon-green 'Play' or 'Continue watching' button sits near the lower-left of this block, aligned with the title and text, drawing the eye similarly to the header CTA. Generous padding (24–32px) separates individual elements within this header, maintaining a light, airy feel despite the amount of information.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal scroll flow; remains static within the central content column and does not stick on scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The season selector is positioned immediately below the series metadata header, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px. It aligns left with the metadata content and spans about 60–80% of the main content column width, leaving breathing room to the right for visual balance. The selector appears as a horizontal row of season tabs or pills (e.g., Season 1, Season 2, etc.), each with flat styling and subtle rounding consistent with the site’s minimal aesthetic. The active season tab uses the neon accent color or an underline bar, making it immediately distinguishable. The overall height of this band is modest—around 48–56px—so it reads as a control strip rather than a full section. On smaller screens, tabs may horizontally scroll, but still stay visually anchored just beneath the series header.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the page but may become sticky at the top of the main content column (just below the global header) once the user scrolls past the metadata header, ensuring the season control remains accessible while browsing long episode lists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The episode list sits directly under the season selector, separated by 24–32px of vertical space. It occupies the full width of the main content column, aligning its left edge with the series title above. Episodes are arranged in a single vertical list with each episode row spanning nearly the entire column width. Each row is a flat, dark block with subtle rounding and no visible shadow, about 72–104px tall depending on content. Within each row, content lays out horizontally: a small thumbnail or episode number on the far left (taking roughly 15–20% width), main episode info (title, duration, maybe a 1–2 line synopsis) in the central 50–60%, and a neon-accented 'Play' or 'Resume' button aligned to the right (20–25% width). Vertical padding of 16–24px inside each item ensures readability, and 8–16px gaps separate rows. The list extends downward, becoming the primary scrollable content of the page, with any pagination or 'Load more' control centered at the bottom of the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives in the normal scroll flow within the central column; expands vertically as episodes increase, pushing related content and footer downward.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The playback controller manifests visually as a floating action surface at the bottom-right corner of the viewport, echoing the mockup’s floating call button. In its compact state, it appears as a circular or rounded-pill button about 56–64px tall, offset roughly 24–32px from both the right and bottom edges of the screen. It uses the fluorescent green accent as its primary color, making it a strong focal point against the dark background. An icon (e.g., play or pause) sits centered, optionally accompanied by a short label such as 'Resume episode' when there’s enough space. When an episode is playing or paused, the button can expand into a small horizontal mini-player bar, still anchored to the bottom-right, occupying about 30–40% of the viewport width and 72–88px in height. This expanded state includes basic controls (play/pause, next, maybe a progress bar) while retaining the neon accent for the main action. The floating controller stays above page content and above the footer, never being obscured as the user scrolls.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed to the viewport’s bottom-right corner, independent of scroll. It appears or changes state based on playback context and may subtly elevate above content via a slight contrast edge, but without heavy shadows, in keeping with the flat design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The back-to-search link is placed at the top of the main content column, directly below the global header with a vertical gap of around 32–40px, and above the series metadata header with about 24px of space below it. It aligns to the far left of the column, beginning at the same horizontal position as the series title. The link is styled as a small, low-contrast text link or icon + label (e.g., a left-facing chevron) in a muted light-gray, ensuring it’s visible but not competing with the hero content. It spans only as much width as the text requires and sits on a single line, leaving generous dark space around it to keep the top of the content area feeling airy. On smaller devices, it remains the first interactive element in the content column, giving users a quick way to step back to search results.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the main content flow; does not stick to the top and scrolls off the screen as the user moves down the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The related series section is positioned below the episode list, separated from the last episode row by a more generous vertical gap of about 32–48px to mark it as a distinct, secondary section. It spans the full width of the main content column but may visually feel slightly narrower if cards don’t occupy all columns. A simple section heading such as 'You might also like' sits left-aligned at the top of this block. Beneath the heading, 3–4 related series are laid out in a single horizontal row on large screens, each card occupying an equal fraction of the column width (e.g., 25–30% each) with 16–24px gaps between them. Each card is flat and dark with subtle rounding, featuring a small poster or thumbnail at the top, the series title, and perhaps one metadata line. On smaller viewports, these cards stack vertically or turn into a horizontal scroll row. The spacing within this section remains moderate so it reads as an optional exploration area rather than competing with the main series content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Part of the normal scroll flow; positioned just above the footer and pushed down as the episode list grows.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading state appears as a high-contrast notification strip following the mockup’s cart notification bar pattern. It is placed within the main content column, directly below the back-to-search link and above the series metadata header area. It spans the full width of the inner column (aligned with the series title) and is about 56–72px tall. The background is a light gray or very muted light tone, sharply contrasting the dark page. Inside, content is aligned horizontally: on the left, a subtle loading spinner or progress icon; to its right, a single line or short sentence such as 'Loading series details…' in dark text. Padding of 12–16px vertically and 16–24px horizontally ensures the strip feels airy. When active, this strip replaces or visually overlays the metadata and episode sections, which either remain hidden or appear dimmed until loading completes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears dynamically in the normal content flow; it pushes down the content below when shown, or overlays a placeholder area if using skeleton content. It scrolls with the page and disappears once loading is complete.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error state also uses the notification strip pattern and occupies the same position as the loading strip within the main content column: just under the back-to-search link and above the usual series metadata content. It spans the full width of the column and is similar in height (56–72px), with a light background but accented by error coloring—such as a red left border line or red icon—while preserving the overall clean aesthetic. On the left sits an error icon (e.g., warning triangle), and to its right a concise error message like 'We couldn’t load this series. Please try again.' with an inline text button or link-style 'Retry' action. Internal padding matches the loading strip (about 12–16px vertical, 16–24px horizontal). When the error strip is visible, the metadata header, season selector, and episode list are either hidden or replaced with a very minimal placeholder, so the user’s attention focuses on the error message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears conditionally in the normal content flow; it scrolls with the page and does not stick. It may replace the loading strip if an error occurs during fetch, maintaining the same spatial footprint to avoid content jump.

name: Help & Support
title: Help & Support
description: Page for viewing FAQs, troubleshooting steps, and contacting support, with contextual navigation to other account and streaming pages.

core_features: Load all FAQ sections and contact options via the getHelpContent interface and render them grouped by section.
Determine and focus the active FAQ section using the URL parameter `section`, derived from links on related pages such as settings.html, billing.html, my_plan.html, and tv_guide.html.
Allow users to switch between FAQ sections (e.g., billing-related, plan-related, settings-related, TV guide-related) without reloading the page, while updating the `section` URL parameter.
Expand and collapse FAQ answers, rendering answer content (including any embedded link markers) and exposing contextual navigation to related functional pages based on `related_page_key`.
Provide contact options (support email and support phone) from `contact_options` returned by getHelpContent, with appropriate click actions (e.g., mailto:, tel:).

Offer guided navigation links from FAQ entries to billing.html, settings.html, my_plan.html, or tv_guide.html using the `related_page_key` field and, where applicable, additional query parameters such as `section=parental_controls`.
Optionally filter or search FAQs client-side within the loaded getHelpContent data, without additional API calls.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Arriving from another page with a focused section (e.g., from billing.html)
1. User clicks a help link on billing.html that navigates to help.html with a URL parameter such as `?section=billing`.
2. The Help & Support page initializes, the help-content-loader component calls getHelpContent once and receives `faq_sections` and `contact_options`.
3. The help-section-router component reads `section` from the URL and normalizes it.
4. The router derives an internal key for each section by transforming each `section_title` (e.g., lowercasing, removing spaces and special characters) and selects the section whose derived key best matches the `section` URL parameter.
5. The faq-section-list highlights the matched section as active, and the faq-list displays only the FAQs belonging to that section.
6. The user expands FAQ items, reads answers, and uses any contextual links (e.g., to billing.html) exposed by the faq-item and faq-context-links components.
7. The user may switch to a different section via the section navigation, which updates the visible FAQs and synchronizes the `section` URL parameter via the help-section-router.

Workflow 2: Using FAQ answers to navigate to functional pages (e.g., parental controls, plan management, recordings)
1. User is viewing the Help & Support page and expands an FAQ item via the faq-list.
2. The faq-item component parses the FAQ’s `answer` string for any inline markup tokens that represent deep links (for example, tokens that encode a target page and optional page parameters).
3. Additionally, the faq-item inspects the FAQ’s `related_page_key` (e.g., values corresponding to billing, my_plan, settings, tv_guide) to determine which functional page the answer should link to.
4. For FAQs associated with settings.html and specifically parental controls, the faq-item or faq-context-links component constructs a navigation URL to settings.html including `?section=parental_controls` when the corresponding help link is clicked.
5. For FAQs associated with billing.html, my_plan.html, or tv_guide.html, the component constructs navigation URLs to those pages (without extra parameters unless encoded in the answer markup tokens) and redirects the browser when the user clicks the help link.
6. The target page then uses its own logic (outside this page) to read any passed parameters and show context-specific UI.

Workflow 3: Contacting support from the Help & Support page
1. User scrolls to or views the contact options area provided by the contact-options-panel.
2. The help-content-loader has already populated `contact_options` from getHelpContent, providing `support_email` and `support_phone`.
3. The contact-options-panel renders clickable actions bound to these values (for example, a mailto link using `support_email` and a tel link using `support_phone`).
4. When the user clicks the email option, the browser opens the default email client with the recipient set to the dynamic `support_email` value.
5. When the user clicks the phone option on a compatible device, the device initiates a call using the dynamic `support_phone` value.
6. If the implementation includes a generic "More help" or fallback action, clicking it can re-trigger a content reload or highlight key FAQ sections, all using already-fetched data from getHelpContent.

Workflow 4: Browsing and refining FAQs across sections
1. User arrives on help.html with or without a `section` parameter.
2. The help-content-loader calls getHelpContent and passes data to the faq-section-list and faq-list components.
3. If a `section` URL parameter is present, help-section-router selects the corresponding section; otherwise, it selects a default (e.g., the first section in `faq_sections`).
4. The faq-section-list displays all section titles as selectable items (tabs or list). The currently active section is visually distinguished based on the router’s state.
5. The user clicks a different section title; an onSectionTabClick handler in faq-section-list notifies help-section-router to update the active section key.
6. The router updates the URL’s `section` parameter (using history APIs or a full navigation, according to implementation) so that reloading or sharing the URL preserves the chosen section.
7. If a client-side search/filter input (faq-filter) is implemented, typing into it triggers filtering logic in faq-list, which restricts the displayed FAQs within the currently active section without additional API calls.
interactions: On page initialization, a data-loading interaction occurs: getHelpContent is invoked once, and a loading state is displayed until it resolves or fails.
Users interact with section navigation (e.g., clicking a section title), which triggers active-section changes and updates the `section` URL parameter.
Users interact with FAQ items by clicking on questions to expand or collapse answers, with the current open/closed state managed client-side per item.

Within expanded FAQ answers, users click contextual help links that are either parsed from answer markup or derived from `related_page_key`, which triggers navigation to billing.html, settings.html, my_plan.html, or tv_guide.html with appropriate URL parameters.
Users interact with the contact options by clicking support email or phone entries, which triggers a mailto: or tel: action constructed from `contact_options`.
If implemented, users can type into a search/filter input to refine visible FAQ items; this interaction updates the filtered FAQ list in real time without further API calls.

If the URL `section` parameter changes (for example, due to browser navigation or manual editing), the help-section-router reacts, re-evaluates which FAQ section is active, and updates the displayed FAQs accordingly.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is the query parameter `section` in the help.html URL. On load, the help-section-router reads this parameter and normalizes it (e.g., trimming whitespace and normalizing case). After getHelpContent returns, the router derives an internal key for each entry in `faq_sections` based on its `section_title` (e.g., by lowercasing and removing or replacing non-alphanumeric characters). The router then selects the FAQ section whose derived key best matches the `section` parameter (e.g., exact match or prefix/substring match). If no match is found or `section` is absent, a default active section is selected (such as the first element in `faq_sections`). The active section key is maintained in an in-memory state variable (not in localStorage) and is used by faq-section-list and faq-list to determine which section is highlighted and which FAQs are rendered. When the user clicks to change sections, the router updates the in-memory active section key and synchronizes the URL by writing the corresponding normalized key back into the `section` query parameter, ensuring that reloads or shared URLs remain context-aware. For navigation out of the page, the faq-item and faq-context-links components build destination URLs based on each FAQ’s `related_page_key` and any encoded deep-link tokens found in the `answer`. For FAQs that need to direct specifically to parental controls inside settings.html, the navigation logic appends `section=parental_controls` as a query parameter. All other temporary UI states—such as which FAQ answers are expanded and any active client-side filters—are kept only in memory within this page and are not persisted to URL or storage unless explicitly tied to the URL parameters.

components.functionality: Initializes the Help & Support page by calling getHelpContent, managing loading and error states, and exposing the returned `faq_sections` and `contact_options` to child components.
Reads and interprets the `section` URL parameter, derives normalized keys from each `section_title` in `faq_sections`, selects the active section, and updates the URL when the user changes sections.
Displays the list of FAQ section titles and allows the user to switch the active section. Communicates section changes back to help-section-router.
Optionally provides a text input that filters FAQ items within the currently active section purely on the client side, without additional API calls.
Renders all FAQ items for the currently active section, applying any active client-side filter, and delegates per-item expand/collapse and link parsing to faq-item components.

Represents a single FAQ entry; toggles the visibility of its answer, parses the `answer` string for any link markup tokens, and, in conjunction with `related_page_key`, creates contextual navigation actions to other pages.

Derives and displays explicit navigation links (e.g., "Go to related settings" or equivalent functional labels) for an FAQ item using its `related_page_key` and any encoded parameters. Constructs URLs to billing.html, settings.html (optionally with `section=parental_controls`), my_plan.html, or tv_guide.html.
Displays support contact methods using `support_email` and `support_phone` from getHelpContent and provides clickable actions for initiating an email or phone call.

Coordinates the placement of section navigation, FAQ content, filter input, and contact options, and ensures that data from help-content-loader is passed in the correct order to downstream components.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: Maintain the linear, top-to-bottom single-column flow from the mockup: global full-width dark header on top, then a left-aligned 'Help & Support' page heading in a centered max-width content column. Immediately below the heading, introduce a full-width, light-background help utility strip (analogous to the cart notification bar) that contains the FAQ section navigation and filters. The main FAQ list follows as a vertical stack of questions, then contextual links, and finally a prominent contact support panel before transitioning into the tall, multi-column dark footer. A floating support/call button is anchored at the bottom-right, outside normal flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Maintain the linear, top-to-bottom single-column flow from the mockup: global full-width dark header on top, then a left-aligned 'Help & Support' page heading in a centered max-width content column. Immediately below the heading, introduce a full-width, light-background help utility strip (analogous to the cart notification bar) that contains the FAQ section navigation and filters. The main FAQ list follows as a vertical stack of questions, then contextual links, and finally a prominent contact support panel before transitioning into the tall, multi-column dark footer. A floating support/call button is anchored at the bottom-right, outside normal flow.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The header remains a relatively shallow band at the top. The help content column is centered and constrained within a 12-column grid, with generous horizontal margins (~64–80px from viewport edges on large screens). The light utility strip for sections and filters occupies a modest vertical band but stands out via contrast. The FAQ list takes up the bulk of the central area, yet retains comfortable spacing between items. The contact options panel is visually substantial but not tall, occupying roughly the lower quarter of the content area above the footer. The footer occupies roughly the bottom third of the page, echoing the mockup, with clear separation from the main help content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain medium content density: the header and the section/filter utility strip are airy with clear breathing room. The FAQ list is moderately dense (stacked items, clear vertical spacing), while the contact panel presents a compact set of options without feeling crowded. The footer is the densest area, with multiple link columns yet readable line spacing. No heavy tables or complex multi-column layouts in the main help body; everything is primarily single-column and scannable.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow starts with the global header (logo left, nav center, neon CTA right), then drops to the 'Help & Support' heading in the upper-left of the content column. From there, the eye moves immediately to the high-contrast light utility strip containing FAQ sections and filters. Once a user or the loader determines which help content to show, attention flows down the vertical stack of FAQ items. At the end of this scroll, attention transitions to the contact options panel, inviting direct support contact. The footer logo and link columns continue the downward flow, with the floating support/call button in the bottom-right acting as a persistent secondary attractor throughout the page.

layout.overall_description: The Help & Support page sits on a deep black background with a centered, max-width content column aligned to a 12‑column grid. A full-width dark header spans the top, with the IPTV brand logo on the left, simple navigation in the center, and a bright neon green CTA button on the right, mirroring the tech/streaming aesthetic. Below the header, the main content begins with a left-aligned 'Help & Support' title inside the central column, set with generous top and bottom margins that echo the airy feel from the mockup. Directly under this heading, a full-width, light-background utility strip stretches across the content column, clearly separated from the dark canvas; within this strip, users can jump between FAQ sections and apply filters. Beneath the strip, the page transitions back to the dark background, where a single-column FAQ list runs vertically with one question per row, each occupying the full text width and separated by consistent vertical spacing. After the list, smaller contextual links guide users to related account or streaming pages, followed by a prominent, horizontally oriented contact options panel inviting chat, email, or call support. The bottom third of the screen is dominated by a tall, dark footer with the brand logo and description on the left and three neat link columns on the right, capped by a thin bottom copyright bar. A floating neon-accented support/call button sits in the bottom-right corner of the viewport at all times, independent of scroll, harmonizing with the neon CTAs and providing a constant escape hatch to direct support.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-content-loader appears only during data fetching and is positioned at the very top of the main content column, directly below the global header and above the 'Help & Support' heading. Visually, it takes the form of a slim horizontal loading indicator spanning roughly 100% of the content column width (aligned to the 12‑column grid, not edge-to-edge with the viewport). Its height is minimal—around 4–6px—using the neon accent color against the dark background for visibility. There is minimal extra vertical padding; it feels like a thin, precise bar sitting flush against the top margin of the page heading. On slower loads, an optional semi-transparent dark scrim can subtly dim only the content column area below the header, while the bar remains fixed to the top of that column, signaling that the Help & Support content is loading or updating.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the top of the main content column, just under the fixed or static header. It does not scroll separately; as soon as the user scrolls the page, it moves with the content. The optional scrim (if used) overlays the help content area but not the header or footer.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-section-router is a non-visual orchestration layer embedded near the top of the help content column. Conceptually, it sits between the 'Help & Support' heading and the utility strip that houses the FAQ sections and filters. It does not occupy visible space; instead, it dictates which subset of content (FAQ list, troubleshooting blocks, contact emphasis) is rendered in the central column. When different help sections are selected via the faq-section-list, the router determines which content appears immediately below the light utility strip without changing the page’s overall single-column layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual and non-interactive from a layout perspective; it does not render any box or layer. It simply controls which child components are mounted into the central content column in the vertical stack beneath the section/filter strip.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-section-list is placed directly below the 'Help & Support' heading inside a light-background strip that spans the full width of the content column. This strip mirrors the notification bar from the cart mockup: it contrasts sharply against the dark page, has subtle rounding on its corners, and includes comfortable padding—around 16–24px vertically and 24–32px horizontally. Within this band, the faq-section-list occupies the upper-left portion or the first row: a horizontal row of section labels (e.g., 'All FAQs', 'Streaming Issues', 'Billing', 'Account'), each aligned to the underlying grid columns. On larger screens, the section labels are arranged left-to-right, starting just inside the strip’s left padding, and may use pill-like or underline styles. They consume roughly the left two-thirds of the strip in width, leaving breathing room on the right side for either empty space or complementary controls (like quick access to the most common section). On smaller screens, the section list wraps into multiple rows within the same light strip, maintaining consistent vertical gaps between items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Behaves as part of a full-width light utility band that scrolls with the page. Optionally, on taller viewports, the entire utility strip (including section list and filter) can become sticky just under the global header while scrolling to keep help navigation accessible, but it remains constrained within the central max-width column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-filter sits within the same light-background utility strip as the faq-section-list, typically on the second row or aligned to the right side depending on available width. On desktop, it appears directly beneath or to the right of the section list, maintaining at least 16px vertical or horizontal separation based on the 8px scale. The filter manifests as a compact search field and/or dropdown controls. The primary search input spans roughly 40–60% of the strip’s width, starting from the left margin if positioned on its own row, while dropdowns or filter tags align to the right if space allows. The controls are vertically centered within the strip’s inner area, giving the impression of a unified, functional band. On mobile, the filter stack appears below the section tabs inside the same light panel, with full-width inputs and clear gaps (16–24px) between each element to maintain legibility and the medium-density feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Scrolls with the content as part of the light utility strip. If the strip is configured as sticky, the faq-filter remains visible beneath the header while the user scrolls through the FAQ list, always aligned within the max-width central column.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-list begins immediately below the light utility strip, transitioning back onto the dark page background. It occupies the full width of the central content column (within the 12‑column grid), with generous top margin—around 24–32px—to create a clear visual separation from the strip above. The list is a single vertical column of FAQ items, each linearly stacked with consistent vertical gaps (16–24px) that keep the list readable without feeling sparse. On large screens, the list width feels similar to a readable text column—roughly 60–70% of the page width or the full max-width container, depending on grid constraints. On smaller screens, the list edge aligns closely with the viewport margins with 16–24px side padding. The top few FAQ items are fully visible above the fold on most displays, reinforcing this region as the core content area of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static vertical list that scrolls normally within the page. It anchors directly under the section/filter strip and pushes subsequent components (context links and contact panel) further down as more content is loaded or expanded.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each faq-item is a full-width row inside the faq-list, aligned to the central grid. It appears as a minimally styled dark tile: background slightly lighter or more matte than the page’s pure black, with subtle corner rounding and no visible drop shadows, consistent with the flat, modern aesthetic. The question text is left-aligned, with approximately 16–24px padding on all sides. When collapsed, the item’s height is determined by one or two lines of text plus padding, and successive items are separated by 8–16px vertical gaps. When expanded, the answer text appears directly below the question within the same tile, maintaining the same left alignment and an internal vertical gap of around 8–12px between question and answer. Neon accents (e.g., a thin left border, caret icon, or hover underline) indicate interactivity without overwhelming the dark, minimalist feel. In the context of the list, faq-items form a clear, rhythmic column that users can scan downward.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Each item expands or collapses vertically in place without shifting horizontally. Expansion pushes neighboring items down while preserving the overall single-column flow. No sticky behavior; they move with normal page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The faq-context-links section sits below the main faq-list, separated by a vertical gap of roughly 24–32px to indicate a shift from generic FAQs to more targeted navigation. It occupies the same central content column width and is left-aligned, but is visually more subtle. At the top of this block, an optional short label such as 'Related help' or 'Explore more support' appears in a slightly smaller or less bright type treatment. Below this label, contextual links are arranged either as a horizontal row of concise text links (e.g., 'Manage Subscription', 'Device Setup', 'Account Security') or as a tight vertical list, depending on available width. On wide screens, expect a row or two of inline links that wrap gracefully, each separated by 16–24px horizontal spacing. On smaller screens, links stack vertically with 8–12px vertical gaps. The text remains light against the dark background, but lacks neon treatments, ensuring it feels clearly secondary to the main FAQ content and contact panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that scrolls with the page. It does not stick or float; it simply forms a transitional section between the FAQ list above and the contact options panel below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-options-panel is the final major content block before the footer, positioned below the faq-context-links with a generous vertical margin—around 32–48px—to visually elevate it as a key call-to-action section. It spans the full width of the central content column and appears as a prominent dark panel distinct from the page background, either via a slightly lighter shade, a subtle border, or a thin neon accent line along the top edge. Inside, content is arranged in a single column on mobile and a two-zone layout on larger screens: on the left, a concise heading like 'Still need help?' plus a brief line of text reassuring users about support availability; on the right (or below on small screens), a horizontal row of contact methods—such as buttons for 'Live Chat', 'Email Support', and 'Call Us'. These buttons follow the site’s button padding patterns (roughly 8–16px vertical and 16–24px horizontal), with the primary contact method (e.g., live chat) using the bright neon accent. The panel’s padding aligns with the spacing system, providing at least 24px of interior space on all sides so the content doesn’t feel cramped even though it is more content-dense than the FAQ items.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block anchored in the vertical flow, directly above the footer. It does not float or stick; instead, it serves as the last major stop in the scroll before users enter the footer area. It works in tandem with the separate floating support button (outside this component list) that remains fixed to the bottom-right of the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The help-layout-controller is the invisible framework that defines the spatial rules for the entire Help & Support page content. It creates a centered max-width container aligned with the global 12‑column grid, beginning just below the global header and ending just above the footer. All visible help components—the page heading, the light utility strip (housing faq-section-list and faq-filter), the faq-list and faq-items, the faq-context-links, and the contact-options-panel—are vertically stacked within this column. The controller enforces consistent horizontal margins (~64–80px to the viewport edge on large screens and 16–24px on small screens) and consistent vertical section gaps (about 32–48px between major blocks) to match the mockup’s rhythm. It also ensures that the tall footer section connects smoothly below the contact panel, leaving some dark background breathing room between them when content is short.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual; it orchestrates positioning, stacking order, and responsive behavior of its child components. It keeps the main help content centered, prevents it from spanning edge-to-edge, and may control breakpoint behavior (e.g., stacking contact methods or reflowing the section/filter strip) without rendering its own visual box.

name: About
title: About
description: Page that loads and displays dynamic information about the IPTV provider, including background, mission, history, partnerships, and supported devices, using the getAboutPageContent interface.

core_features: Load company information (name, mission, timeline, partnerships, supported devices) from the getAboutPageContent interface on page initialization.
Display a structured overview of the company, with dedicated sections for mission, history/timeline, partnerships, and supported devices.
Allow users to navigate directly to specific sections of the About page via URL parameters (for example, focusing on mission, timeline, partnerships, or devices).

Enable deep linking into specific timeline entries or specific partnerships via URL parameters, so that external links (e.g., from marketing campaigns) can open the About page scrolled to or focused on a relevant detail.
Provide simple in-page navigation (tabs or links) that updates URL parameters instead of local state, ensuring the current section can be bookmarked or shared.
Handle data-loading and error states for the getAboutPageContent interface, with a retry mechanism if the call fails.
user_workflows:
View general About information from footer:
1. User clicks the "About" link in the site footer.
2. Browser navigates to about.html with no additional URL parameters.
3. The page calls getAboutPageContent with no parameters.
4. On success, the page displays the default section (for example, an overview that shows company name and mission, plus links to other sections).
5. User can click in-page navigation links to view different sections; each click updates the URL with an appropriate section parameter.

Open About page directly to a section from an external link:
1. User clicks an external link that targets about.html with a section parameter (e.g., about.html?section=mission or about.html?section=devices).
2. The page loads and calls getAboutPageContent.
3. After data is loaded, the section router reads the section parameter from the URL.
4. The page scrolls to or activates the matching section so it is in view.
5. If the user changes section using in-page navigation, the URL is updated to reflect the new section value.

Highlight a specific timeline entry:
1. User follows a link such as about.html?section=timeline&timeline_year=<some-year-value> (the concrete year value comes from a dynamic timeline entry, not hardcoded in the page).
2. Page initializes, calls getAboutPageContent.
3. Once timeline data is available, the router reads timeline_year from the URL.
4. The timeline section highlights or scrolls to the entry whose year matches the timeline_year parameter.
5. If the user selects a different timeline entry, the component updates the timeline_year parameter in the URL so the new state can be shared or bookmarked.

Highlight a specific partnership:
1. User follows a link such as about.html?section=partnerships&partner=<partner-identifier-derived-from-partner_name>.
2. Page loads and calls getAboutPageContent.
3. After partnerships are loaded, the router reads the partner parameter.
4. The partnerships section expands or focuses the matching partnership entry.
5. If the user clicks on another partnership, the partner parameter in the URL is updated accordingly.

Filter or focus supported devices:
1. User opens about.html with an optional devices-related parameter (e.g., about.html?section=devices&device=<device-filter-value> derived from one of the supported_devices entries).
2. Page loads and calls getAboutPageContent.
3. The router interprets the section and device parameters.
4. The supported devices section is shown and filtered or focused based on the device parameter, if present.
5. If the user interacts with the supported devices list (e.g., chooses a different filter), the component updates the URL device parameter to the newly selected value.
interactions: Click on footer "About" link: navigates the user to about.html without additional parameters; page loads and renders content based on getAboutPageContent.

Click on in-page navigation items (e.g., Mission, Timeline, Partnerships, Supported Devices): updates the section parameter in the URL (using the History API or a full navigation) and causes the corresponding section to become active/visible and, if needed, scrolled into view.

Click on a specific timeline entry: updates the timeline_year parameter in the URL to the year of the clicked entry and optionally scrolls to that entry; on reload or share, the same entry will be highlighted.
Click on a specific partnership entry: updates the partner parameter in the URL to an identifier derived from the selected partner_name and expands or focuses that partnership’s details.
Click on a supported device filter or item: updates the device parameter in the URL to match the chosen device name or category, and filters or focuses the supported_devices list accordingly.
Retry button in case of failed data load: if getAboutPageContent fails, the page shows an error state with a retry control; clicking retry re-invokes getAboutPageContent.

Internal anchor or 'Back to top' link interactions: update the URL hash or section parameter to reflect the new visible subsection without reloading data, relying on the already-fetched getAboutPageContent response.
page_functionality.state_logic: The About page uses only URL parameters and the result of getAboutPageContent for state management. No localStorage or other client-side persistence is used. Key parameters: 1) section: determines which main About subsection is active. Supported values are stable application-level constants such as 'overview', 'mission', 'timeline', 'partnerships', and 'devices'. If section is missing or not recognized, a default section (e.g., 'overview') is used. 2) timeline_year: optional; when section is 'timeline', timeline_year indicates which timeline entry should be highlighted or scrolled into view. The value must be matched against the year field of one of the timeline items returned by getAboutPageContent; if there is no match, the timeline section shows all entries without a highlighted item. 3) partner: optional; when section is 'partnerships', partner identifies which partnership should be expanded or focused. The page derives a stable identifier from each partner_name (for example, by transforming the partner_name returned by getAboutPageContent into a URL-safe key) and compares it to the partner parameter. If none match, the partnerships list is shown in its default collapsed state. 4) device: optional; when section is 'devices', device represents either a filter or a focused device. The value is compared against the supported_devices array returned by getAboutPageContent. If a match is found, the list is filtered or the matching device is visually emphasized; otherwise, all supported devices are displayed by default. The page lifecycle is: a) On initial load, the page parses URL parameters and stores only control state (section, timeline_year, partner, device) in memory. b) The page calls getAboutPageContent once; responses populate in-memory state used by all components. c) When URL parameters change via user actions (e.g., clicking navigation or specific items), the page updates the URL (query parameters and/or hash) and recomputes the active section and focused elements without re-calling getAboutPageContent, unless a full page reload occurs. d) On full reload with parameters present, the same logic runs again so that deep-linked states are reproducible.

components.functionality: Initializes the About page by invoking getAboutPageContent, managing loading, success, and error states, and providing the resulting data (company_name, mission, timeline, partnerships, supported_devices) to other components via an internal data context or props.

Reads and interprets URL parameters (section, timeline_year, partner, device) on load and whenever they change. Determines the active section, the optionally focused timeline entry, partnership, and device. Exposes this computed state to section components and triggers scroll-to-section behavior after data from getAboutPageContent is available.

Provides navigation controls (e.g., a set of links or buttons) that let the user switch between main About sections (overview, mission, timeline, partnerships, devices). When a navigation item is clicked, this component requests a section change from the router, which updates the section parameter in the URL.

Displays a concise overview that may combine the company_name and mission data. Serves as the default view when no section parameter is specified or when the section parameter resolves to an overview state.
Displays the mission text and optionally emphasizes the company_name to clarify whose mission is being presented. This section is active when the router resolves section to the mission view.

Lists the company's historical timeline entries returned by getAboutPageContent. Supports focusing or highlighting a specific entry based on the timeline_year URL parameter and user interactions. When a user clicks on a timeline entry, it requests an update of the timeline_year parameter, enabling deep linking to that entry.

Displays the list of partnerships from getAboutPageContent. Allows expanding or focusing a specific partnership based on the partner URL parameter. When a user selects a partnership, the component requests an update of the partner parameter so that the chosen partnership can be deep-linked.

Shows the list of supported_devices (e.g., device/platform names) from getAboutPageContent. Supports filtering or focusing based on the device URL parameter. When the user interacts with device-related controls (like choosing a device to view more details or applying a filter), the component requests updates to the device parameter in the URL.

Ensures that when the active section or focused item (timeline entry, partnership, device) changes—either due to URL changes or in-page navigation—the page scrolls to the appropriate section or item. It listens to changes from the router and section components and performs scrolling only after data is loaded.

Shows loading indicators while getAboutPageContent is being fetched and displays an error message with a retry control if the fetch fails. When the user clicks retry, it calls back into the about-content-loader to re-run getAboutPageContent.

layout.grid_system: Visually consistent with a 12-column grid constrained to a centered max-width container for content; header and footer span full width with inner content aligned to this grid.
layout.layout_pattern: The About page sits between the same full-width dark header and tall multi-column footer seen in the mockup. Inside the centered max-width content container, the layout follows a simple top-to-bottom flow: the dynamic About content loader initializes silently, then the page title (global, not in this list) appears as a large left-aligned heading. Directly below that, the status/notification strip occupies a full-width row within the content container. Next comes a horizontal section navigation bar that lets users jump between About sections. Beneath this, the individual content sections (Company Overview, Mission, Timeline, Partnerships, Supported Devices) are stacked vertically as a single main column of text content. At the bottom, there’s generous vertical space before the tall footer, which mirrors the mockup with a logo/description block on the left and three link columns. A global floating CTA button remains anchored at the bottom-right, hovering above this flow.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The About page sits between the same full-width dark header and tall multi-column footer seen in the mockup. Inside the centered max-width content container, the layout follows a simple top-to-bottom flow: the dynamic About content loader initializes silently, then the page title (global, not in this list) appears as a large left-aligned heading. Directly below that, the status/notification strip occupies a full-width row within the content container. Next comes a horizontal section navigation bar that lets users jump between About sections. Beneath this, the individual content sections (Company Overview, Mission, Timeline, Partnerships, Supported Devices) are stacked vertically as a single main column of text content.
